topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET631 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advanced Design of Concrete Structures** is the specialized branch of Structural Engineering that deals with the analysis, proportioning, and detailing of Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) elements subjected to complex loading and boundary conditions, using the **Limit State Method (LSM)** as codified in **IS 456:2000** and supplemented by specialized codes such as IS 13920 (Ductile Detailing), IS 875 (Loads), and IS 3370 (Liquid Retaining Structures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit State** is defined in **Clause 2.3.2 of IS 456:2000** as:
> *"A limit state is a state of impending failure, beyond which a structure ceases to perform its intended function satisfactorily, in terms of safety and serviceability."*

A structure is deemed **safe** only when ALL possible limit states are satisfied simultaneously, i.e., when the **Design Strength ≥ Design Load Effect** for every limit state considered.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Bridge Test" Analogy)

Imagine you are designing a **footbridge** that must support a crowd of people.

- **Working Stress Method (WSM) — The "Cautious Grandfather" Approach**: Your grandfather says, *"Let me only use 1/3rd of the material's actual capacity, just to be extra safe."* This guarantees safety but leads to **bulky, uneconomical, oversized** bridges. The factor of safety is high, but the material is grossly underutilized.

- **Ultimate/Collapse Method — The "Reckless Daredevil" Approach**: A daredevil says, *"Let's push the material to its absolute breaking point!"* This gives **slender, economical** sections, but leaves **zero margin** for unexpected overloads, material variability, or workmanship defects. The bridge is one storm away from disaster.

- **Limit State Method (LSM) — The "Smart Engineer" Approach**: The smart engineer says, *"Let me consider TWO conditions:*
  - *Condition 1: Will the bridge collapse? (Collapse/Ultimate Limit State)*
  - *Condition 2: Will people feel safe walking on it? Will cracks be too wide? Will it deflect too much? (Serviceability Limit State)*
  - *And I will use a SMART, scientific safety factor — not too high, not too low — calibrated by statistics!"*

This **dual-criterion, probability-based** philosophy is the modern heart of concrete design.

## 1.3 Why LSM Replaced WSM — Historical Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Evolution of Concrete Design Philosophy in India:**
> - **Pre-1953**: Unreinforced masonry and plain concrete empirical rules
> - **1953–1970**: **Working Stress Method (WSM)** — codified in IS 456:1957
> - **1970–2000**: **Transitional Period** — both WSM and Ultimate Load Method (ULM) in IS 456:1978
> - **2000–Present**: **Limit State Method (LSM)** — dominant method in IS 456:2000 and IS 456:2024 (Draft)

The shift to LSM was driven by three realizations:
1. **WSM uses a single, arbitrary safety factor (~3.0)** that cannot account for different variabilities of dead load, live load, wind, and seismic loads.
2. **WSM does not directly check for serviceability** (deflection, cracking) — it only checks stress.
3. **Statistical reality**: Concrete strength varies by ±15% to ±20% even in controlled conditions. LSM uses **characteristic strength** (5% fractile) to handle this scientifically.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Must-Knows)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partial safety factor for concrete | $\gamma_c$ | **1.50** | IS 456:2000, Table 5.1.2 |
| Partial safety factor for steel | $\gamma_s$ | **1.15** | IS 456:2000, Table 5.1.2 |
| Partial safety factor for Dead Load | $\gamma_f$ (DL) | **1.50** (1.2 with DL counter) | IS 456:2000, Table 5.3.1 |
| Partial safety factor for Live Load | $\gamma_f$ (LL) | **1.50** | IS 456:2000, Table 5.3.1 |
| Partial safety factor for Wind/Seismic | $\gamma_f$ (WL/EQ) | **1.20** (1.50 if primary) | IS 456:2000, Table 5.3.1 |
| Characteristic compressive strength (cube) | $f_{ck}$ | **15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60 N/mm²** | IS 456:2000, Table 2.1 |
| Characteristic yield strength of steel | $f_y$ | **250, 415, 500, 550 N/mm²** | IS 1786 (Fe 415, Fe 500) |
| Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete | $E_c$ | **$5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm²** | IS 456:2000, Cl. 6.2.3.1 |
| Modulus of Elasticity of Steel | $E_s$ | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200,000)** | IS 456:2000, Cl. 6.2.3.1 |
| Design bond stress (plain bars) | $\tau_{bd}$ | **0.6 to 1.2 N/mm²** | IS 456:2000, Table 4.4 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Limit States (Two-Branch System)

The Limit State Method is divided into **two primary branches** as per IS 456:2000:

### A. Ultimate Limit State (ULS) — Clause 5.3
The structure must **NOT collapse** under the most unfavorable combination of loads. ULS checks include:
- **Flexure (bending failure)**
- **Shear (diagonal tension failure)**
- **Torsion (twisting failure)**
- **Compression / Buckling (column failure)**
- **Punching shear (two-way slab failure)**
- **Axial tension (uplift)**
- **Fatigue failure** (for bridges, machine foundations)
- **Fire resistance** (for fire-engineered structures)

### B. Serviceability Limit State (SLS) — Clause 5.4
The structure must remain **comfortable and functional** under working loads. SLS checks include:
- **Deflection limits** (Cl. 23.2 of IS 456:2000) — typically **L/250** for deflection
- **Crack width limits** (Cl. 35.3.2) — max **0.3 mm** for normal exposure
- **Vibration limits** (Cl. 23.3)
- **Durability** (Cl. 8) — min cement content, max w/c ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: In the 14-mark "Ultimate Limit State" derivation questions, students are expected to derive the **stress block parameters** (k, j, x_u/d) — these are foundational. Memorize the steps for flexural design of singly and doubly reinforced beams.

## 2.2 Design Philosophy — The "Master Equation"

The **single governing equation** of all Limit State Design is:

$$\boxed{\text{Design Strength} \geq \text{Design Load Effect}}$$

Which translates mathematically to:

$$R_d = \frac{R_k}{\gamma_m} \geq S_d = \gamma_f \times S_k$$

Where:
- $R_d$ = Design strength (resistance)
- $R_k$ = Characteristic strength (resistance)
- $S_d$ = Design load effect (action)
- $S_k$ = Characteristic load effect (action)
- $\gamma_m$ = Partial safety factor for material
- $\gamma_f$ = Partial safety factor for load

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Trap**: Students often confuse $\gamma_m$ with $\gamma_f$. Remember:
> - $\gamma_m$ **REDUCES** strength (divides) → material is weaker than tested
> - $\gamma_f$ **INCREASES** load (multiplies) → applied load is higher than nominal
> - This creates a **safety buffer** on BOTH sides of the equation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Formula | Description | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_d = \dfrac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_c}$ | **Design compressive strength of concrete** | IS 456, Cl. 5.1.4 |
| 2 | $f_{yd} = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_s}$ | **Design yield strength of steel** | IS 456, Cl. 5.1.4 |
| 3 | $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | **Short-term modulus of elasticity of concrete** | IS 456, Cl. 6.2.3.1 |
| 4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\text{modular ratio factor}}{\gamma_m}$ | **Effective modular ratio** | IS 456, Cl. B-1.3 |
| 5 | $f_s = 0.58 f_y \dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d}$ | **Steel stress in LSM flexure** | IS 456, Cl. G-1.1 |
| 6 | $p_{t,\text{lim}} = \dfrac{0.36 f_{ck} x_{u,\text{max}}/d}{0.87 f_y}$ | **Limiting percentage of steel** | IS 456, SP-16 |
| 7 | $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 \dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d}(1 - 0.42\dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d}) f_{ck} b d^2$ | **Limiting moment of resistance** | IS 456, Annex G |
| 8 | $V_{u,\text{lim}} = \tau_c \, b \, d$ | **Nominal shear strength** | IS 456, Table 19 |
| 9 | $\rho_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{0.85}{f_y}$ | **Minimum tensile steel in beams** | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.1 |
| 10 | $\dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d} = 0.48$ for Fe415 | **Limiting neutral axis depth factor** | IS 456, Annex G |
| 11 | $\dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d} = 0.53$ for Fe500 | **Limiting neutral axis depth factor** | IS 456, Annex G |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU LaTeX Rule**: In all table cells, use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value — never the literal pipe `|` character, as it breaks Markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Load Combinations (IS 456:2000, Table 5.3.1)

For **Limit State of Collapse**, the following load combinations **MUST** be considered:

| Combination | Dead Load (DL) | Live Load (LL) | Wind Load (WL) | Seismic (EQ) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1.5 | 1.5 | — | — |
| 2 | 1.5 | — | 1.5 | — |
| 3 | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.2 | — |
| 4 | 1.2 | 1.2 | — | 1.2 |
| 5 | 1.5 | — | — | 1.5 |
| 6 | 0.9 | — | 1.5 | — |
| 7 | 0.9 | 1.5 | — | — |
| 8 | 1.5 (counter) | 0.7 (LL) | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combination 8** (DL counter to LL) is the **most commonly missed** combination. This accounts for cases where a member is primarily in **tension** (uplift, cantilever) and dead load is the *only* stabilizing force. KTU board questions frequently test this.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Limit State Method is the **backbone** of:
- **High-rise RCC buildings** (column design, shear wall coupling)
- **Long-span bridges** (box girders, segmental construction)
- **Nuclear containment structures** (pre-stressed concrete domes)
- **Offshore platforms** (corrosion + fatigue + wave loading)
- **Nuclear waste storage vessels** (radiation + thermal + seismic)

Every skyscraper you see in Kochi, Bengaluru, or Mumbai was designed using **IS 456 LSM** principles — making this Module 1 the most critical foundation for the entire course.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Characteristic Strength (Statistical Basis)

### Step 1 — Define the Problem
Concrete is a **heterogeneous** material. Even with identical mix proportions, two cubes cast from the same batch will give different compressive strengths. We need a **statistically meaningful** definition of "strength."

### Step 2 — Assume a Probability Distribution
Tests on thousands of cubes show that the strength distribution approximates a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**:

$$f(f_c) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\dfrac{(f_c - f_m)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

Where:
- $f_m$ = Mean strength
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation
- $f_c$ = Individual cube strength

### Step 3 — Apply the 5% Fractile Rule
The **characteristic strength** $f_{ck}$ is defined as the strength **below which not more than 5% of test results are expected to fall**.

For a normal distribution, the 5% fractile corresponds to a value **1.65 standard deviations below the mean**:

$$f_{ck} = f_m - 1.645\,\sigma$$

For the KTU numerical, this is often simplified to:

$$f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65\,\sigma$$

> **Conversion Logic**: The factor **1.645** comes from the standard normal table. For a one-tailed test at 5%, $P(Z < -1.645) = 0.05$. This is the statistical backbone of safety in concrete design.

### Step 4 — Establish the Standard Deviation (IS 456, Cl. 4.4)
As per IS 456, for a **good degree of quality control**:
- $\sigma \approx 5.0$ N/mm² (for M20 and above)

Therefore:

$$f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65 \times 5.0 = f_m - 8.25 \text{ N/mm²}$$

So a concrete with mean strength $f_m = 30$ N/mm² has $f_{ck} \approx 21.75$ N/mm², classified as **M20**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Stress-Strain Curve of Concrete (IS 456 Idealized)

### Step 1 — Empirical Basis
From lab tests on standard cylinders (150 mm dia × 300 mm height), the **real** stress-strain curve is:
- Linear-elastic up to ~30% of ultimate strength
- Non-linear with progressive micro-cracking
- Peak at strain $\epsilon_0 \approx 0.002$
- Descending branch (strain-softening)

### Step 2 — Idealization for Design
IS 456 (Cl. 6.2.1) simplifies this to a **Parabolic-Rectangular** curve:

$$f_c = 0.446\,f_{ck} \cdot \left[1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{\epsilon_c}{0.002}\right)^2\right] \quad \text{for } \epsilon_c \leq 0.002$$

$$f_c = 0.446\,f_{ck} \quad \text{for } 0.002 \leq \epsilon_c \leq 0.0035$$

> **Conversion Logic**: The factor **0.446** comes from: average stress in parabolic portion = (2/3) × peak stress, and peak stress = $0.67 f_{ck}$ (reduction factor for sustained loading and size effect). So:
> $$\frac{2}{3} \times 0.67 \times f_{ck} = 0.4467 \approx 0.446\,f_{ck}$$

### Step 3 — Design Stress-Strain Curve
For **ultimate limit state design**, IS 456 (Annex G) further simplifies to a **Rectangular Stress Block**:

$$f_c = 0.446\,f_{ck} \quad \text{(constant) over depth } 0.42\,x_u$$

Where $x_u$ is the **neutral axis depth** at ultimate state.

### Step 4 — Area of Rectangular Stress Block
The **compressive force** in concrete is:

$$C_u = 0.446\,f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot (0.42\,x_u) = 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_u$$

> **Conversion Logic**: The factor **0.36** is the **KTU-highest-weight constant** — it appears in:
> - Limiting moment of resistance
> - Compressive force in singly reinforced sections
> - SP-16 design charts (Table 2 to Table 5)
>
> **Memorize it.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Limiting Moment of Resistance (Most Asked in KTU)

### Step 1 — Define the Section
Consider a **singly reinforced rectangular beam**:
- Width: $b$
- Effective depth: $d$
- Area of tension steel: $A_{st}$
- Neutral axis depth: $x_u$

### Step 2 — Equilibrium of Forces
At ULS, the **compressive force** in concrete equals the **tensile force** in steel:

$$C_u = T_u$$

Substituting:

$$0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_u = 0.87\,f_y\,A_{st}$$

> **Conversion Logic**: The factor **0.87** on $f_y$ accounts for:
> - Partial safety factor $\gamma_s = 1.15$ → design $f_y = f_y/1.15 = 0.87 f_y$
> - Strain-hardening loss (strain at yield is 0.002, but max useful strain is 0.0035)

### Step 3 — Solve for $A_{st}$ (Steel Required)

$$A_{st} = \dfrac{0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_u}{0.87\,f_y}$$

### Step 4 — Express $x_u$ in Terms of Material Constants
The **maximum** allowable neutral axis depth (for ductile failure) is:

$$x_{u,\text{max}} = k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot d$$

Where:
- $k_1 = 0.446$ (stress block parameter)
- $k_2 = 0.002/\epsilon_c$ where $\epsilon_c = 0.0035$ → $k_2 = 0.572$
- Therefore: $x_{u,\text{max}}/d = 0.446 \times 0.572 = 0.256$ ... wait, this is incorrect.

**Correct derivation** (per IS 456, Annex G):

The condition is that the **bottom fibre steel reaches $f_y$** before concrete crushes. Strain compatibility gives:

$$\dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d} = \dfrac{0.0035}{0.0035 + (f_y / (1.15 \times E_s)) + 0.002}$$

For **Fe415** ($f_y = 415$ N/mm², $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²):

$$\dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d} = \dfrac{0.0035}{0.0035 + (415 / 2.3 \times 10^5) + 0.002} = \dfrac{0.0035}{0.0035 + 0.001804 + 0.002} = \dfrac{0.0035}{0.007304} = 0.479 \approx 0.48$$

For **Fe500**:

$$\dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d} = \dfrac{0.0035}{0.0035 + 0.002174 + 0.002} = \dfrac{0.0035}{0.007674} = 0.456 \approx 0.46$$

> **Note**: IS 456 specifies **0.48 for Fe415** and **0.46 for Fe500** to ensure ductile failure mode.

### Step 5 — Substitute $x_{u,\text{max}}$ to Get $M_{u,\text{lim}}$
Taking moments about the **centroid of compressive force** (at depth $0.42 x_u$ from top):

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = T_u \cdot z = 0.87 f_y A_{st,\text{lim}} \cdot (d - 0.42 x_{u,\text{max}})$$

Substituting $A_{st,\text{lim}}$ from equilibrium:

$$A_{st,\text{lim}} = \dfrac{0.36 f_{ck} b x_{u,\text{max}}}{0.87 f_y}$$

So:

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.87 f_y \cdot \dfrac{0.36 f_{ck} b x_{u,\text{max}}}{0.87 f_y} \cdot (d - 0.42 x_{u,\text{max}})$$

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 f_{ck} b x_{u,\text{max}} (d - 0.42 x_{u,\text{max}})$$

Letting $k = x_{u,\text{max}}/d$:

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 f_{ck} b d^2 \cdot k(1 - 0.42 k)$$

For **Fe415** ($k = 0.48$):

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 f_{ck} b d^2 \times 0.48 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.48)$$

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 f_{ck} b d^2 \times 0.48 \times 0.7984$$

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$$

> **Conversion Logic**: The constant **0.138** for Fe415 and **0.133** for Fe500 are the KTU board favorites. They appear in the SP-16 design charts as the Y-axis intercept.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Modular Ratio for Crack-Width Calculations

### Step 1 — Definition
The **modular ratio** $m$ is the ratio of the modulus of elasticity of steel to that of concrete:

$$m = \dfrac{E_s}{E_c} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5}{5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}} = \dfrac{40}{\sqrt{f_{ck}}}$$

### Step 2 — Effective Modular Ratio (for LSM)
In LSM, since concrete's strength is reduced by $\gamma_c = 1.5$, the **effective** modular ratio is:

$$m_{eff} = \dfrac{40}{\sqrt{f_{ck}} \cdot \gamma_c} = \dfrac{40}{1.5 \sqrt{f_{ck}}} = \dfrac{26.67}{\sqrt{f_{ck}}}$$

> **Conversion Logic**: This reduced modular ratio is used only for computing **deflection** and **crack width** in SLS checks, NOT for strength design. KTU questions on this are common in Part A.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Effective Depth of a Beam (Rules of Thumb)

### Step 1 — Empirical Deflection Control
For simply supported beams with span $L$, IS 456 Cl. 23.2.1 specifies:

$$L/d = \begin{cases} 20 & \text{for simply supported} \\ 26 & \text{for continuous} \\ 7 & \text{for cantilevers} \end{cases}$$

These are BASIC ratios. They must be **multiplied by modification factors** $k_1, k_2, k_3$ for:
- $k_1$ = tension reinforcement percentage (Table 13, IS 456)
- $k_2$ = compression reinforcement (Table 14, IS 456)
- $k_3$ = span type (Table 15, IS 456)

### Step 2 — Typical Effective Depth
For a typical **residential beam** (Fe415, 0.5% steel, simply supported, 5 m span):

$$d = \dfrac{L}{20} = \dfrac{5000}{20} = 250 \text{ mm}$$

Total depth = $d + \text{effective cover} = 250 + 50 = 300$ mm (overall).

> **Conversion Logic**: Effective cover = clear cover + (half of bar diameter) = 25 + (16/2) = 33 mm. Rounded to 40-50 mm for design simplicity. KTU examiners expect students to know this **rule-of-thumb** sizing for preliminary design.

## 3.6 Master Summary Table — All KTU Module 1 Constants

| Symbol | Name | Value | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\gamma_c$ | Partial safety factor (concrete) | **1.50** | $f_d = f_{ck}/\gamma_c$ |
| $\gamma_s$ | Partial safety factor (steel) | **1.15** | $f_{yd} = f_y/\gamma_s$ |
| 0.446 | Peak stress factor in parabola | **0.446** | Stress block |
| 0.36 | Area factor for rectangular block | **0.36** | $C_u = 0.36 f_{ck} b x_u$ |
| 0.42 | Depth factor for rectangular block | **0.42** | Depth of CGS |
| 0.87 | Effective steel yield factor | **0.87** | $T_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st}$ |
| 0.48 | $x_{u,\text{max}}/d$ for Fe415 | **0.48** | Ductile failure check |
| 0.46 | $x_{u,\text{max}}/d$ for Fe500 | **0.46** | Ductile failure check |
| 0.138 | $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ coeff for Fe415 | **0.138** | $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ |
| 0.133 | $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ coeff for Fe500 | **0.133** | $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.133 f_{ck} b d^2$ |
| 5000 | $E_c$ formula coefficient | **5000** | $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ |
| 0.002 | Concrete strain at peak stress | **0.002** | Parabolic curve |
| 0.0035 | Ultimate concrete strain | **0.0035** | Rectangular block limit |
| 0.85/$f_y$ | Minimum steel ratio | **$0.85/f_y$** | $\rho_{min}$ for beams |
| 0.12% | Min steel for slabs | **0.12%** | Fe415 slabs |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Evolution of Concrete Design Philosophy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pre 1953 Empirical Rules Masonry Plain Concrete"] --> B["1953 1970 WSM Era IS 456 1957 Working Stress Method Single FS 3 0"]
    B --> C["1970 2000 Transitional Era IS 456 1978 WSM plus ULM Coexistence"]
    C --> D["2000 Present LSM Dominant IS 456 2000 Limit State Method Two Criteria"]
    D --> E["Future IS 456 2024 Draft Performance Based Design"]
    
    A --- A1["Safety via Past Experience No Math"]
    B --- B1["Elastic Theory FS 3 0 Bulky Sections"]
    C --- C1["ULM used for Columns WSM for Beams Confusion"]
    D --- D1["ULS plus SLS Statistical Basis IS 456 2000"]
    E --- E1["Reliability Index Beta Methods Probabilistic"]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style B fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#98FB98
    style D fill:#87CEEB
    style E fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Limit State Method Architecture (The Two-Branch Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LS["Limit State Method IS 456 Cl 2 3 2"]
    
    LS --> ULS["Ultimate Limit State ULS Cl 5 3"]
    LS --> SLS["Serviceability Limit State SLS Cl 5 4"]
    
    ULS --> U1["Flexure Bending Failure"]
    ULS --> U2["Shear Diagonal Tension"]
    ULS --> U3["Torsion Twisting"]
    ULS --> U4["Compression Axial plus Bending"]
    ULS --> U5["Buckling Column Slenderness"]
    ULS --> U6["Punching Shear Slabs"]
    ULS --> U7["Fatigue Cyclic Loading"]
    
    SLS --> S1["Deflection L over 250 L over 350"]
    SLS --> S2["Crack Width Max 0 3 mm Normal Exposure"]
    SLS --> S3["Vibration Floor Systems"]
    SLS --> S4["Durability Min Cement Max w over c"]
    SLS --> S5["Fire Resistance IS 1642"]
    
    U1 --> U1A["Singly Reinforced Beams"]
    U1 --> U1B["Doubly Reinforced Beams"]
    U1 --> U1C["Flanged Beams T and L"]
    U1 --> U1D["Continuous Beams"]
    
    S2 --> S2A["Flexural Cracks 0 3 mm"]
    S2 --> S2B["Shear Cracks 0 3 mm"]
    S2 --> S2C["Combined Tension plus Shear"]
    
    style LS fill:#FF6347,color:#fff
    style ULS fill:#FFA500
    style SLS fill:#32CD32
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — LSM Design Process Flow (Step-by-Step Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1 Load Assessment DL LL WL EQ as per IS 875"]
    P2["Step 2 Load Combinations Table 5 3 1 IS 456 8 Combinations"]
    P3["Step 3 Structural Analysis Bending Moment Shear Force Axial Force"]
    P4["Step 4 Member Sizing Preliminary L over d Rules and Material Choice"]
    P5["Step 5 ULS Design Check Mu_lim versus Mu_actual"]
    P6["Step 6 SLS Check Deflection Crack Width"]
    P7["Step 7 Detailing Anchorage Lap Splicing as per SP 34"]
    P8["Step 8 Drawing and BBS Bar Bending Schedule"]
    
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9["Final Safe Economical Design"]
    
    P5 -.->|"If Mu over Mu_lim greater than 1"| P5A["Redesign as Doubly Reinforced or Increase Section"]
    P5A --> P5
    P6 -.->|"If Deflection Exceeds Limit"| P6A["Increase Depth or Add Compression Steel"]
    P6A --> P6
    
    style P1 fill:#FFD700
    style P5 fill:#FF6347
    style P6 fill:#32CD32
    style P9 fill:#9370DB
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Stress-Strain Idealization Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Real["Real Lab Curve"]
        R1["Linear Elastic up to 0 3 fck"]
        R2["Nonlinear Micro Cracking"]
        R3["Peak at 0 002 strain"]
        R4["Descending Branch Strain Softening"]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    end
    
    subgraph IS1["IS 456 Parabolic Rectangular Cl 6 2 1"]
        I1["Parabola Peak 0 446 fck at 0 002"]
        I2["Plateau 0 446 fck from 0 002 to 0 0035"]

    end
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Limit State" as per IS 456:2000. List the two main categories of limit states.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **limit state** is a state of impending failure of a structure beyond which it ceases to perform its intended function satisfactorily in terms of safety and serviceability (1.5 Marks).

The two main categories of limit states as per IS 456:2000 are:
1. **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** — pertaining to safety and collapse prevention (0.75 Mark)
2. **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** — pertaining to comfort, deflection, and crack control (0.75 Mark)

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the values of partial safety factors for (a) concrete and (b) steel as per IS 456:2000. Why are they different?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

(a) Partial safety factor for concrete: $\gamma_c = \mathbf{1.50}$ (1 Mark)
(b) Partial safety factor for steel: $\gamma_s = \mathbf{1.15}$ (1 Mark)

**Reason for difference (1 Mark):**
Concrete has higher variability in strength (±15-20% scatter) compared to steel (±5-10% scatter) due to its heterogeneous nature, casting and curing conditions. Therefore, a higher safety factor is assigned to concrete.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 1 Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (A): [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Choice A]
**(a)** Explain the philosophy of the **Limit State Method of Design**. Compare it with the Working Stress Method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported RCC beam has a clear span of **6 m**, support width 300 mm, factored bending moment $M_u = 250$ kN·m, and effective depth $d = 500$ mm. Use **M20 concrete and Fe415 steel**. Check if the section is **singly reinforced or needs doubly reinforced** design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — LSM Philosophy (7 Marks)

The **Limit State Method** is a probability-based design philosophy codified in IS 456:2000 that ensures the structure satisfies **two criteria simultaneously**:

**1. Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** — The structure must not collapse under factored loads. The design equation is:
$$\boxed{R_d = \dfrac{R_k}{\gamma_m} \geq S_d = \gamma_f \times S_k}$$

**2. Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** — The structure must perform satisfactorily under service loads (deflection, cracking, vibration within limits).

**Comparison with WSM:**

| Aspect | WSM (Old) | LSM (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Elastic theory | Probabilistic + Statistics |
| Safety factor | Single FS (~3.0) | Two partial factors ($\gamma_f$ and $\gamma_m$) |
| Failure check | Stress check only | Strength + Serviceability |
| Material utilization | Conservative (bulky) | Optimized (economical) |
| Load consideration | Single working load | Multiple factored combinations |
| Code reference | IS 456:1978 (old) | IS 456:2000 (current) |

**Advantages of LSM (2 Marks):**
- Economical use of materials (saves 15-25% steel)
- Considers both ULS and SLS
- Different safety factors for dead load, live load, wind, and seismic
- Based on characteristic strength (5% fractile) — scientifically sound

**Disadvantages of LSM (1 Mark):**
- Computationally intensive
- Requires checks of multiple load combinations
- Designer needs higher analytical skills

#### Part (b) — Numerical Check (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $L_{\text{clear}} = 6$ m, support = 300 mm
- Effective span: $L_{\text{eff}} = 6.0 + 0.3 = 6.3$ m
- $M_u = 250$ kN·m = $250 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- $b = 300$ mm (assumed), $d = 500$ mm
- $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm²

**Step 1:** Compute $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ for Fe415:

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$$

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times (500)^2$$

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times 250000$$

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 207 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 207 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2:** Compare $M_u$ with $M_{u,\text{lim}}$:

$$M_u = 250 \text{ kN·m} > M_{u,\text{lim}} = 207 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3: Conclusion (1 Mark)**

Since $M_u > M_{u,\text{lim}}$, the section **CANNOT** be designed as singly reinforced. The section must be **redesigned** as either:
1. **Doubly reinforced section** (add compression steel $A_{sc}$) — economical if section size is fixed, OR
2. **Increase section dimensions** — recompute with $d$ such that $M_{u,\text{lim}} \geq M_u$.

**For option 2:** Required $d$:

$$d_{\text{req}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_u}{0.138 f_{ck} b}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{250 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 300}}$$

$$d_{\text{req}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{250 \times 10^6}{828000}} = \sqrt{301932} = 549.5 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt $d = 550$ mm and overall depth $D = 600$ mm (1 Mark for adopting standard).

---

### Question B1 (B): [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Choice B]
**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **idealized stress-strain curves** for concrete and steel as per IS 456:2000. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular beam 250 mm wide × 500 mm effective depth is reinforced with 4 bars of 16 mm diameter (Fe415). Use M25 concrete. Check whether the section is **under-reinforced, balanced, or over-reinforced**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Stress-Strain Curves (7 Marks)

**Concrete — Parabolic-Rectangular Curve (IS 456, Cl. 6.2.1):**

For $0 \leq \epsilon_c \leq 0.002$ (rising parabolic portion):
$$\sigma_c = 0.446 f_{ck}\left[1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{\epsilon_c}{0.002}\right)^2\right]$$

For $0.002 \leq \epsilon_c \leq 0.0035$ (rectangular plateau):
$$\sigma_c = 0.446 f_{ck} = \text{constant}$$

**Key Points of Concrete Curve (3 Marks):**
- **Modulus of Elasticity**: $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm²
- **Peak strain**: $\epsilon_0 = 0.002$ (at maximum stress $0.446 f_{ck}$)
- **Ultimate strain**: $\epsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$
- **Failure type**: Brittle; gains strength up to 0.002 strain, then softens

**Steel — Idealized Elasto-Plastic Curve (IS 456, Cl. 6.2.2):**

For $\epsilon_s \leq f_y / E_s$ (elastic):
$$\sigma_s = E_s \cdot \epsilon_s$$

For $\epsilon_s \geq f_y / E_s$ (perfectly plastic plateau):
$$\sigma_s = f_y = \text{constant}$$

**Key Points of Steel Curve (2 Marks):**
- **Modulus of Elasticity**: $E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- **Yield strain**: $\epsilon_y = f_y / E_s$ (e.g., 0.00207 for Fe415)
- **Ultimate strain**: $\epsilon_{su} \geq 0.012$ (for ductility)
- **Failure type**: Ductile; yields at $f_y$, then large plastic deformation
- **No strain hardening** considered in LSM

**Design Simplification:** For ULS design, the rectangular stress block (constant $0.446 f_{ck}$ over $0.42 x_u$ depth) is used. For steel, the design yield strength is $f_{yd} = 0.87 f_y$.

#### Part (b) — Under/Over-Reinforced Check (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $b = 250$ mm, $d = 500$ mm
- $A_{st} = 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25$ mm²
- $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm²

**Step 1:** Compute the actual neutral axis depth from equilibrium:

$$0.36 f_{ck} b x_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st}$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \times 415 \times 804.25}{0.36 \times 25 \times 250}$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{290423}{2250} = 129.08 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the actual $x_u / d$ ratio:

$$\dfrac{x_u}{d} = \dfrac{129.08}{500} = 0.258$$

**Step 3:** Compare with limiting value for Fe415:

$$\dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d} = 0.48$$

**Step 4:** Classification (1 Mark)

Since $x_u/d = 0.258 < 0.48 = x_{u,\text{max}}/d$, the section is **UNDER-REINFORCED** (1 Mark).

**Step 5:** Calculate Moment of Resistance (1 Mark):

$$M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} (d - 0.42 x_u)$$

$$M_u = 0.87 \times 415 \times 804.25 \times (500 - 0.42 \times 129.08)$$

$$M_u = 290423 \times (500 - 54.21)$$

$$M_u = 290423 \times 445.79 = 129.45 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 129.45 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 6:** Verify minimum steel (1 Mark):

$$A_{st,\text{min}} = \dfrac{0.85}{f_y} \cdot b \cdot d = \dfrac{0.85}{415} \times 250 \times 500 = 256 \text{ mm}^2$$

Since $A_{st} = 804.25 \text{ mm}^2 > A_{st,\text{min}} = 256 \text{ mm}^2$ → OK

**Conclusion:** The section is **under-reinforced, safe, and ductile** (will give ample warning before failure). 

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 PITFALLS WHERE KTU STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN MODULE 1:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.87 factor on $f_y$**: When computing $T_u$, you MUST use $0.87 f_y$, not $f_y$. This is worth 1-2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Wrong $x_{u,\text{max}}/d$ value**: Students use 0.48 for Fe500 or 0.46 for Fe415 — code-specific. **Memorize the table.**
> 
> 3. **Using mean strength instead of characteristic strength**: Always $f_{ck}$, never $f_m$ in design equations.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the 0.42 in lever arm**: $z = d - 0.42 x_u$, NOT $d - 0.5 x_u$ (which is for elastic theory). The 0.42 comes from the centroid of the rectangular stress block.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting minimum steel check**: IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1 requires $A_{st,\text{min}} = 0.85 b d / f_y$ for beams. If $A_{st,provided} < A_{st,\text{min}}$, section is unsafe even if $M_u < M_{u,\text{lim}}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MODULE 1 RAPID REVISION — MUST MEMORIZE:**

**Definition Block:**
- ☐ Limit State = state of impending failure (IS 456, Cl. 2.3.2)
- ☐ ULS = Collapse prevention; SLS = Comfort + Durability
- ☐ Characteristic strength = 5% fractile = $f_m - 1.65\sigma$

**Numerical Constants (KTU Board Favorites):**
- ☐ $\gamma_c = 1.50$, $\gamma_s = 1.15$
- ☐ 0.446 (peak stress factor), 0.36 (block area factor), 0.42 (block depth factor)
- ☐ 0.87 (effective steel yield), 0.48 for Fe415, 0.46 for Fe500
- ☐ $0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ = $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ for Fe415
- ☐ $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$, $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

**Equations to Derive (within 3 minutes):**
- ☐ $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ (for Fe415)
- ☐ $A_{st} = (0.5 f_{ck}/f_y)[1 - \sqrt{1 - (4.6 M_u/(f_{ck} b d^2))}] \cdot b d$
- ☐ Lever arm equation: $z = d - 0.42 x_u$

**Load Combinations (Table 5.3.1):**
- ☐ 8 standard combinations — memorize all
- ☐ Most missed: Combination 8 (DL counter to LL) for tension members

**Design Philosophy Check:**
- ☐ $R_d = R_k / \gamma_m \geq S_d = \gamma_f \times S_k$
- ☐ Always check BOTH ULS and SLS
- ☐ Under-reinforced section ($x_u < x_{u,\text{max}}$) → Ductile failure → Safe
- ☐ Over-reinforced section ($x_u > x_{u,\text{max}}$) → Brittle failure → **NEVER ALLOWED**

**Practical Engineering Wisdom:**
- ☐ For preliminary design, use $L/d = 20$ (simply supported), $L/d = 26$ (continuous)
- ☐ Min steel in beams: $0.85 b d / f_y$
- ☐ Min steel in slabs: 0.12% of gross cross-sectional area (for Fe415)
- ☐ Effective cover: 25-50 mm for beams, 15-20 mm for slabs

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — ADVANCED DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES (PECET631)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET631 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis and Design of Rectangular Portal Frames for Vertical Loading, Approximate Methods for Structural Analysis and Design for Vertical Loads, Pattern Loading, Lateral Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rectangular Portal Frame — Formal Definition

A **Rectangular Portal Frame** is a two-dimensional, statically indeterminate rigid frame consisting of two vertical columns rigidly connected to one (or more) horizontal beam(s) (rafters), forming a closed rectangular bay. The joints between the columns and beams are **rigid (moment-resisting) connections** capable of transmitting axial forces, shear forces, and bending moments.

In reinforced concrete construction, portal frames are commonly used for:
- Single-storey industrial sheds
- Ground-floor frames of multi-storey buildings
- Flyovers and underpasses
- Pump houses and culvert portals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The frame is *rigidly jointed* (monolithic). A true pin/hinge at the beam-column joint converts the structure into a three-hinged or two-hinged arch, which is **not** classified as a portal frame in the design sense.

**Key Geometric Parameters:**
- Span of frame, $L$ (clear span between column centres)
- Height of column, $H_c$ (from base to soffit of beam)
- Total height, $H = H_c + h_b$ (where $h_b$ = depth of beam)
- Cross-section: Columns $b_c \times d_c$ ; Beam $b_b \times d_b$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Imagine a Doorway of Concrete"

> 🏛️ **Real-World Analogy:** Stand in front of a typical concrete doorway. The two vertical door jambs are the **columns**, and the horizontal lintel beam on top is the **rafter (beam)**. Now imagine that the lintel is **not** resting on the jambs — it is *cast monolithically* (poured in one continuous pour) so that the corners where they meet **cannot rotate independently**. That rigid corner is the *portal action*. If you push the doorway sideways, the entire frame sways as one rigid unit, generating bending in both columns and the beam — this is the *portal effect*.

When gravity loads (say, a heavy slab) push down on the beam:
- The beam tends to **bend downward at its centre** (positive sagging moment).
- Because the ends are *clamped* to the columns, the corners resist rotation, generating **negative (hogging) moments at the joints**.
- The columns bend in *double curvature*, picking up axial compression plus bending.

This is fundamentally different from a simply-supported beam because the joints **lock in rotational continuity**.

---

## 1.3 Approximate Methods of Analysis — Formal Overview

Because portal frames are highly indeterminate (a single-bay, single-storey frame is **3 times statically indeterminate**), exact methods like Slope-Deflection or Matrix Stiffness can be tedious for preliminary design and for hand calculation in design offices. Hence, **Approximate Methods** were developed that rely on simplified assumptions about the location of *inflection points* (points of zero bending moment).

| Method | Best Suited For | Key Assumption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Portal Method** | Low-to-medium rise frames under **lateral load** | Inflection at mid-height of each column and mid-span of each beam |
| **Cantilever Method** | Tall frames under **vertical loads** | Inflection at mid-height of columns; axial force in columns varies linearly with distance from centroid |
| **Factor (Substitute-Frame) Method** | Continuous beams and frames under **vertical gravity loads** | Treat the structure at each storey as a substitute frame consisting of the beam and the columns immediately above and below, with far ends fixed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Inflection Point:** A point along the length of a flexural member where the **bending moment equals zero** and the curvature changes sign (the elastic curve changes from concave-up to concave-down or vice-versa). It is also referred to as the *point of contraflexure*.

---

## 1.4 Pattern Loading — Formal Definition

**Pattern Loading** (also called *checkerboard loading* or *skip loading*) is a live-load arrangement strategy used in the design of **continuous beams and rigid frames** to obtain the **maximum possible design moments and shears** at critical sections. Only a selected pattern of spans is loaded with live load, while the dead load is placed on all spans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pattern loading?** Continuous beams and frames exhibit *influence lines* that cross zero or change sign along the structure. Placing live load on *every* span simultaneously rarely produces the worst-case moment at any single section. KTU examiners love to test this concept because it is the basis of economical design of continuous members.

---

## 1.5 Lateral Loads on Portal Frames — Formal Definition

**Lateral Loads** are horizontal forces acting on a structure, primarily arising from:
- **Wind pressure** (governed by IS 875 Part 3 : 2015)
- **Earthquake ground motion** (governed by IS 1893 Part 1 : 2016)
- **Imposed sway** during construction or accidental eccentricity

For portal frames, the lateral load $V$ at any level acts at the **roof/floor level** and is resisted by the columns in *double curvature*, with shear distributed by the chosen analytical method.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Common Mistake:** Students often forget that for a *single-bay* portal, **both columns must share the lateral shear equally** under the portal method (since both are "interior" in the sense that the assumption is per panel, not per column). The exterior/interior distinction only applies to **multi-bay** frames.

---

## 1.6 Summary Snapshot of Section 1

| Term | One-Line Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Portal Frame** | Two-hinged-base, rigid-jointed rectangular frame |
| **Indeterminacy** | 3 (for single-bay, single-storey) |
| **Portal Method** | Inflection points at mid-span of all members |
| **Cantilever Method** | Inflection at mid-column; axial force linear with $x$ |
| **Pattern Loading** | Selective span loading to maximise design moments |
| **Lateral Load** | Horizontal force (wind or seismic) at roof level |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Statically Indeterminate Degree for Portal Frames

For a planar closed rectangular frame:

$$ \text{Degree of Indeterminacy, } D_s = 3C - R - m $$

Where:
- $C$ = number of closed loops (for a single-bay portal, $C = 1$)
- $R$ = number of released reactions (e.g., pinned bases release moment)
- $m$ = number of internal hinges / releases

For a **fixed-base single-bay single-storey portal frame**:
- $C = 1$, $R = 6$ (3 reactions × 2 supports), $m = 0$
- $D_s = 3(1) - 6 - 0 = -3$ (which means **3 degrees redundancy**)

For **fixed-base single-bay, two-storey** portal frame:
- $C = 2$, $R = 6$, $m = 0$
- $D_s = 3(2) - 6 = 0$ — wait, recompute for closed loops:
  - Each storey adds 1 closed loop → 2 loops
- $D_s = 3(2) - 6 = 0$? No — recheck:
  - For a 2-storey 1-bay portal: storeys = 2, bays = 1
  - Indeterminacy = $3 \times (\text{storeys}) - 3 = 3 \times 2 - 3 = 3$? 

Let me use the **standard KTU textbook formula**:

$$ D_s = 3m - 3n - r $$

Where $m$ = number of members, $n$ = number of joints, $r$ = number of reaction components. For fixed supports, each contributes 3 reactions.

For single-bay single-storey (3 members, 4 joints, 6 reactions):
$$ D_s = 3(3) - 3(4) + 6 = 9 - 12 + 6 = 3 \;\checkmark $$

For single-bay two-storey (5 members, 6 joints, 6 reactions):
$$ D_s = 3(5) - 3(6) + 6 = 15 - 18 + 6 = 3 \;\checkmark $$

For single-bay three-storey:
$$ D_s = 3(7) - 3(8) + 6 = 21 - 24 + 6 = 3 \;\checkmark $$

> 🎯 **Pattern:** For a **single-bay, multi-storey fixed-base portal frame, the degree of static indeterminacy is always 3**, regardless of the number of storeys. This is a famous result and a common viva question at KTU!

---

## 2.2 Fixed End Moments (FEMs) for Common Loadings

These are the **starting points** of moment distribution / slope-deflection analysis. For a beam (or column) of length $L$ with both ends fixed:

| Loading Case | FEM at Near End (FEM_AB) | FEM at Far End (FEM_BA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Point load $P$ at distance $a$ from A, $b = L-a$ from B | $-\dfrac{Pab^2}{L^2}$ | $+\dfrac{Pa^2 b}{L^2}$ |
| UDL $w$ over full span | $-\dfrac{wL^2}{12}$ | $+\dfrac{wL^2}{12}$ |
| Concentrated moment $M_0$ at A | $+M_0 \cdot \dfrac{b}{L} \cdot \left(2 - \dfrac{3b}{L}\right)$ ... *use table* | $-M_0 \cdot \dfrac{a}{L} \cdot \left(2 - \dfrac{3a}{L}\right)$ |
| Triangular load (0 at A, $w$ at B) | $-\dfrac{wL^2}{30}$ | $+\dfrac{wL^2}{20}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** Clockwise moments acting on the **member ends** are taken as **positive**. Therefore, a FEM producing *clockwise rotation at the joint* is positive. The moments applied *by the joint on the member end* (or vice-versa) follow Newton's third law with opposite signs.

---

## 2.3 Portal Method — Detailed Assumptions and Formulas

### 2.3.1 Underlying Assumptions
1. An **inflection point** (point of zero moment) exists at the **mid-span of every beam**.
2. An **inflection point** exists at the **mid-height of every column**.
3. The **horizontal shear** at any storey is distributed among the columns of that storey such that:
   - Each **interior column** carries **twice** the shear of an **exterior column**.
   - **Shear per exterior column** = $\dfrac{V}{2n}$ where $V$ = total lateral shear at the storey, $n$ = total number of columns.
   - **Shear per interior column** = $\dfrac{V}{n}$.
4. The shear in each column produces a moment equal to *shear × half-height* at the column top (= bottom).

### 2.3.2 Lateral Load Distribution

For a frame with $N$ bays, total columns $= N + 1$.

| Column Position | Shear Carried |
| :--- | :--- |
| Exterior column (both end columns) | $V_e = \dfrac{V}{2N}$ |
| Interior column (each middle column) | $V_i = \dfrac{V}{N}$ |

Check: $(N+1)\cdot V_{avg} = V$ → Verify with 2 columns in 1 bay:
- Both are "interior" by the per-panel rule: each carries $V/2$ ✓

### 2.3.3 Moment Formulas (Portal Method, Lateral Load)

For a column of height $H_c$ (clear height between supports):
- **Column moment at top** $M_{top} = V_{col} \cdot \dfrac{H_c}{2}$
- **Column moment at bottom** $M_{bot} = -V_{col} \cdot \dfrac{H_c}{2}$

For a beam of length $L$ between columns with end shears $H_1$ (left) and $H_2$ (right):
- **Beam end moment** $M_{left} = H_1 \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$
- **Beam end moment** $M_{right} = H_2 \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$

---

## 2.4 Cantilever Method — Detailed Assumptions and Formulas

### 2.4.1 Underlying Assumptions
1. Inflection points exist at the **mid-height of all columns**.
2. Inflection points exist at the **mid-span of all beams**.
3. The **axial stress** (and hence axial force) in a column is **proportional to its horizontal distance from the centroid of the column layout** at that storey.
4. Equilibrium of vertical forces gives the column axial forces directly once the position of the line of action of total vertical reaction is known.

### 2.4.2 Application to Vertical Loads on Tall Frames

For vertical loads on tall frames, the cantilever method assumes that the building acts like a **vertical cantilever** fixed at the base. The stresses vary linearly about the centroidal axis (a Bernoulli/Euler assumption extended to frames).

For a column at horizontal distance $x_i$ from the centroid of the column layout:
$$ F_i = \dfrac{F \cdot x_i}{\sum x_i^2} \cdot x_i \cdot A_i $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If columns have **equal cross-sectional area**, the axial force simplifies to:
> $$ F_i = \dfrac{F \cdot x_i^2}{\sum x_i^2} $$
> where $F$ = total vertical load at the storey acting with eccentricity.

---

## 2.5 Factor Method (Substitute Frame Method) for Vertical Loads

### 2.5.1 Assumptions
- The frame is analysed one floor at a time
- At each floor, only the **beam** of that floor and the **columns immediately above and below** are considered
- The far ends of the columns (top of upper column and bottom of lower column) are taken as **fixed**
- The beam is analysed with its loads and the resulting moments at the column tops are carried up/down into adjacent floors

### 2.5.2 The Famous ACI Coefficients

The Factor Method is built upon ACI Standard 318 coefficients for **continuous beams and slabs**. For a continuous beam with:
- **Equal spans**
- **Uniformly distributed live load** not exceeding **three times** the dead load
- **Prismatic members**

The coefficients give moments and shears directly:

| Location | Moment Coefficient (M = coeff × wL²) | Shear Coefficient (V = coeff × wL) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Interior negative moment at support** | $\dfrac{1}{10}$ | — |
| **Positive moment in end span** | $\dfrac{1}{11}$ | — |
| **Negative moment at first interior support** | $\dfrac{1}{9}$ | — |
| **Positive moment in interior span** | $\dfrac{1}{14}$ | — |
| **Shear in end span (at face of exterior support)** | — | $0.4$ |
| **Shear in end span (at first interior support)** | — | $0.6$ |
| **Shear in interior span** | — | $0.5$ |

> [!WARNING]
> These coefficients are valid **only when**: (a) live load ≤ 3 × dead load, (b) spans are approximately equal (max-min ratio ≤ 1.2), (c) prismatic members. Outside these limits, **rigorous analysis** is required.

---

## 2.6 Pattern Loading — Influence Lines and Maximum Moments

### 2.6.1 Influence Line for Moment at a Section

For a continuous beam, the influence line for bending moment at a particular section (e.g., at the centre of a span) has:
- **Alternating positive and negative ordinates** along the span
- The live load must be placed on all spans where the influence line ordinate is **positive**, and unloaded where it is **negative**

### 2.6.2 Pattern Loading Rules (Müller-Breslau Principle Application)

For a continuous beam/frame:

| Maximum Effect | Load Pattern |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Maximum positive moment in span $n$** | Load span $n$ and **every alternate** span |
| **Maximum negative moment at support $n$** | Load spans on **both sides** of support $n$ and **alternate** |
| **Maximum shear at support** | Load spans adjacent to the support and **alternate** |
| **Maximum reaction at support** | Load **all spans adjacent** to the support |
| **Maximum positive moment in a span of a frame** | Load **the span and alternate spans**, leaving the rest |
| **Maximum negative moment in a span** | Load **both adjacent spans**, leaving alternate |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "LOAD ALL, SKIP ALL"** : To get *worst positive* moment at a section, load that section's span AND every *other* span, leaving the gaps empty. To get *worst negative*, load the two adjacent spans.

---

## 2.7 Lateral Loads — Wind and Seismic

### 2.7.1 Wind Loads (IS 875 Part 3 : 2015)

The basic wind pressure at a height $z$:
$$ p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2 $$

where $V_z$ is the design wind speed at height $z$ (in m/s), obtained as:
$$ V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4 $$

- $V_b$ = basic wind speed (50-year return period map value from IS 875)
- $k_1$ = probability factor (risk coefficient)
- $k_2$ = terrain, height and structure size factor
- $k_3$ = topography factor
- $k_4$ = importance factor for the cyclonic region

The total wind force on the frame:
$$ F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z $$

where $C_f$ = force coefficient, $A_e$ = effective frontal area.

### 2.7.2 Seismic Loads (IS 1893 Part 1 : 2016)

The base shear for a single-storey portal:
$$ V_B = A_h \cdot W $$

where:
$$ A_h = \dfrac{Z \cdot I \cdot S_a}{2 \cdot R \cdot g} $$

- $Z$ = zone factor (0.10 to 0.36 for Zones II to V)
- $I$ = importance factor (1.0 to 1.5)
- $S_a/g$ = spectral acceleration ratio (from response spectrum)
- $R$ = response reduction factor (for OMRF concrete = 3.0; for SMRF = 5.0)
- $W$ = seismic weight

---

## 2.8 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Degree of indeterminacy (single-bay, fixed base) | $D_s = 3$ | Always 3, any number of storeys |
| 2 | FEM (UDL on beam, both ends fixed) | $\dfrac{wL^2}{12}$ | kN·m |
| 3 | FEM (Point load $P$, both ends fixed) | $\dfrac{Pab^2}{L^2}$ | kN·m (near end) |
| 4 | Column shear (Portal, exterior) | $V_e = \dfrac{V}{2N}$ | $N$ = number of bays |
| 5 | Column shear (Portal, interior) | $V_i = \dfrac{V}{N}$ | kN |
| 6 | Column end moment (Portal) | $M = V_{col} \cdot \dfrac{H_c}{2}$ | kN·m |
| 7 | Beam end moment (Portal) | $M = H \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$ | kN·m |
| 8 | Cantilever method axial force | $F_i = \dfrac{F \cdot x_i^2}{\sum x_i^2}$ | Equal areas |
| 9 | ACI moment coefficient (interior support) | $\dfrac{wL^2}{10}$ | Equal spans |
| 10 | Wind pressure at height $z$ | $p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$ | N/m² |
| 11 | Design wind speed | $V_z = V_b k_1 k_2 k_3 k_4$ | m/s |
| 12 | Base shear (seismic) | $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ | kN |
| 13 | Horizontal seismic coefficient | $A_h = \dfrac{Z I S_a}{2 R g}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Carry-over factor (moment distribution) | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ | for prismatic members |
| 15 | Distribution factor | $DF_{AB} = \dfrac{K_{AB}}{\sum K}$ | $K = \dfrac{I}{L}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipe (`|`) characters** appear in this table; absolute values use spacing or implicit context to avoid markdown parsing issues.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Utility of These Methods

- **Portal Method** → Used by design offices for **preliminary sizing** of beams and columns of industrial frames subjected to wind. The values are within 10–15% of rigorous results.
- **Cantilever Method** → Used for **tall buildings** (≥ 10 storeys) where the deformation is dominated by cantilever bending of the entire tower.
- **Factor Method** → Used in **routine design** of continuous beams and slabs (especially when spans are equal), forming the basis of moment coefficients in design handbooks.
- **Pattern Loading** → Foundation of **economic design**; without it, every section would be designed for moment magnitudes 30–40% higher than necessary.
- **Wind/Seismic Analysis** → Mandatory by **IS 875 Part 3** and **IS 1893** for all framed structures in India.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Indeterminacy of a Single-Bay Multi-Storey Fixed-Base Portal is Always 3

We will start from the **fundamental plane-frame indeterminacy formula** and apply it algebraically.

### Step 1 — Statement of the General Formula

For a 2D plane frame:
$$ D_s = 3m + r - 3n $$

Where:
- $m$ = number of members (line elements)
- $n$ = number of joints (nodes)
- $r$ = number of reaction components from external supports
- The factor **3** arises from the three equilibrium equations available in 2D ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$)

### Step 2 — Count for Single-Bay, $k$-Storey Fixed-Base Portal

- Number of columns: $2k$
- Number of beams: $k$
- Total members: $m = 3k$
- Number of joints: each storey has 2 beam-column joints, plus 2 base joints = $2k + 2$
- Total joints: $n = 2k + 2$
- Reactions: each fixed base gives 3 reactions; 2 bases → $r = 6$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$ D_s = 3(3k) + 6 - 3(2k + 2) $$

$$ D_s = 9k + 6 - 6k - 6 $$

$$ D_s = 3k $$

### Step 4 — Re-examination — Aha, the Formula Gives 3k!

Let me recount. For **single-bay, single-storey** ($k = 1$):
- $m = 3$ (2 columns + 1 beam) ✓
- $n = 4$ (4 joints: 2 corners + 2 bases)
- $r = 6$

$$ D_s = 3(3) + 6 - 3(4) = 9 + 6 - 12 = 3 \;\checkmark $$

For **single-bay, two-storey** ($k = 2$):
- $m = 5$ (4 columns + 2 beams − wait, two storeys means 2 column pairs above each other = 4 columns, 2 beams = 6 members)
- $n = 6$ (4 intermediate joints + 2 bases)

$$ D_s = 3(6) + 6 - 3(6) = 18 + 6 - 18 = 6 $$

> 🎯 **Corrected Result:** For a single-bay, $k$-storey portal with fixed bases, the indeterminacy is $D_s = 3k$, *not* 3!

| Storeys ($k$) | $D_s$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 3 |
| 2 | 6 |
| 3 | 9 |
| 4 | 12 |

This is the standard result used in KTU textbooks (Pillai & Menon, Varghese, etc.).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Portal Method Shear Distribution

We start from the assumptions:
1. Inflection point at mid-height of every column → each column behaves like a simply-supported member in shear.
2. The total storey shear $V$ must be distributed among columns.
3. The **stress distribution assumption**: the columns of a bent carry horizontal shear in proportion to their *panel position*.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let $N$ = number of bays, $n = N + 1$ = number of columns.
Let $V_e$ = shear in one exterior column, $V_i$ = shear in one interior column.

### Step 2 — Equilibrium

Total horizontal equilibrium at the storey level:
$$ V = 2 V_e + (N - 1) V_i $$

### Step 3 — Apply Assumption 3 (Shear Distribution Rule)

By the portal method convention:
$$ V_i = 2 V_e $$

### Step 4 — Solve

Substituting $V_i = 2 V_e$ into equilibrium:
$$ V = 2 V_e + (N - 1) \cdot 2 V_e = 2 V_e \cdot [1 + (N-1)] = 2 V_e \cdot N $$

Therefore:
$$ V_e = \dfrac{V}{2N} $$

And:
$$ V_i = \dfrac{V}{N} $$

### Step 5 — Special Case Verification

For a single-bay frame ($N = 1$):
- $V_e = V / 2$
- $V_i = V$ (but there are no interior columns!)
- Actually, for $N = 1$, both columns are technically "exterior" but the only interior reference is the joint between them. The Portal Method simplifies to: each column carries $V/2$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Beam End Moments in a Portal Frame under UDL (Vertical Loads)

Consider a single-bay, single-storey portal frame with fixed bases, subjected to UDL $w$ on the beam. Use the **Moment Distribution Method**.

### Step 1 — Identify Members and Their Stiffness

Beam (length $L$): $K_B = \dfrac{I_B}{L}$
Columns (length $H$): $K_C = \dfrac{I_C}{H}$

### Step 2 — Compute Fixed End Moments

$$ FEM_{BA} = -\dfrac{wL^2}{12}, \quad FEM_{AB} = +\dfrac{wL^2}{12} $$

### Step 3 — Distribution Factors at Joint B

At joint B, two members meet: beam BA and column BC.
$$ DF_{BA} = \dfrac{K_{BA}}{K_{BA} + K_{BC}}, \quad DF_{BC} = \dfrac{K_{BC}}{K_{BA} + K_{BC}} $$

By symmetry of single-bay single-storey frame:
- Both joints A and D (top corners) are identical
- Both column bases B-C and A-D are identical

### Step 4 — Distribution and Carry-Over

| Operation | End A | End B |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initial FEM | $+wL^2/12$ | $-wL^2/12$ |
| Release joint | Multiply by $DF_{BA}$ ... | Multiply by $DF_{BA}$ ... |
| Carry-over (× 1/2) | to far end | to far end |

### Step 5 — Final Moment Equations

For symmetric frame with $I_C/I_B = \alpha$ and $H/L = \beta$:

Let $K_C = I_C/H$ and $K_B = I_B/L$, with $K_C / K_B = \alpha / \beta$.

The final moment at the corner (after distribution) is:

$$ M_{corner} = -\dfrac{wL^2}{12} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + 2(K_C / K_B)} \cdot ... $$

For the special case where **columns are very stiff** ($K_C \to \infty$):
$$ M_{corner} \to -\dfrac{wL^2}{12} $$ (i.e., fixed-end condition)

For the special case where **columns are very flexible** ($K_C \to 0$):
$$ M_{corner} \to 0 $$ (i.e., pinned condition — beam behaves as simply supported)

The general expression (after full moment distribution with carry-over) is:

$$ M_A = \dfrac{wL^2}{12} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1 - (DF_B)(1/2)}{1 - (1/2)(DF_B)(DF_C - DF_B)} \right] \quad \text{[Complex form]} $$

For clarity, when **column-beam stiffness ratio** $k = K_C / K_B$:

$$ M_{corner} = \dfrac{wL^2}{12} \cdot \dfrac{2k}{2k + 3} $$

And the mid-span positive moment:
$$ M_{mid} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} - M_{corner} $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Pattern Loading Effect on a Three-Span Continuous Beam

### Step 1 — Influence Line for Reaction at Middle Support (B)

For a 3-span continuous beam ABCD with equal spans $L$:
- IL for $R_B$: 
  - Span AB: positive ordinate, peak at A-end ≈ 0.4
  - Span BC: positive ordinate, peak ≈ 1.1 at centre of BC
  - Span CD: positive ordinate ≈ 0.4 at D

To maximise $R_B$, **load all three spans** with live load.

### Step 2 — Influence Line for Moment at Centre of Span BC

- Span AB: contributes *negative* ordinate at this section
- Span BC: contributes *positive* ordinate, max at centre ≈ 0.15
- Span CD: contributes *negative* ordinate

To **maximise positive** moment at centre of BC: load span BC only (since AB and CD give negative contribution).

To **maximise negative** moment at centre of BC: load spans AB and CD (skip BC).

### Step 3 — Numerical Magnitudes

For uniform $w_L$ live load over span BC only, the positive moment at mid-BC in a 3-span continuous beam with equal spans is:

$$ M_{mid,BC}^{+} = 0.075 \cdot w_L L^2 $$

For $w_L$ over AB and CD only (skipping BC):
$$ M_{mid,BC}^{-} = -0.025 \cdot w_L L^2 $$

> [!NOTE]
> The coefficients 0.075 and 0.025 are tabulated in standard texts (Pillai & Menon Table 8.3; Varghese Table on continuous beams). These are extracted from influence-line ordinates and can be reproduced using three-moment equations or stiffness analysis.

---

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Procedure: Portal Frame under Lateral Load using Portal Method

**Problem:** A single-bay, single-storey reinforced concrete portal frame has span $L = 6 \; m$, column height $H = 4 \; m$. A horizontal force of $V = 40 \; kN$ acts at the roof level. Compute (a) column shears, (b) column end moments, (c) beam end moments.

### Step 1 — Identify Configuration

- Number of bays, $N = 1$
- Total columns = 2 (both are "exterior" per portal rule, but since $N = 1$, both carry equal shear)

### Step 2 — Column Shears

By portal method, for $N = 1$:
$$ V_{col} = \dfrac{V}{2N} = \dfrac{40}{2(1)} = 20 \; kN $$

Each column carries **20 kN** lateral shear.

### Step 3 — Inflection Point Locations

- Each column: inflection at $H/2 = 4/2 = 2 \; m$ from base
- Each column: inflection at $H/2 = 2 \; m$ from top
- Beam: inflection at $L/2 = 6/2 = 3 \; m$ from either end

### Step 4 — Column End Moments

$$ M_{top} = M_{bottom} = V_{col} \cdot \dfrac{H}{2} = 20 \cdot 2 = 40 \; kN \cdot m $$

The signs are opposite at top and bottom because of double curvature.

### Step 5 — Beam End Moments

Each column top applies a horizontal shear $H = 20 \; kN$ to the beam end.
$$ M_{beam,end} = H \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = 20 \cdot 3 = 60 \; kN \cdot m $$

### Step 6 — Verification by Overall Equilibrium

Sum of moments about the centreline:
- Beam moment at left end (60 kN·m) + Beam moment at right end (60 kN·m) = 120 kN·m
- These must be balanced by the column moments producing a couple to resist $V$.
- Couple from columns = $V \cdot H = 40 \cdot 4 = 160 \; kN \cdot m$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy Warning:** The portal method does **not** satisfy overall equilibrium exactly because of its simplifying assumptions. The discrepancy (160 vs 120) must be corrected by adjusting either the column or beam moments. Standard procedure: scale up **beam moments** by the ratio $V \cdot H / M_{beam,coupled}$ to balance the equation.

For our case:
$$ \text{Scale factor} = \dfrac{160}{120} = 1.333 $$

Corrected beam end moments: $60 \times 1.333 = 80 \; kN \cdot m$

This **equilibrium correction** is mandatory in portal method applications and is a frequently-tested step in KTU university exams.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Lateral Load on a Two-Bay, Two-Storey Portal

Let us now compute using the Portal Method:

**Configuration:** 2 bays × 2 storeys
- Column positions (x-coordinates): $x_1 = 0, x_2 = L, x_3 = 2L$
- Lateral load at roof: $V_{roof} = 60 \; kN$
- Lateral load at first floor: $V_1 = 40 \; kN$
- Total base shear: $V = 100 \; kN$

### Top Storey (Roof) Shear Distribution

- Number of bays, $N = 2$
- $V_e = V_{roof} / (2N) = 60 / 4 = 15 \; kN$ (each exterior column)
- $V_i = V_{roof} / N = 60 / 2 = 30 \; kN$ (interior column)

| Column | Position | Shear (kN) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Left exterior | $x = 0$ | 15 |
| Interior | $x = L$ | 30 |
| Right exterior | $x = 2L$ | 15 |
| **Total** | — | **60** ✓ |

### Bottom Storey (Above Foundation) Shear Distribution

- Total shear = $V_1 + V_{roof} = 40 + 60 = 100 \; kN$
- $V_e = 100 / 4 = 25 \; kN$
- $V_i = 100 / 2 = 50 \; kN$

| Column | Shear (kN) |
| :---: | :---: |
| Left exterior | 25 |
| Interior | 50 |
| Right exterior | 25 |
| **Total** | **100** ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The portal method distribution applies **independently** at each storey level. The shears in the columns of the lower storey include the cumulative shear from all storeys above.

---

## 3.7 Pattern Loading — Detailed Example on a Three-Span Beam

### Setup

Three-span continuous beam ABCD with equal spans $L = 4 \; m$.
- Dead load: $w_D = 15 \; kN/m$ (uniform, on all spans)
- Live load: $w_L = 25 \; kN/m$

### Case A — Maximum Positive Moment at Centre of Span BC

**Loading:** Load spans AB and CD with live load; **skip span BC**.

**Analysis:** Use three-moment theorem or tabulated coefficients.

The positive moment at mid-BC for this loading pattern:
$$ M_{mid,BC}^{+} = +0.101 \, w_L L^2 + \text{(positive contribution from dead load)} $$
$$ M_{mid,BC}^{+} = +0.101 \cdot 25 \cdot 16 + \text{(DL contribution)} $$
$$ = +40.4 \; kN \cdot m \; \text{from LL alone} $$

### Case B — Maximum Negative Moment at Centre of Span BC

**Loading:** Load span BC only with live load.

$$ M_{mid,BC}^{-} = -0.025 \, w_L L^2 + \text{(DL contribution)} $$
$$ = -0.025 \cdot 25 \cdot 16 = -10.0 \; kN \cdot m \; \text{from LL alone} $$

The dead load contribution is always positive at mid-span (sagging).

| Case | Live Load Pattern | LL Moment at Mid-BC (kN·m) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Maximum + | Load AB, CD (skip BC) | +40.4 |
| Maximum − | Load BC only | −10.0 |

> 🎯 **Conclusion:** The "load alternate spans" rule gives **maximum positive sagging**, while "load only the span in question" gives **maximum negative** (hogging) at midspan in this case — *opposite* to the rule for supports, because the IL for midspan moment peaks within the span itself.

---

## 3.8 Wind Load on Portal Frame — Step-by-Step

### Given Data

- Location: Kochi (Kerala), Zone III as per IS 875
- Basic wind speed $V_b = 39 \; m/s$
- Building category: Class B (urban/suburban)
- Building height $H = 8 \; m$ (single storey)
- Frame spacing = 4 m
- Roof slope: flat

### Step 1 — Design Wind Speed at Height $z = 8 \; m$

Category 2, Class B, Height 8 m → $k_2 \approx 0.98$
$k_1 = 1.0, k_3 = 1.0, k_4 = 1.0$

$$ V_z = 39 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.98 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 = 38.22 \; m/s $$

### Step 2 — Design Wind Pressure

$$ p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2 = 0.6 \cdot (38.22)^2 = 0.6 \cdot 1460.77 = 876.46 \; N/m^2 = 0.876 \; kN/m^2 $$

### Step 3 — Wind Force per Unit Length on the Windward Face

Effective height exposed (taking half roof height for roof face):
$$ F_{ww} = C_{pe} \cdot p_z \cdot \text{spacing} \cdot \text{effective height} $$

For $C_{pe} = +0.8$ (external pressure coefficient, windward):
$$ F_{ww} = 0.8 \cdot 0.876 \cdot 4 \cdot 8 = 22.4 \; kN \; \text{(total on windward face)} $$

For $C_{pe} = -0.5$ (leeward suction):
$$ F_{lw} = 0.5 \cdot 0.876 \cdot 4 \cdot 8 = 14.0 \; kN \; \text{(total suction on leeward face)} $$

### Step 4 — Net Horizontal Force on Frame

$$ V = F_{ww} + F_{lw} = 22.4 + 14.0 = 36.4 \; kN $$

This force is assumed to act at mid-height of the frame for portal method analysis.

> [!TIP]
> For **low-rise frames**, IS 875 permits distributing the lateral force as a **uniform load** along the height — a simplification that is often acceptable and is the basis of the portal method.

---

## 3.9 Verification: Carry-Over Factor Derivation

### Statement

For a prismatic beam of length $L$ with end A initially rotated by $\theta_A$ while end B is fixed, the moment at A is $M_A = 4EI\theta_A/L$, and the **carry-over moment** at B is $M_B = 2EI\theta_A/L$.

### Derivation Using Slope-Deflection Equation

$$ M_{AB} = \dfrac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_A + \theta_B - 3\psi) $$
$$ M_{BA} = \dfrac{2EI}{L}(2\theta_B + \theta_A - 3\psi) $$

With $\theta_B = 0$ (fixed) and $\psi = 0$ (no settlement):
$$ M_{AB} = \dfrac{4EI\theta_A}{L} $$
$$ M_{BA} = \dfrac{2EI\theta_A}{L} $$

### Carry-Over Factor

$$ COF = \dfrac{M_{BA}}{M_{AB}} = \dfrac{2EI\theta_A / L}{4EI\theta_A / L} = \dfrac{1}{2} $$

> ✅ **Confirmed:** $COF = 1/2$ for all prismatic members, a fundamental constant in moment distribution.

---

## 3.10 Summary of the Analytical Matrix

| Method | Loading Type | Computation Time | Accuracy | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slope-Deflection | Any | Medium | Exact | ≤ 5 storeys |
| Moment Distribution | Any | Medium | Exact (with sway correction) | ≤ 10 storeys |
| Portal Method | Lateral | Low | ± 15% | Preliminary design |
| Cantilever Method | Vertical/Lateral | Low | ± 10–20% | Tall frames |
| Factor Method | Vertical | Very low | ± 10% | Continuous beams |
| Stiffness Matrix (Software) | Any | High | Exact | Final design |

## 4.3 Moment Distribution Procedure — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Step 1: Compute Member Stiffness K = I/L] --> B[Step 2: Compute Distribution Factors at Each Joint]
    B --> C[Step 3: Calculate Fixed End Moments FEM]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is a portal frame? List any two advantages of portal frames over trusses for industrial buildings."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **portal frame** is a two-dimensional, statically indeterminate rigid frame consisting of two columns rigidly connected to one or more horizontal beams, forming a closed rectangular shape. The joints between columns and beams are designed to transmit axial force, shear, and moment, ensuring full rotational continuity.

**Advantages of portal frames over trusses:**
1. **Simpler construction**: Portal frames have fewer members (only columns and beams) compared to trusses, which require numerous diagonal web members, gusset plates, and connections. This reduces fabrication and erection time.
2. **Clear headroom**: Portal frames provide unobstructed internal space since there are no internal diagonal members, making them ideal for warehouses, industrial sheds, and showrooms.
3. **Aesthetic appeal**: Portal frames can be designed with curved or tapered profiles, giving a sleeker architectural appearance.
4. **Easier maintenance**: With fewer connection details, portal frames are easier to inspect and maintain over their service life.

*(Any two clearly stated advantages — 1 mark each; definition — 1 mark)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Explain the concept of pattern loading in continuous beams. Why is it not necessary to load all spans simultaneously?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pattern loading** is a live-load arrangement technique in which only a selected pattern of spans of a continuous beam (or frame) is loaded with live load, while the rest remain unloaded. The dead load is placed on all spans.

**Reason for selective loading:**

The bending moment (or shear) at any section of a continuous structure depends on which spans are loaded and which are not, as dictated by the **influence line diagram** for that effect. The influence line ordinates alternate in sign along the length of the structure:

- Where the IL ordinate is **positive**, loading that span *adds* to the effect.
- Where the IL ordinate is **negative**, loading that span *subtracts* from the effect.

Therefore, to obtain the **maximum possible** design effect (moment or shear) at a particular section, we must load only those spans where the IL ordinate has the *same sign* as the desired effect. Loading all spans simultaneously often produces intermediate (and sometimes smaller) effects than the maximum achievable by selective loading.

**Practical rules:**
- Maximum positive moment in a span → load the span and alternate spans.
- Maximum negative moment at a support → load the two adjacent spans and alternate.
- Maximum reaction → load all adjacent spans.

*(Concept — 1 mark; reason via IL — 1 mark; practical rule — 1 mark)*
