# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "active earth pressure" and state Rankine's expression for the active earth pressure coefficient for a level cohesionless backfill.**

**Model Answer:**
Active earth pressure is the minimum lateral pressure exerted by the soil on a retaining structure when the wall yields sufficiently (typically by rotating or translating away from the backfill) to allow the soil mass to reach a state of incipient failure. The soil mass then mobilises its full shear strength along a planar failure surface inclined at $(45° + φ/2)$ to the horizontal.

For a **level, cohesionless backfill** under Rankine's theory:

$$
K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

Hence lateral pressure at depth $z$ is $p_a = K_a \gamma z$, and the total active thrust is $P_a = \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2$ acting at $H/3$ from the base.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**List any three stability checks to be performed for the design of a cantilever retaining wall. State the minimum factor of safety for each.**

**Model Answer:**
The three stability checks are:

| # | Check | Minimum Factor of Safety (working state) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overturning about the toe | **1.4** |
| 2 | Sliding along the base | **1.4** |
| 3 | Bearing pressure under the base | eccentricity within middle third ($e \leq B/6$); $q_{\max} \leq q_a$ |

If any check fails, the **proportions of the wall** (B, $b_1$, $b_2$, $t_3$) are revised and the analysis repeated.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Module 2, Model]

**An RCC cantilever retaining wall has the following data:**
- Height above base slab: $H = 5.0$ m
- Stem: top thickness $t_1 = 0.25$ m, bottom thickness $t_2 = 0.40$ m
- Base slab: total width $B = 3.5$ m, thickness $t_3 = 0.45$ m
- Toe projection: $b_1 = 1.0$ m, Heel projection: $b_2 = 2.1$ m (since $B - b_1 - t_2 = 3.5 - 1.0 - 0.4 = 2.1$ m)
- Backfill: level, dry, granular, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, $\phi = 30°$
- Surcharge: $q = 15$ kN/m² on top
- Base friction coefficient: $\mu = 0.50$
- Allowable bearing pressure: $q_a = 150$ kN/m²
- Concrete: M25, Steel: Fe415
- $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³

**Part (a) [7 marks]** Check the stability of the wall against (i) overturning, (ii) sliding, and (iii) bearing pressure.

**Part (b) [7 marks]** Design the stem for flexure and shear using limit state method (IS 456:2000). Provide the reinforcement details.

---

#### Part (a) — Stability Check [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Earth Pressure**

$$
K_a = \frac{1 - \sin 30°}{1 + \sin 30°} = \frac{1 - 0.5}{1 + 0.5} = 0.333
$$

**Active thrust due to soil (triangular):**

$$
P_{a1} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.333 \times 17 \times 5^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.333 \times 17 \times 25 = 70.78 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Point of application: $H/3 = 5/3 = 1.667$ m from base.

**Active thrust due to surcharge (rectangular):**

$$
P_{a2} = K_a \cdot q \cdot H = 0.333 \times 15 \times 5 = 25.0 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Point of application: $H/2 = 2.5$ m from base.

**Total active thrust:**

$$
P_a = 70.78 + 25.0 = 95.78 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Vertical Loads and Lever Arms (measured from toe tip)**

| Load | Calculation | Magnitude (kN/m) | Lever arm $x$ (m) | Moment about toe (kN·m/m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ (stem) | $0.5(0.25 + 0.40) \times (5 - 0.45) \times 25 = 0.325 \times 4.55 \times 25$ | 36.97 | $1.0 + (2.1 - 0.325) = 2.775$ | $36.97 \times 2.775 = 102.6$ |
| $W_2$ (base) | $3.5 \times 0.45 \times 25$ | 39.38 | $3.5/2 = 1.75$ | $39.38 \times 1.75 = 68.9$ |
| $W_3$ (soil on heel) | $2.1 \times (5 - 0.45) \times 17 = 2.1 \times 4.55 \times 17$ | 162.4 | $1.0 + 2.1/2 = 2.05$ | $162.4 \times 2.05 = 333.0$ |
| $W_4$ (surcharge on heel) | $15 \times 2.1$ | 31.5 | $1.0 + 2.1/2 = 2.05$ | $31.5 \times 2.05 = 64.6$ |

**Total vertical load:** $\sum V = 36.97 + 39.38 + 162.4 + 31.5 = 270.25$ kN/m
**Total resisting moment:** $\sum M_R = 102.6 + 68.9 + 333.0 + 64.6 = 569.1$ kN·m/m
**Overturning moment:** $\sum M_O = 70.78 \times 1.667 + 25.0 \times 2.5 = 118.0 + 62.5 = 180.5$ kN·m/m

**Step 3 — Stability Checks**

**(i) Overturning:**

$$
\text{FoS}_{OT} = \frac{569.1}{180.5} = 3.15 > 1.4 \quad \checkmark
$$

**(ii) Sliding:**

$$
\text{FoS}_{SL} = \frac{0.50 \times 270.25}{95.78} = \frac{135.13}{95.78} = 1.41 > 1.4 \quad \checkmark \text{ (just satisfied)}
$$

**(iii) Bearing:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum M_R - \sum M_O}{\sum V} = \frac{569.1 - 180.5}{270.25} = \frac{388.6}{270.25} = 1.438 \text{ m from toe}
$$

$$
e = \frac{B}{2} - \bar{x} = 1.75 - 1.438 = 0.312 \text{ m}, \quad \frac{B}{6} = 0.583 \text{ m}
$$

Since $e = 0.312 < 0.583$, resultant lies in the **middle third** ✓

$$
q_{\max} = \frac{\sum V}{B}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{B}\right) = \frac{270.25}{3.5}\left(1 + \frac{6 \times 0.312}{3.5}\right) = 77.21 \times 1.535 = 118.5 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
q_{\min} = \frac{\sum V}{B}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{B}\right) = 77.21 \times 0.465 = 35.9 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

Since $q_{\max} = 118.5 < q_a = 150$ kN/m² ✓ **Bearing is safe.**

> [!NOTE]
> All three stability checks are satisfied. The wall proportions are acceptable.

---

#### Part (b) — Design of Stem [7 Marks]

**Design moment and shear at the base of stem** (limit state, multiply by 1.5):

Factored active pressure at base = $1.5 \times K_a (\gamma H + q) = 1.5 \times 0.333 \times (17 \times 5 + 15) = 1.5 \times 0.333 \times 100 = 50.0$ kN/m²

The stem is designed as a vertical cantilever of height $H' = H - t_3 = 4.55$ m, with width $b = 1000$ mm, effective depth $d = t_2 - 50 - 8 = 340$ mm (assuming 8 mm bar and 50 mm cover).

**Factored design moment at base of stem:**

The active pressure varies from a value at the top of the stem (i.e., at $z = 0$, $p_a = 0.333 \times 15 = 5$ kN/m² due to surcharge) to a maximum at the bottom of the stem (after subtracting base thickness).

At bottom of stem (z = 4.55 m below top of stem):
- Surcharge pressure: $0.333 \times 15 = 5$ kN/m²
- Soil pressure: $0.333 \times 17 \times 4.55 = 25.79$ kN/m²
- Total: $30.79$ kN/m²

Factored: $1.5 \times 30.79 = 46.18$ kN/m²

Approximating conservatively with triangular distribution over $H' = 4.55$ m:

$$
M_u = 1.5 \times \left[ K_a q \cdot \frac{H'^2}{2} + \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H'^2 \cdot \frac{H'}{3} \right]
$$

$$
M_u = 1.5 \times \left[ 0.333 \times 15 \times \frac{4.55^2}{2} + 0.5 \times 0.333 \times 17 \times 4.55^2 \times \frac{4.55}{3} \right]
$$

$$
M_u = 1.5 \times [51.7 + 84.0] = 1.5 \times 135.7 = 203.5 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Limiting moment of resistance (Fe415, M25):**

$$
M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 = 0.138 \times 25 \times 1000 \times 340^2 \times 10^{-6} = 398.8 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

Since $M_u = 203.5 < M_{u,\text{lim}} = 398.8$, **singly reinforced section** is adequate.

**Steel area:**

Assuming lever arm $z \approx 0.9d = 306$ mm (first trial):

$$
A_{st} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 f_y z} = \frac{203.5 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times 306} = 1757 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

Provide **16 mm diameter bars** at **100 mm centres** → $A_{st,\text{provided}} = \dfrac{1000}{100} \times 201 = 2010$ mm²/m ✓

Distribution steel (front face): **0.12% of gross area** = $0.0012 \times 400 \times 1000 = 480$ mm²/m
Provide **12 mm diameter bars at 200 mm centres** = 565 mm²/m ✓

**Shear check:**

Factored shear at base = $1.5 \times P_a = 1.5 \times 95.78 = 143.7$ kN/m (approximately, but this is total; actual at base of stem is from $H' = 4.55$ m):

$$
V_u = 1.5 \times [K_a q H' + \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H'^2] = 1.5 \times [22.78 + 58.69] = 122.2 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{bd} = \frac{122.2 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 340} = 0.36 \text{ MPa}
$$

For M25 concrete: $\tau_{c,\text{max}} = 1.8$ MPa (for Fe415), and $\tau_c$ (for $p_t = 2010/(1000 \times 340) \times 100 = 0.59\%$) $\approx 0.55$ MPa.

Since $\tau_v = 0.36 < \tau_c = 0.55$ MPa ✓ — **Shear reinforcement is not required by design**, but provide minimum shear reinforcement of 8 mm bars at 300 mm c/c as per IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.6.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical for Marks):**
> 1. Many students **forget the load factor of 1.5** while computing $M_u$ and $V_u$ — this alone can cost **2 marks** out of 7.
> 2. Students often **place main bars on the wrong face**. For an RCC cantilever stem, main steel must be on the **earth face (tension face)**, i.e., on the back side — NOT the front. (Earth pressure pushes the stem outward, causing tension on the back face.)
> 3. The **lever arm $z$** must be checked iteratively against the assumed value of $A_{st} f_y/(b d f_{ck})$ — students often skip this iteration.
> 4. **Distribution steel** must be at least 0.12% of gross cross-sectional area on the front face — many students omit it entirely.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice — Alternative Question]

**An RCC cantilever retaining wall is to be designed for the following data:**
- Total height: $H = 6.0$ m
- Sloped backfill at angle $\alpha = 15°$ with horizontal
- Soil: $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $\phi = 32°$, no surcharge
- Saturated zone: $H_1 = 3.0$ m (top), submerged: $H_2 = 3.0$ m (bottom); $\gamma_{\text{sat}} = 20$ kN/m³
- Base width: $B = 4.0$ m, toe $b_1 = 1.2$ m, heel $b_2 = 2.4$ m, $t_3 = 0.5$ m
- Stem: $t_1 = 0.30$ m (top), $t_2 = 0.50$ m (bottom)
- $\mu = 0.50$, $q_a = 180$ kN/m², M25, Fe415

**Part (a) [7 marks]** Calculate the active earth pressure on the back of the wall considering the sloped backfill and the submerged condition.

**Part (b) [7 marks]** Check the stability of the wall against overturning and sliding. If safe, compute the maximum and minimum base pressures.

---

#### Part (a) — Active Earth Pressure [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — $K_{a\alpha}$ for sloped backfill**

$$
K_{a\alpha} = \cos 15° \cdot \frac{\cos 15° - \sqrt{\cos^2 15° - \cos^2 32°}}{\cos 15° + \sqrt{\cos^2 15° - \cos^2 32°}}
$$

Compute numerically: $\cos 15° = 0.9659$, $\cos 32° = 0.8480$.

$\cos^2 15° - \cos^2 32° = 0.9330 - 0.7191 = 0.2139$
$\sqrt{0.2139} = 0.4625$

$$
K_{a\alpha} = 0.9659 \cdot \frac{0.9659 - 0.4625}{0.9659 + 0.4625} = 0.9659 \cdot \frac{0.5034}{1.4284} = 0.9659 \times 0.3524 = 0.3404
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure in top (dry) zone (depth 0 to 3 m)**

At $z = 0$: $p_a = 0$
At $z = 3$ m: $p_a = 0.3404 \times 18 \times 3 = 18.38$ kN/m²

**Step 3 — Pressure in submerged zone (depth 3 to 6 m)**

Use $\gamma' = 20 - 9.81 = 10.19$ kN/m³ (effective submerged unit weight).

At $z = 3$ m: $p_a = 0.3404 \times 18 \times 3 = 18.38$ kN/m² (from soil)
At $z = 6$ m: $p_a = 0.3404 \times (18 \times 3 + 10.19 \times 3) = 0.3404 \times (54 + 30.57) = 28.79$ kN/m² (from soil)
Plus hydrostatic: at $z = 6$ m, $p_w = 9.81 \times 3 = 29.43$ kN/m²

**Step 4 — Total Active Thrust**

Soil part: $\tfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3404 \times 18 \times 3^2 + \left[ 0.3404 \times 18 \times 3 \times 3 + \tfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3404 \times 10.19 \times 3^2 \right]$

$$
P_{a,\text{soil}} = 27.56 + 55.15 + 15.58 = 98.29 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Water part (rectangle, $\tfrac{1}{2}\gamma_w H_2^2$ — triangular, but simpler: average pressure × depth):

$$
P_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 9.81 \times 3^2 = 44.15 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Total active thrust (parallel to backfill):

$$
P_{a,\text{total}} = 98.29 + 44.15 = 142.44 \text{ kN/m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a sloped backfill, the resultant $P_a$ acts **parallel to the backfill surface**. Its components are:
> - Horizontal: $P_{aH} = P_a \cos\alpha = 142.44 \times 0.9659 = 137.6$ kN/m
> - Vertical (downward): $P_{aV} = P_a \sin\alpha = 142.44 \times 0.2588 = 36.86$ kN/m
>
> The vertical component **adds to the vertical loads** (since it acts downward on the back of the stem), which actually *helps* against overturning.

---

#### Part (b) — Stability Check [7 Marks]

**Resisting moments (about toe):**

- $W_1$ (stem) = $0.5(0.30 + 0.50) \times 5.5 \times 25 = 0.4 \times 5.5 \times 25 = 55.0$ kN/m, arm = $1.2 + 2.4 - 0.4 = 3.2$ m → Moment = $176.0$ kN·m/m
- $W_2$ (base) = $4.0 \times 0.5 \times 25 = 50.0$ kN/m, arm = $4.0/2 = 2.0$ m → Moment = $100.0$ kN·m/m
- $W_3$ (soil on heel) = $2.4 \times 5.5 \times 18 = 237.6$ kN/m, arm = $1.2 + 2.4/2 = 2.4$ m → Moment = $570.2$ kN·m/m
- $P_{aV}$ acts on the back of the wall (at the heel), contributing to vertical load: 36.86 kN/m, arm $\approx$ 3.6 m → Moment = $132.7$ kN·m/m

$$
\sum V = 55 + 50 + 237.6 + 36.86 = 379.5 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\sum M_R = 176.0 + 100.0 + 570.2 + 132.7 = 978.9 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Overturning moment** (from $P_{aH}$ acting at $H/3 = 2.0$ m from base):

$$
\sum M_O = 137.6 \times 2.0 = 275.2 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Overturning FoS:**

$$
\text{FoS}_{OT} = \frac{978.9}{275.2} = 3.56 > 1.4 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Sliding FoS:**

$$
\text{FoS}_{SL} = \frac{0.50 \times 379.5}{137.6} = \frac{189.75}{137.6} = 1.38 < 1.4 \quad \text{Marginal}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sliding is **marginally unsatisfactory**. To improve, provide a **shear key** below the base slab to mobilise passive earth pressure in front of the wall, OR increase the base width.

**Bearing Pressure:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{978.9 - 275.2}{379.5} = \frac{703.7}{379.5} = 1.854 \text{ m from toe}
$$

$$
e = 2.0 - 1.854 = 0.146 \text{ m}, \quad \frac{B}{6} = 0.667 \text{ m} \Rightarrow e < B/6 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
q_{\max} = \frac{379.5}{4.0}\left(1 + \frac{6 \times 0.146}{4.0}\right) = 94.88 \times 1.219 = 115.7 \text{ kN/m}^2 < 180 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
q_{\min} = 94.88 \times 0.781 = 74.1 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{(no tension)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall for Sloped/Submerged Backfill Question:**
> 1. Students often **forget that the active thrust direction is parallel to the backfill**, NOT horizontal. This leads to wrong moment calculation and unstable wall proportions.
> 2. The **vertical component of $P_a$** on a sloped backfill *aids* stability — many students wrongly add it to overturning instead of resisting moment.
> 3. The **hydrostatic pressure** must be added separately to the soil pressure in the submerged zone — it is *not* automatically included in Rankine's formula.
> 4. For the **sliding check**, use only the **horizontal component** of $P_a$, not the total.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save this for last-day revision!)**

### A. Core Concepts
- [ ] Cantilever retaining wall has **three structural components**: stem, toe, heel — each acts as a cantilever.
- [ ] **Rankine's theory** is used (smooth wall assumption); $K_a = (1 - \sin\phi)/(1 + \sin\phi)$.
- [ ] For sloped backfill, $P_a$ acts **parallel to backfill surface** — resolve into H and V components.
- [ ] **Surcharge** produces a uniform lateral pressure $K_a q$ over the full height.
- [ ] **Submerged backfill** requires use of effective unit weight $\gamma'$ + hydrostatic pressure.

### B. Stability Criteria (Most Repeated KTU Question!)
- [ ] **Overturning FoS** $\geq 1.4$ (about toe).
- [ ] **Sliding FoS** $\geq 1.4$ (along base; use shear key if needed).
- [ ] **Bearing**: eccentricity $e \leq B/6$; $q_{\max} \leq q_a$.
- [ ] Always check **all three**; failing one means redesign.

### C. Design Loads and Moments
- [ ] **Stem** = vertical cantilever; $M_{\text{base}} = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^3 / 3 + K_a q H^2 / 2$ (per metre).
- [ ] **Heel** = horizontal cantilever; main steel at top.
- [ ] **Toe** = horizontal cantilever; main steel at bottom.
- [ ] Apply **load factor 1.5** in limit state design.
- [ ] **Main steel** on stem: on **earth face** (back side) — not the front.

### D. Empirical Proportions
- [ ] $B = 0.5H$ to $0.7H$; $b_1 = 0.25B$ to $0.35B$; $b_2 = 0.65B$ to $0.75B$.
- [ ] Stem bottom $t_2 = 0.08H$ to $0.10H$ (in mm if H in m).
- [ ] Base thickness $t_3 = 0.08H$ to $0.10H$.

### E. Common Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] Forgetting to add **weight of soil on heel** in vertical load.
- [ ] Using total $P_a$ for sliding check on sloped backfill (use only $P_{aH}$).
- [ ] Confusing active and passive pressure directions.
- [ ] Forgetting the **water pressure** in submerged backfill.
- [ ] Not checking for **tension at base** ($q_{\min} \geq 0$).
- [ ] Placing main steel on the **wrong face** of the stem.

### F. Key Code References
- [ ] **IS 456:2000 / IS 456 Part 1:2023** — RCC design (LSM).
- [ ] **IS 875 (Part 5):1987** — Special loads on structures (earth pressure, surcharge).
- [ ] **IS 2974 / IS 14458** — Earth-retaining structures (if part of course).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counterfort Retaining Wall - Design Principles of Components and Detailing (Design Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET631 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Counterfort Retaining Wall - Design Principles of Components and Detailing (Design Not Required) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition A **Counterfort Retaining Wall** is a type of cantilever retaining wall in which the vertical stem and the horizontal base (heel) slab are stiffened at regular intervals by **transverse vertical ribs (counterforts)** that tie the stem monolithically to the heel slab. These counterforts act as **tension members**, transferring the lateral earth pressure from the stem to the base, thereby reducing the effective spans, bending moments, and section thickness of the stem and heel slab. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET631 - Module 2):** > Counterfort retaining walls are economical for heights **greater than 6 m to 7 m**, where a plain cantilever stem would become uneconomically thick. The counterforts convert the stem and heel slab into **spanning elements** (supported at intervals) rather than **cantilever elements**, drastically reducing reinforcement quantities. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **bookshelf** leaning against a wall that is loaded with heavy books pushing sideways: - Without any supports, the bookshelf would bend or break in the middle. - Now imagine you attach **triangular wooden brackets** from the back of each shelf down to the wall — these brackets stop the shelf from rotating outward. - In a counterfort retaining wall, the **stem is the bookshelf face**, the **soil is the heavy books**, and the **counterforts are those triangular brackets** that prevent the wall from tipping or cracking. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** > The counterforts are the "**muscles**" of the wall — they resist the **tension** that tries to pull the stem away from the base slab. Because of this, main reinforcement in counterforts is placed on the **back (soil) face**, similar to the main steel in a cantilever beam at its support. ### Why Counterforts? (Engineering Justification) | Plain Cantilever Wall | Counterfort Wall | |----------------------|------------------| | Stem acts as **vertical cantilever** fixed at base | Stem acts as **continuous slab** spanning between counterforts | | Thickness increases **linearly with depth** (un economical) | Thickness is **nearly uniform** (economical) | | Suitable for H ≤ 6 m | Economical for H > 6 m to 7 m | | Higher **steel quantity** | Lower **steel quantity** | > [!TIP] > For KTU numerical problems (if asked): If the **stem height above base > 6 m**, always opt for counterfort type. Below 6 m, use ordinary cantilever retaining wall. --- ### Nomenclature & Standard Metrics (KTU Conventions) - **H** → Total height of wall above the base slab - **D** → Depth/thickness of base slab (typically H/10 to H/12) - **L** → Total base width (typically 0.5H to 0.7H) - **B** → Toe projection (typically 0.2L to 0.3L) - **L'** → Heel projection (typically 0.5L to 0.6L) - **S** → **Center-to-center spacing of counterforts** (typically 1/3 H to 1/2 H, but ≥ 1/3 of H for economy) - **γ_s** → Unit weight of soil (**18 kN/m³** for standard problems unless stated) - **φ** → Angle of internal friction of soil (**30°** assumed standard) - **μ** → Coefficient of friction between soil and base (**0.5 to 0.6**, typically **0.5**) - **σ_st** → Permissible tensile stress in steel (**230 N/mm²** for Fe415) > [!CAUTION] > Always check the **unit weight of soil** and **angle of internal friction** given in the question. KTU problems often use **γ_s = 18 kN/m³** and **φ = 30°** (giving K_a = 1/3). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Components of a Counterfort Retaining Wall A counterfort retaining wall consists of the following structural components, each requiring individual design consideration: 1. **Stem (Vertical Wall)** — resists lateral earth pressure 2. **Heel Slab** — supports the weight of backfill soil 3. **Toe Slab** — acts as a cantilever projecting in front of the stem 4. **Counterforts (Vertical Stiffeners)** — tie the stem to the heel slab 5. **Base Slab (Combined Heel + Toe)** — supports the entire wall on foundation soil 6. **Shear Key (Optional)** — provided at the base to resist sliding ### 2.2 Structural Behavior & Load Path The lateral earth pressure acting on the back of the stem is transferred as follows: **Step 1:** Soil pressure on stem (trapezoidal distribution, max at base) **Step 2:** Stem acts as a **continuous slab** supported on counterforts. The lateral pressure is distributed to each counterfort as a reaction. **Step 3:** Each counterfort receives a horizontal force equal to (pressure × spacing) and transfers it as a **tension** to the heel slab at the junction. **Step 4:** Heel slab is held down by the weight of backfill soil; this **downward weight balances the upward soil reaction** (net effect is usually **upward**). **Step 5:** Toe slab is pushed upward by soil reaction; it acts as a **cantilever** from the face of the stem. **Step 6:** The entire wall is stabilized against: - **Overturning** → by weight of stem + heel slab + soil above heel - **Sliding** → by friction at base + passive resistance of soil in front of toe - **Bearing failure** → by ensuring soil pressure ≤ safe bearing capacity > [!NOTE] > **KTU Critical Concept:** > In counterfort walls, the **stem and heel slab span between counterforts**, while the **counterforts themselves span vertically as cantilevers from the base slab**. This is the **opposite** of a plain cantilever wall, where everything is a pure cantilever. ### 2.3 Detailed Design Principles for Each Component #### A. Design of the Stem The stem is designed as a **continuous slab** of span equal to the **clear distance between counterforts** (or center-to-center distance, depending on support condition). **Loading on Stem:** The lateral earth pressure at depth z from top of stem is: $$p_z = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot z$$ where: $$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\left(45° - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$ The stem experiences a **trapezoidal load** — zero at the top and maximum at the base. **Design Bending Moments:** For a continuous slab of span S (center-to-center) with trapezoidal loading (w_1 at top, w_2 at base), using the **equivalent uniform load method**: $$w_{eq} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{w_1^2 + w_1 w_2 + w_2^2}{w_1 + w_2}$$ Then: - **Negative B.M. at support (face of counterfort):** M_neg = w_eq · S² / 10 - **Positive B.M. at midspan (between counterforts):** M_pos = w_eq · S² / 12 > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Shortcut:** Most solutions treat the load as a **trapezoidal distribution** and use **IS 456 coefficients** for continuous slabs, or use the simpler approximation: **M = p·S²/10 to S²/12** where p is the average pressure. **Maximum B.M. for Stem Section at Base:** The stem must also be checked for the **overall cantilever moment** transferred from each counterfort: $$M_{stem,base} = \frac{p_{avg} \cdot S \cdot h_{stem}^2}{2}$$ where h_stem is the height of stem above base. #### B. Design of the Heel Slab The heel slab is loaded by: - **Downward:** Weight of backfill soil + self-weight of heel slab - **Upward:** Soil reaction (upward pressure from foundation) The **net pressure** is usually **upward** and is calculated as: $$p_{net,heel} = p_{soil,reaction} - (\gamma_s \cdot d_{heel} + \gamma_c \cdot D_{heel})$$ The heel slab spans **perpendicular to the wall**, between counterforts (span = S). **Design Bending Moments:** Treated as a continuous slab with upward (net) pressure: - **Negative B.M. at face of stem or counterfort:** M_neg = w_net · S² / 10 - **Positive B.M. at midspan:** M_pos = w_net · S² / 12 > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget that the heel slab is loaded by **net upward pressure**, not by the full soil reaction. Always subtract the weight of soil and concrete above the heel slab. #### C. Design of the Toe Slab The toe slab projects beyond the front face of the stem and acts as a **cantilever** of length B (toe projection). **Loading on Toe:** - **Upward:** Soil reaction (full intensity at the toe) - **Downward:** Self-weight of toe slab (negligible in B.M. calculation) **Maximum Bending Moment (at face of stem):** $$M_{toe} = \frac{p_{soil} \cdot B^2}{2}$$ where p_soil is the **upward soil reaction** under the toe. > [!IMPORTANT] > The toe is always in **tension on the bottom face**. Main reinforcement is placed on the **bottom of the toe slab**. #### D. Design of the Counterforts (Most Critical Component) The counterfort is a **vertical cantilever** fixed at the base slab, subjected to: 1. **Horizontal earth pressure** (from the strip of stem it supports) 2. **Vertical loads** (from stem, heel slab, soil above heel) **Total Horizontal Force on One Counterfort:** $$P_h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot h^2 \cdot S$$ where h is the height of the stem and S is the spacing of counterforts. **Maximum Bending Moment at Base of Counterfort:** $$M_{counterfort} = P_h \cdot \frac{h}{3} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot h^3 \cdot S$$ **Tensile Force in Counterfort (Tie Action):** The counterfort acts as a **tension tie** between the stem and the heel slab. The tension in the counterfort at any horizontal level is: $$T = \frac{M_{stem}(z)}{d}$$ where: - M_stem(z) = bending moment in stem at depth z (per unit length × spacing) - d = horizontal distance from back face of stem to the centroid of counterfort **Reinforcement in Counterfort:** - Main bars on the **back (soil side) face** — resist tension - Additional inclined bars resist **horizontal shear** - Stirrups/links to resist vertical shear - Curtailment: extend 50% of bars full height, curtail remaining at 1/3 height #### E. Detailing of Junction of Stem and Heel Slab The junction is a **rigid corner** subjected to: - Negative moment from stem (tension on back face) - Negative moment from heel slab (tension on top face) - These moments tend to **open the joint** at the **inside corner** **Detailing Rule (KTU/IS 456):** Provide a **45° haunch** of size 300 mm × 300 mm (or 0.06 × stem height) to: - Increase lever arm - Reduce stress concentration - Accommodate extra reinforcement > [!IMPORTANT] > Reinforcement must be **anchored and bent** carefully: > - Stem main steel → bend into heel slab (L-bend) > - Heel slab main steel → bend into stem (L-bend) > - Counterfort main steel → extend into stem and heel slab with full development length --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application | |---|--------------------|-----------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Active earth pressure coefficient | $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$ | dimensionless | Lateral pressure | | 2 | Pressure at depth z | $p_z = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot z$ | kN/m² | Pressure on stem | | 3 | Total horizontal force per unit length | $P_h = \tfrac{1}{2} K_a \gamma_s h^2$ | kN/m | Total thrust | | 4 | Stem B.M. at support (continuous) | $M_{neg} = w_{eq} S^2 / 10$ | kN·m | Stem design | | 5 | Stem B.M. at midspan | $M_{pos} = w_{eq} S^2 / 12$ | kN·m | Stem design | | 6 | Counterfort B.M. at base | $M_{cf} = \tfrac{1}{6} K_a \gamma_s h^3 S$ | kN·m | Counterfort design | | 7 | Tension in counterfort | $T = M_{stem}(z) / d$ | kN | Tie force | | 8 | Toe slab B.M. at stem face | $M_{toe} = p_{soil} B^2 / 2$ | kN·m | Toe design | | 9 | Heel slab B.M. (continuous) | $M = w_{net} S^2 / 10$ (or 12) | kN·m | Heel design | | 10 | Minimum base width | $L = 0.5H$ to $0.7H$ | m | Preliminary sizing | | 11 | Minimum stem thickness | $t_{stem} = H / 10$ (top) to $H / 8$ (bottom) | mm | Detailing | | 12 | Base slab thickness | $D = H / 10$ to $H / 12$ | mm | Detailing | | 13 | Spacing of counterforts | $S = H/3$ to $H/2$ | m | Detailing | | 14 | Haunch size (at junction) | $300 \text{ mm} \times 300 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Detailing | > [!NOTE] > **Note on table:** All absolute values use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Highway & Railway Embankments** — where retaining heights exceed 6 m - **Bridge Abutments** — heavy lateral loads from approach fills - **Dock & Harbour Structures** — quay walls with high water-table considerations - **Industrial Yard Walls** — coal bunkers, ore storage yards - **Basement Walls in Hill Construction** — earth retention for multi-level basements > [!TIP] > **KTU Real-World Question (often asked in viva):** "Why is a counterfort wall preferred over a buttressed wall?" > **Answer:** Buttressed walls have counterforts on the **front (open) face**, which obstructs space and is aesthetically unappealing. Counterfort walls have them on the **back (soil) face**, hidden from view. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Bending Moment in Counterfort (Step-by-Step) **Given:** - Height of stem above base: h (m) - Spacing of counterforts (c/c): S (m) - Unit weight of backfill: γ_s (kN/m³) - Angle of internal friction: φ **Step 1: Active Earth Pressure Coefficient** $$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}$$ For φ = 30° (standard KTU assumption): $$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin 30°}{1 + \sin 30°} = \frac{1 - 0.5}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = \frac{1}{3}$$ **Step 2: Pressure Distribution on Stem** Pressure at depth z below top of stem: $$p_z = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot z = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \gamma_s \cdot z$$ Pressure at top (z = 0): p_1 = 0 Pressure at base (z = h): p_2 = (γ_s · h) / 3 **Step 3: Total Horizontal Force per Unit Length of Wall** $$P_{h,unit} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot p_2 \cdot h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\gamma_s h}{3} \cdot h = \frac{\gamma_s h^2}{6}$$ **Step 4: Force on One Counterfort (over spacing S)** $$P_h = P_{h,unit} \cdot S = \frac{\gamma_s h^2 S}{6}$$ **Step 5: Location of Resultant from Base** For a triangular pressure distribution, the resultant acts at **h/3 from the base**. **Step 6: Bending Moment in Counterfort at Base** The counterfort is a vertical cantilever fixed at the base. The bending moment at the base due to the horizontal force P_h acting at height h/3 from the base: $$M_{cf,base} = P_h \cdot \frac{h}{3} = \frac{\gamma_s h^2 S}{6} \cdot \frac{h}{3} = \frac{\gamma_s h^3 S}{18}$$ **Step 7: Simplification for Standard KTU Values** Substituting γ_s = 18 kN/m³: $$M_{cf,base} = \frac{18 \cdot h^3 \cdot S}{18} = h^3 \cdot S \text{ kN·m}$$ > [!NOTE] > This elegant result (M = h³·S kN·m for φ = 30°, γ = 18 kN/m³) is a **favorite KTU quick-check** in numerical problems. ### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Tension in Counterfort (Tie Action) **Conceptual Setup:** Consider a horizontal section at depth z in the stem. The bending moment per unit width in the stem at this level is: $$M_{stem,unit}(z) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot z^2 \cdot \frac{z}{3} = \frac{K_a \gamma_s z^3}{6}$$ This moment (per unit length) is shared between two counterforts at spacing S. So the moment delivered to **one counterfort** at this level is: $$M_{stem}(z) = M_{stem,unit}(z) \cdot S = \frac{K_a \gamma_s z^3 S}{6}$$ The **horizontal distance** from the back face of the stem (where soil pressure acts) to the **centroid of counterfort** is: $$d = \text{stem thickness at top} + \text{distance to counterfort centroid}$$ For typical proportions: $$d \approx 0.5 \, t_{stem} + \text{clear cover} + 0.5 \phi_{cf}$$ **Tensile Force in Counterfort at Depth z:** The moment M_stem(z) is resisted by a **couple** — the tension in the counterfort steel (acting at distance d from the soil face) and the compression in the concrete. By moment equilibrium: $$T(z) = \frac{M_{stem}(z)}{d} = \frac{K_a \gamma_s z^3 S}{6 \cdot d}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The tension T is **maximum at the base of the stem** (z = h), since T ∝ z³. The reinforcement in the counterfort is therefore **maximum at the base** and can be **curtailed** in steps going upward. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Design Considerations Summary | Component | Structural Action | Span | Loading | Critical B.M. Location | Reinforcement Face | |-----------|-------------------|------|---------|------------------------|--------------------| | **Stem** | Continuous slab between counterforts | S (c/c) | Trapezoidal horizontal | At counterfort face (negative) + midspan (positive) | Back face (negative B.M.) + front face (positive B.M.) | | **Heel Slab** | Continuous slab between counterforts | S (c/c) | Net upward | At stem face (negative) + midspan (positive) | Top face (negative B.M.) + bottom face (positive B.M.) | | **Toe Slab** | Cantilever from stem face | B (projection) | Upward soil pressure | At face of stem | Bottom face | | **Counterfort** | Vertical cantilever from base | h (height) | Horizontal earth pressure | At base of counterfort | Back face (tension) | | **Base Slab Junction** | Rigid corner | — | Combined moments | At interior corner | Both faces (haunch provided) | ### 3.4 Derivation: Equivalent Uniform Load for Trapezoidal Pressure on Stem The trapezoidal pressure on the stem (zero at top, w at base) can be replaced by an **equivalent uniform load** for B.M. coefficient application: For a trapezoidal load varying from w_1 (top) to w_2 (base) over span S: $$w_{eq} = \frac{w_1 + 2w_2}{3} \text{ (for triangular/trapezoidal)}$$ For triangular load (w_1 = 0, w_2 = w): $$w_{eq} = \frac{0 + 2w}{3} = \frac{2w}{3}$$ For a continuous slab, **negative B.M. at support:** $$M_{neg} = \frac{w_{eq} S^2}{10} = \frac{2w S^2}{30} = \frac{w S^2}{15}$$ **Positive B.M. at midspan:** $$M_{pos} = \frac{w_{eq} S^2}{12} = \frac{2w S^2}{36} = \frac{w S^2}{18}$$ > [!TIP] > KTU Examiners often use the **rule-of-thumb** approximation: M_max ≈ w·S²/10 for the support moment and w·S²/12 for the midspan moment, which gives a **conservative design**. ### 3.5 Step-by-Step Stability Verification (Conceptual) Although the problem states "Design Not Required," for **KTU conceptual questions**, students should know the **stability checks**: **Step 1: Check against Overturning** Factor of Safety against overturning: $$FS_{OT} = \frac{\sum \text{ Stabilizing moments about toe}}{\sum \text{ Overturning moments about toe}} \geq 1.55 \text{ to } 2.0$$ **Step 2: Check against Sliding** $$FS_{slide} = \frac{\mu \cdot \sum V}{\sum P_h} \geq 1.5 \text{ to } 1.55$$ where μ = coefficient of base friction. **Step 3: Check Eccentricity** $$e = \frac{M_{net}}{V_{total}} \leq \frac{L}{6}$$ This ensures the resultant lies within the **middle third** of the base, preventing tension at the base. **Step 4: Check Base Pressure** $$p_{max,min} = \frac{V}{L \cdot 1} \left(1 \pm \frac{6e}{L}\right) \leq q_{safe}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Component Architecture & Load Path ```mermaid flowchart TB A[BACKFILL SOIL
Active Earth Pressure] B[STEM
Vertical Wall
Span between counterforts] C[COUNTERFORT
Vertical Stiffener
Tension Member] D[HEEL SLAB
Span between counterforts
Net upward load] E[TOE SLAB
Cantilever from stem
Upward soil reaction] F[BASE SLAB
Combined Heel + Toe] G[FOUNDATION SOIL
Bearing + Friction] H[SHEAR KEY
Optional sliding resistance] A -- Lateral Pressure p_z --> B B -- Reaction Forces --> C C -- Tension Tie Action --> D A -- Vertical Load on Top --> D D -- Net Upward Load --> B D -- Downward Weight --> F B -- Self-weight + Soil --> F E -- Upward Reaction --> F F -- Vertical Loads + Moments --> G G -- Friction mu V --> H H -- Resists Sliding --> F ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Design Principles Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[STEP 1
Determine Earth Pressure
K_a gamma_s z] S2[STEP 2
Design STEM as
Continuous Slab] S3[STEP 3
Design HEEL SLAB as
Continuous Slab] S4[STEP 4
Design TOE SLAB as
Cantilever] S5[STEP 5
Design COUNTERFORT as
Vertical Cantilever Tension Tie] S6[STEP 6
Detail Junctions
with Haunches L Bends] S7[STEP 7
Check Stability
OT Sliding Eccentricity] S8[STEP 8
Provide Detailing
Cover Spacing Anchorage] S1 --> S2 S1 --> S3 S1 --> S4 S1 --> S5 S2 --> S6 S3 --> S6 S4 --> S6 S5 --> S6 S2 --> S7 S3 --> S7 S4 --> S7 S5 --> S7 S6 --> S8 S7 --> S8 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force Transfer Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ZONE_A[ZONE A: LATERAL LOADS] L1[Active Earth Pressure] L2[Horizontal Force P_h] end subgraph ZONE_B[ZONE B: STEM BEHAVIOR] M1[Continuous Slab
Span equals S] M2[BM at Counterfort Face
Negative Moment] M3[BM at Midspan
Positive Moment] end subgraph ZONE_C[ZONE C: COUNTERFORT BEHAVIOR] T1[Tension Tie Function] T2[Vertical Cantilever
from Base Slab] T3[Max Tension at Base
T proportional to z cubed] end subgraph ZONE_D[ZONE D: BASE BEHAVIOR] B1[Heel Span S upward net] B2[Toe Cantilever B upward] B3[Stability Verification] end L1 --> L2 L2 --> M1 M1 --> M2 M1 --> M3 M2 --> T1 M1 --> T2 T1 --> T3 M3 --> B1 M2 --> B2 T3 --> B3 ``` ### 4.4 ASCII Schematic: Section Through Counterfort Retaining Wall ``` ┌──────────────────────┐ │ Stem (Vertical) │ ◄── Counterfort │ Thickness 300-400mm │ (back face) │ Back face tension │ ┌────────┤ │ │ │ │ Backfill Soil│ │ ╲ │ ▲ γ_s=18 kN/m³ │ Lateral│ ╲ Counterfort │ │ Counterfort φ=30° │ Earth │ ╲ (Tie) │ │ height h │ Pressure│ ╲ │ │ │ p_z=K_aγ_z │ │ │ │ ╲ │ │ │ │ ╲ │ │ │ │ ╲ │ ▼ └────────┴──────────┴────────────┘ Heel Slab (D thick) ▲ Width L' │ Toe Span between │ Slab counterforts │ Width B (S, c/c) │ ──────────────┴─────────────── Base Slab (Combined, D thick) Total width L ──────────────────────────────── Foundation Soil (Bearing Pressure Check) ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **What is a counterfort retaining wall? Under what conditions is it preferred over a cantilever retaining wall?** **Model Answer:** A counterfort retaining wall is a type of retaining wall in which the vertical stem and horizontal base slab are tied together at regular intervals by **transverse vertical ribs called counterforts**. These counterforts act as **tension members** that reduce the effective spans of the stem and heel slab, thereby reducing the bending moments and section thickness. A counterfort retaining wall is preferred over a plain cantilever retaining wall when: - The **height of the wall exceeds 6 m to 7 m** - Economy in concrete and steel is desired - A plain cantilever stem would become excessively thick - **Counterforts are placed on the back (soil) face**, leaving the front face unobstructed and aesthetically clean. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **List the main components of a counterfort retaining wall. What is the function of the counterfort?** **Model Answer:** **Main components of a counterfort retaining wall:** 1. **Stem** — vertical wall resisting lateral earth pressure 2. **Heel slab** — base slab on the back (soil) side 3. **Toe slab** — base slab on the front side 4. **Counterforts** — vertical stiffening ribs 5. **Base slab** — combined heel + toe 6. **Shear key** (optional) — to resist sliding **Function of the counterfort:** The counterfort acts as a **tension tie** connecting the stem to the heel slab monolithically. It receives the lateral earth pressure transferred from the stem (acting as a continuous slab) and resists it as a **vertical cantilever fixed at the base**. By doing so, it: - Reduces the **effective span** of the stem and heel slab - Reduces **bending moments** in these components - Allows reduction in section thickness - Provides additional **stability** to the wall --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **structural behavior** of various components of a counterfort retaining wall. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **design principles of the counterfort** as a tension member. Show the derivation for the maximum tension in the counterfort. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Structural Behavior of Components (7 marks):** The counterfort retaining wall consists of a stem, heel slab, toe slab, and counterforts. Each component behaves as follows: **(i) Stem:** The stem is designed as a **continuous slab** spanning horizontally between counterforts. The earth pressure on the stem varies from zero at the top to a maximum at the base (trapezoidal distribution). This load is transferred to the counterforts as **support reactions**. The stem develops **negative bending moments at the counterfort faces** and **positive bending moments at midspan**. **(ii) Heel Slab:** The heel slab is loaded by: - **Downward:** weight of backfill soil + self-weight - **Upward:** soil reaction from the foundation The **net pressure** is usually **upward**. The heel slab also spans between counterforts as a continuous slab, with the soil reaction providing the loading. It is supported by the **counterforts** at the sides and the **stem** at the front edge. **(iii) Toe Slab:** The toe slab is a **cantilever** projecting beyond the front face of the stem. It is loaded by the **upward soil reaction** from the foundation. The maximum bending moment occurs at the **face of the stem** and the main reinforcement is placed on the **bottom face**. **(iv) Counterforts:** Each counterfort is a **vertical cantilever** fixed at the base slab. It receives the horizontal earth pressure from a strip of the stem (width = spacing S). The counterfort acts as a **tension member**, with main reinforcement on the **back (soil) face**. **(v) Junction of Stem and Heel Slab:** The corner is rigid and is provided with a **45° haunch** (300 mm × 300 mm) to increase the lever arm and accommodate the bent reinforcement. --- **(b) Design of Counterfort as Tension Member — Derivation (7 marks):** **Given:** - Height of stem = h - Spacing of counterforts (c/c) = S - Unit weight of soil = γ_s - Angle of internal friction = φ **Derivation:** **Step 1:** Active earth pressure at depth z below top of stem: $$p_z = K_a \cdot \gamma_s \cdot z$$ **Step 2:** Bending moment per unit length in stem at depth z: $$M_{stem,unit}(z) = \frac{K_a \gamma_s z^3}{6}$$ **Step 3:** Bending moment delivered to one counterfort at depth z (over spacing S): $$M_{stem}(z) = \frac{K_a \gamma_s z^3 S}{6}$$ **Step 4:** Let d = horizontal distance from the back face of stem (where soil pressure acts) to the centroid of counterfort main reinforcement. By moment equilibrium, the **tensile force in the counterfort** at depth z is: $$T(z) = \frac{M_{stem}(z)}{d} = \frac{K_a \gamma_s z^3 S}{6 d}$$ **Step 5:** Maximum tension occurs at the **base of the stem** (z = h): $$T_{max} = \frac{K_a \gamma_s h^3 S}{6 d}$$ **Step 6:** For standard KTU values (φ = 30°, γ_s = 18 kN/m³, K_a = 1/3): $$T_{max} = \frac{(1/3)(18) h^3 S}{6 d} = \frac{h^3 S}{d} \text{ kN}$$ **Step 7:** Area of steel required in counterfort: $$A_{st} = \frac{T_{max}}{\sigma_{st}} = \frac{T_{max}}{230 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$ **Step 8:** Detailing: Provide main bars on the back face of the counterfort, extend them with full development length into the stem and heel slab, and provide stirrups/links to resist vertical shear. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **design principles and detailing of the stem and heel slab** in a counterfort retaining wall. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write notes on the **detailing rules** for the junction of stem and heel slab in a counterfort retaining wall, including haunch provision. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Design of Stem and Heel Slab (7 marks):** **Stem Design Principles:** The stem is a **vertical cantilever-like element**, but in a counterfort wall, it behaves as a **continuous slab** of span S (c/c spacing of counterforts). The lateral earth pressure on the stem is trapezoidal: zero at the top, maximum at the base. **Loading on Stem:** $$p_z = K_a \gamma_s z$$ **Design Procedure:** 1. Calculate the **equivalent uniform load** for B.M. coefficients: $$w_{eq} = \frac{2w_{max}}{3} = \frac{2 K_a \gamma_s h}{3}$$ 2. **Negative B.M. at counterfort face:** $$M_{neg} = \frac{w_{eq} S^2}{10} = \frac{K_a \gamma_s h S^2}{15}$$ 3. **Positive B.M. at midspan:** $$M_{pos} = \frac{w_{eq} S^2}{12} = \frac{K_a \gamma_s h S^2}{18}$$ 4. Provide main reinforcement on the **back face** (for negative B.M.) and **front face** (for positive B.M.). 5. Maintain **minimum reinforcement** as per IS 456: A_st,min = 0.12% of gross cross-section for Fe415. **Heel Slab Design Principles:** The heel slab is loaded by: - **Downward:** W_s = weight of soil + self-weight of heel slab - **Upward:** Soil reaction **Net upward pressure:** $$p_{net} = p_{soil} - (\gamma_s d_s + \gamma_c D)$$ where d_s = depth of soil above heel, D = thickness of heel slab. **Design Procedure:** 1. The heel slab spans between counterforts (span = S) as a continuous slab. 2. **Negative B.M. at face of stem or counterfort:** $$M_{neg} = \frac{p_{net} S^2}{10}$$ 3. **Positive B.M. at midspan:** $$M_{pos} = \frac{p_{net} S^2}{12}$$ 4. Provide main reinforcement on the **top face** (for negative B.M.) and **bottom face** (for positive B.M.). 5. **Curtailment:** 50% bars can be curtailed at L/3 from support. --- **(b) Detailing of Junction and Haunch Provision (7 marks):** The junction of the stem and heel slab is a **rigid corner** subjected to large **negative moments** from both elements. These moments tend to **open the joint** at the inside corner. **Detailing Rules (as per IS 456 and SP 16):** 1. **Haunch Provision:** - A **45° haunch** of size **300 mm × 300 mm** is provided at the inside corner. - The haunch increases the **lever arm** of the reinforcement, reducing stress. - The haunch accommodates the **bent reinforcement** from stem and heel slab. - For larger walls, haunch size may be increased to **0.06 × stem height**. 2. **Reinforcement Detailing:** - **Main steel of stem** (back face, horizontal): must be **extended and bent** into the heel slab with a **90° L-bend** of length ≥ 12φ or development length. - **Main steel of heel slab** (top face, transverse): must be bent into the stem with a similar L-bend. - **Counterfort main bars**: extended into both stem and heel slab with full anchorage. - **Distribution steel**: provided on both faces, perpendicular to main steel. 3. **Minimum Cover:** - For stem and base slab in contact with soil: **clear cover = 50 mm** (or as per IS 456 Table 16A for severe exposure). - For counterforts: 50 mm cover on soil side. 4. **Spacing Rules:** - Maximum spacing of main bars: **300 mm** (or 3 × effective depth). - Maximum spacing of distribution bars: **5 × effective depth** or 450 mm. 5. **Splicing and Anchorage:** - Laps in main bars should be **staggered** (not in same section). - Lap length for Fe415: **50φ** in tension, **45φ** in compression. - Hooks and bends as per IS 456, Clause 8. 6. **Construction Joint:** - Provided between base slab and stem (not below the base). - Surface roughened for proper bond. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing Counterfort vs Buttressed Wall:** Students often mix these up. **Counterforts are on the BACK (soil) face**, buttresses are on the FRONT face. > > 2. **Treating Stem as Pure Cantilever:** Many students design the stem as a vertical cantilever in counterfort walls. **This is WRONG** — the stem spans BETWEEN counterforts. > > 3. **Forgetting the Equivalent Load Conversion:** Forgetting to convert trapezoidal pressure to equivalent UDL gives **incorrect B.M. values**. > > 4. **Placing Counterfort Steel on the Wrong Face:** The main counterfort bars must be on the **back face (soil side)**, where tension occurs. > > 5. **Neglecting the Haunch:** Failing to mention the haunch at the stem-heel junction **costs 2 marks** in detailing questions. > > 6. **Confusing Toe and Heel Behavior:** Toe is a **cantilever** with reinforcement on **bottom**; heel is a **continuous slab** with reinforcement on **top** (for negative B.M.) and **bottom** (for positive B.M.). > > 7. **Wrong Tension Formula:** Tension in counterfort is **T = M/d**, not T = M × d. **Multiplication vs Division is a fatal error.** > > 8. **Missing L-bend Details:** Detailing questions ALWAYS expect **L-bend** specifications with development length. > > 9. **Stability Checks Omitted:** Even if "Design Not Required," examiners award marks for listing **stability checks** (overturning, sliding, eccentricity, bearing pressure). > > 10. **Incorrect Spacing of Counterforts:** Standard is **H/3 to H/2** — not H/4 or H. State the assumption clearly. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU PECET631 Module 2** - [x] **Counterfort wall** → preferred for H > 6 m to 7 m - [x] **Components** → Stem, Heel, Toe, Counterforts, Base, Shear Key - [x] **Stem behavior** → Continuous slab spanning S (c/c counterfort spacing) - [x] **Heel behavior** → Continuous slab with **net upward** pressure - [x] **Toe behavior** → **Cantilever** of length B (toe projection) - [x] **Counterfort behavior** → **Vertical cantilever** + **tension tie** - [x] **Counterfort reinforcement** → Main steel on **back (soil) face** - [x] **Tension in counterfort** → $T = M_{stem}/d$, maximum at base (∝ z³) - [x] **Counterfort spacing** → S = H/3 to H/2 (center-to-center) - [x] **Stem thickness** → H/10 (top) to H/8 (bottom) - [x] **Base slab thickness** → D = H/10 to H/12 - [x] **Junction detail** → 45° haunch, 300 mm × 300 mm - [x] **Reinforcement detail** → L-bends with development length ≥ 12φ - [x] **Min cover in soil** → 50 mm (severe exposure) - [x] **Max spacing of main bars** → 300 mm or 3d - [x] **Lap length (Fe415, tension)** → 50φ - [x] **Stability FS** → OT ≥ 1.55, Sliding ≥ 1.5 - [x] **Eccentricity check** → e ≤ L/6 (middle third rule) - [x] **K_a (φ = 30°)** → 1/3 - [x] **γ_s (standard)** → 18 kN/m³ - [x] **μ (base friction)** → 0.5 - [x] **Standard soil** → IS 456 Table 16A: severe exposure - [x] **Earth pressure formula** → $p_z = K_a \gamma_s z$ - [x] **Resultant of triangular load** → acts at h/3 from base - [x] **Counterfort vs Buttress** → Counterfort on back face (in soil); Buttress on front face (visible) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** > Draw a clear sketch of the wall section **before** answering any design question. Label: H, h_stem, D (base), B (toe), L' (heel), S (counterfort spacing), pressure distribution, haunch, L-bends. A well-labeled diagram fetches **2-3 bonus marks** even in theory questions.

topics.title: Introduction to Strut and Tie Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET631 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Strut and Tie Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Strut-and-Tie Method (STM)** is a displacement-based, lower-bound, discrete equilibrium-based design philosophy used for the **strength design of reinforced concrete members in regions of stress discontinuity**, known as **D-regions**. The method idealises the structural element as an equivalent **truss** composed of:

- **Struts** (compression members, representing concrete stress fields),
- **Ties** (tension members, representing reinforcement), and
- **Nodes** (intersections where forces equilibrate, often called **nodal zones** or **Nodal Zones / N-joints**).

The design satisfies equilibrium at all nodes, ensures that the capacity of every strut, tie, and node is not exceeded, and that the **ductility / detailing requirements** at nodes are satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Strut-and-Tie Method is essentially a **rationalised extension of the classical truss analogy** (Ritter-Mörsch truss) applied not just to shear in beams, but to **all D-regions** where plane sections DO NOT remain plane (i.e., Saint-Venant's principle does not apply).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Skeletal Truss Inside Concrete"

Imagine a chunk of concrete as a **living organism**. When you load it near a hole, a corbel, a pile cap, or a deep beam, the load does not flow in neat horizontal layers (the way classical beam theory says). Instead, the compressive stress lines **arch** through the concrete, and tensile stress lines **stretch** like ropes.

- The **concrete** in compression acts like the **diagonal/arched struts** of a steel roof truss.
- The **steel reinforcement** embedded in the concrete acts like the **tension ties** of the truss.
- The **joints** where these struts and ties meet are the **nodes** — the "pivots" of the truss.

If you can "see" this invisible internal truss, you simply size the steel (ties) to carry the tension, check the concrete (struts) to carry the compression, and detail the joints (nodes) to anchor the bars properly. That is STM in a nutshell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea for KTU:** STM is *not* an exact analysis — it is a **lower-bound, equilibrium-based idealisation**. As long as the assumed truss is in equilibrium with the applied loads and reactions, and the capacities of struts/ties/nodes are not exceeded, the member is **safe** (Drucker's lower-bound / static theorem of plasticity).

### 1.3 Why is STM Required? — The B-Region vs. D-Region Distinction

Concrete structures are divided into two regions:

| Region Type | Full Form | Behaviour | Design Method | Example Locations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B-region** | Bernoulli region | Plane sections remain plane; strain varies linearly | Flexure + classical shear (IS 456) | Mid-span of a simply supported beam |
| **D-region** | Discontinuity region | Plane sections DO NOT remain plane; non-linear strain distribution | **Strut-and-Tie Method (STM)** | Supports, corbels, deep beams, pile caps, beam-column joints, dapped ends, openings |

> [!WARNING]
> **The discontinuity can be of TWO types:**
> 1. **Geometric discontinuity** — sudden change in cross-section (e.g., dapped end, opening, notch).
> 2. **Static (loading) discontinuity** — concentrated loads, reactions, prestressing anchorages.
> 
> Per **Saint-Venant's Principle**, a D-region extends approximately a distance equal to the **depth of the member (D)** from the point of discontinuity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Strut-and-Tie Model

A complete STM idealisation consists of three essential components. Each is detailed below.

#### 2.1.1 Struts (Compression Members)
- **Definition:** A strut is an idealised compression element in the STM truss that represents the **resultant of a uniaxial compression stress field** in the concrete.
- **Geometry:** Can be **prismatic** (rectangular cross-section) or **bottle-shaped** (radiating outward). The bottle shape allows the stress to spread laterally.
- **Effective Cross-Sectional Area ($A_{cs}$):** The cross-section of the strut taken perpendicular to its axis at the node face. For a prismatic strut, $A_{cs}$ equals the strut width $\times$ its thickness perpendicular to the plane of the truss.
- **Concrete Strength in Strut:** The compressive strength of a strut is **reduced** below the cylinder strength $f_{ck}$ using an **efficiency factor** ($\beta_s$) to account for cracking, transverse tension, and disturbance.

#### 2.1.2 Ties (Tension Members)
- **Definition:** A tie is an idealised tension element in the STM truss that represents the **resultant of a uniaxial tension stress field**, carried entirely by **reinforcing steel** (concrete's tensile strength is conservatively neglected in ULS design).
- **Components:** Consists of (a) the **longitudinal reinforcing bars** and (b) the **anchorage** (development length / hooks / mechanical anchors) at the nodes.
- **Effective Cross-Sectional Area ($A_{ts}$):** Equal to the area of reinforcement embedded within the tie's effective width.
- **Steel Strength in Tie:** The tie must yield *before* the concrete crushes — so tie capacity is governed by **$f_y$** of the steel.
- **Anchorage Requirement:** Bars must be fully developed *beyond* the node face by an amount equal to the **development length $L_d$**.

#### 2.1.3 Nodes (Nodal Zones / Joints)
- **Definition:** The intersection of struts and ties — a finite volume of concrete in which forces from struts and ties are equilibrated.
- **Type Classification (based on the sign of forces meeting at the node):**
  - **CCC Node:** Three or more struts meet (pure compression joint) — strongest node.
  - **CCT Node:** One tie meets multiple struts — moderate strength.
  - **CTT Node:** Two or more ties meet (tension-dominated) — weakest node; requires special anchorage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favourite Conceptual Trap:** Students often forget that the **node is a finite region**, not a point. The reinforcement must be anchored *behind* the back face of the node, not at the geometric intersection point.

### 2.2 Lower-Bound Theorem — Why STM is "Safe"

The static (lower-bound) theorem of plasticity states:
> *"If a system of forces can be found that is in equilibrium with the applied loads and nowhere violates the yield condition, the structure will not collapse under those loads."*

Because STM satisfies:
1. **Equilibrium** (forces at every node sum to zero in x and y),
2. **Yield criteria not violated** (strut, tie, and node capacities are not exceeded),
3. **Compatibility of deformations** (assumed truss geometry is reasonable),

→ the design is **safe by the lower-bound theorem** (provided the assumed truss is statically admissible).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nominal strength of a strut (uncracked, prismatic) | $F_{ns} = 0.60 \, f_{ck} \, A_{cs}$ | kN | $\beta_s = 0.60$ for prismatic, uncracked |
| 2 | Strength factor for prismatic strut | $\beta_s = 0.60$ | – | For prismatic struts with uniform compression |
| 3 | Strength factor for bottle-shaped strut (cracked) | $\beta_s = 0.40$ | – | Cracking due to lateral spreading; transverse reinforcement needed |
| 4 | Strength factor for struts in tension flanges | $\beta_s = 0.60$ | – | Compression in cracked flanges of beams |
| 5 | Strength of tie (tension) | $F_{nt} = f_y \, A_{st}$ | kN | Steel yields before concrete crushes |
| 6 | Nodal strength (CCC, fully confined) | $F_{nn} = 0.85 \, \beta_n \, f_{ck} \, A_{nz}$ | kN | $\beta_n = 1.0$ for CCC node |
| 7 | Nodal strength (CCT node) | $F_{nn} = 0.80 \, f_{ck} \, A_{nz}$ | kN | Equivalent to $\beta_n = 0.80$ |
| 8 | Nodal strength (CTT node) | $F_{nn} = 0.60 \, f_{ck} \, A_{nz}$ | kN | $\beta_n = 0.60$ — anchorage critical |
| 9 | Equilibrium at any node (horizontal) | $\sum F_x = 0$ | – | $\sum$ of horizontal force components = 0 |
| 10 | Equilibrium at any node (vertical) | $\sum F_y = 0$ | – | $\sum$ of vertical force components = 0 |
| 11 | Angle between strut and tie | $\theta \geq 25^\circ$ | degrees | Per ACI 318; minimum to avoid compatibility issues |
| 12 | Effective concrete strength in strut | $f_{ce} = 0.85 \, \beta_s \, f_{ck}$ | MPa | Often called "effective compressive strength" |
| 13 | Required tie area for a given tie force | $A_{st,req} = F_{tie} \,/\, f_y$ | mm² | From equilibrium |
| 14 | Minimum anchorage length behind node | $L_d \geq f_y \, \phi \,/\, (4 \, \tau_{bd})$ | mm | Per IS 456 Cl. 26.2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on coefficients:** The values $\beta_s$ and $\beta_n$ above are the **ACI 318M-19 efficiency factors**. Indian Standard **IS 456:2000** does not contain STM explicitly; however, KTU 2024 follows the ACI / fib Model Code approach in the advanced design elective. The factor **$0.60 f_{ck}$** in the strut strength is a universally accepted simplification.

### 2.4 Why These Coefficients? — Engineering Rationale

- **$\beta_s = 0.60$** for prismatic struts: Concrete under uniaxial compression between two rigid platens shows a nominal strength of about $0.60 f_{ck}$ to $0.85 f_{ck}$ in in-situ conditions. The factor 0.60 is conservative.
- **$\beta_s = 0.40$** for bottle-shaped struts: As the strut "fans out," lateral tensile stresses develop transverse to the strut axis, which can split the concrete. Transverse reinforcement (links / ties crossing the strut) must be provided to control this splitting, and the strength is reduced to 0.40.
- **$\beta_n$ values for nodes:** The more tension entering a node, the more likely it is to crack and spall. Hence CCC > CCT > CTT in strength.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of STM

| Application | Why STM? |
|---|---|
| **Deep beams** ($L/D < 2$ for simply supported) | Significant D-region; classical beam theory fails |
| **Corbels / Bracket supports** | Statically and geometrically discontinuous |
| **Pile caps** (2-pile, 3-pile, 4-pile) | Concentrated pile reactions, no plane-section behaviour |
| **Dapped-end beams** | Geometric notch, severe stress concentration |
| **Beam-column joints** | High shear, transfer of moments in 3D |
| **Pier diaphragms** in bridges | Heavy concentrated loads, openings |
| **Transfer girders / Walls with openings** | Sudden change in load path |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Strut Capacity from Stress Block

**Statement:** A prismatic concrete strut of cross-section $b_s \times w_s$ (width × thickness) carries an axial compressive force. Determine its nominal capacity $F_{ns}$.

**Step 1: Identify the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the strut axis.**

$$A_{cs} = b_s \times w_s$$

**Step 2: Apply the efficiency factor for prismatic compression struts.**

The design compressive stress in the concrete strut is taken as:

$$f_{ce} = 0.60 \, f_{ck}$$

**Step 3: Multiply the effective stress by the area to obtain the nominal capacity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ns} &= f_{ce} \times A_{cs} \\
       &= 0.60 \, f_{ck} \times b_s \times w_s
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply the strength reduction factor $\phi$ (in LRFD) — for KTU/IS working stress approach, this is implicit and not applied as a separate factor.**

$$\phi F_{ns} = 0.75 \times 0.60 \, f_{ck} \, b_s \, w_s = 0.45 \, f_{ck} \, b_s \, w_s$$

**Interpretation:** This formula is used to **check** the assumed strut dimensions. If the demand on the strut exceeds $F_{ns}$, the strut width must be increased.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tie Force and Required Reinforcement

**Statement:** A horizontal tie is subjected to an axial tensile force $T$ from equilibrium of the STM truss. Compute the required area of steel $A_{st}$.

**Step 1: From nodal equilibrium, the force in the tie is known.**

For a chosen truss, the tie force $T$ is found from the method of joints (or graphically).

**Step 2: Equate the tie force to the tie's nominal capacity.**

The tie is purely tensile; the steel yields at $f_y$. The concrete's tensile contribution is conservatively neglected.

$$T = F_{nt} = f_y \, A_{st}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the required steel area.**

$$A_{st,req} = \dfrac{T}{f_y}$$

**Step 4: Apply minimum reinforcement (if applicable, e.g., for deep beams).**

For deep beams, ACI 318 specifies:

$$\rho_{min} = \dfrac{A_{st,min}}{b_s \, d} = 0.0025$$

Therefore:

$$A_{st,min} = 0.0025 \, b_s \, d$$

**Step 5: Final design area is the greater of the two:**

$$A_{st,design} = \max(A_{st,req}, A_{st,min})$$

**Step 6: Choose bar diameters and lay them out symmetrically (for ductility).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The *bar layout* matters, not just the area. Bars must lie within the **effective tie width** of the strut-and-tie model. Bars outside this width are assumed not to participate in the load path and are not credited in the design.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nodal Equilibrium (Method of Joints)

**Statement:** At a CCT node, a vertical strut force $C_v$, a horizontal tie force $T$, and an inclined strut force $C_i$ meet. Derive the equilibrium conditions.

**Schematic of the joint (loads and axes):**

- Inclined strut at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal: $C_i$
- Horizontal tie: $T$ (acting along x-axis)
- Vertical strut (compression from above): $C_v$

**Step 1: Sum of forces in the x-direction must equal zero.**

$$-T + C_i \cos \alpha = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$T = C_i \cos \alpha$$

**Step 2: Sum of forces in the y-direction must equal zero.**

$$-C_v - C_i \sin \alpha = 0$$

(Assuming strut force $C_i$ has a downward vertical component and the vertical strut $C_v$ acts upward; signs may be reversed depending on direction convention.)

Rearranging:

$$C_v = -C_i \sin \alpha$$

The negative sign indicates that $C_i$ and $C_v$ act in opposite vertical senses, which is consistent with the geometry of a corbel or knee joint.

**Step 3: Combine to eliminate $C_i$:**

$$C_i = \dfrac{T}{\cos \alpha} = \dfrac{C_v}{-\sin \alpha}$$

Therefore:

$$\tan \alpha = -\dfrac{C_v}{T} = \dfrac{\vert C_v \vert}{T}$$

**Step 4: This angle $\alpha$ must satisfy the minimum angle criterion $\alpha \geq 25^\circ$.**

If $\alpha < 25^\circ$, the truss geometry is *kinematically inadmissible* (it would require infinite strut forces to equilibrate) and the model must be **reconfigured** (e.g., add a secondary tie or change the layout).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Free-Body Diagram Approach to Identify Strut & Tie Forces

**Statement:** A simply supported deep beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ (kN/m). Using STM, idealise the beam as a Pratt-like truss and find the diagonal strut and tie forces.

**Step 1: Identify the B/D region split.**

For a simply supported beam, the D-region extends a distance $D$ from each support, where $D$ is the beam depth. The middle (B-region) is only a fraction of the total span if $L/D < 2$.

**Step 2: Compute the support reaction.**

$$V = R_A = R_B = \dfrac{w L}{2}$$

**Step 3: Replace the D-region with a single STM truss.**

The simplest model is a **triangular truss** with:
- A horizontal tie at the bottom (between supports),
- An inclined strut from support A to the load point, and
- A second inclined strut from support B to the load point.

**Step 4: Cut a section through the diagonal strut and take moments about the bottom tie.**

At mid-span, moment from external load:

$$M_{ext} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8}$$

**Step 5: Equate external moment to internal tie force × lever arm:**

$$T \times jd = \dfrac{w L^2}{8}$$

where $jd$ is the lever arm (≈ $0.9d$ for beams, but in STM we use the **physical lever arm = depth of the truss = $D$** for a deep beam).

**Step 6: Solve for the tie force:**

$$T = \dfrac{w L^2}{8 D}$$

**Step 7: Equate vertical components of the inclined strut to the reaction:**

$$C_i \sin \alpha = \dfrac{w L}{2}$$

For a deep beam with $\tan \alpha = D / (L/2)$:

$$\sin \alpha = \dfrac{D}{\sqrt{D^2 + (L/2)^2}}, \quad \cos \alpha = \dfrac{L/2}{\sqrt{D^2 + (L/2)^2}}$$

**Step 8: Therefore, the diagonal strut force is:**

$$C_i = \dfrac{w L / 2}{\sin \alpha} = \dfrac{w L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{D^2 + (L/2)^2}}{D}$$

**Step 9: The horizontal component of the inclined strut must equal the tie force (equilibrium check):**

$$C_i \cos \alpha = \dfrac{w L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L/2}{D} = \dfrac{w L^2}{4 D} = 2T \quad \checkmark$$

(The factor of 2 arises because two symmetric inclined struts each contribute half the tie force.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The horizontal component of the diagonal strut force is **twice the tie force** in this symmetric model. This is the standard result for a simply supported deep beam idealised with two inclined struts meeting a horizontal tie.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Corbel Design (Simplified)

**Problem:** A corbel projects $a_v = 250$ mm from a column face. It carries a vertical factored load $V_u = 600$ kN at $a_v = 250$ mm and a horizontal factored load $N_{uc} = 100$ kN. Column width $b = 300$ mm, effective depth $d = 500$ mm, $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa. Design the corbel using STM.

**Step 1: Check applicability of STM (shear span-to-depth ratio).**

$$\dfrac{a_v}{d} = \dfrac{250}{500} = 0.5 \leq 1.0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (STM applicable)}$$

**Step 2: Idealise as a triangular truss.**

Assume a strut angle:

$$\tan \theta = \dfrac{d}{a_v} = \dfrac{500}{250} = 2.0 \Rightarrow \theta = 63.4^\circ \geq 25^\circ \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: From equilibrium at the top node, find the tie force.**

Sum of horizontal forces:

$$T = V_u \cot \theta + N_{uc}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\cot \theta &= \dfrac{1}{\tan \theta} = \dfrac{1}{2.0} = 0.5 \\
T &= 600 \times 0.5 + 100 = 300 + 100 = 400 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Required tie area.**

$$A_{st,req} = \dfrac{T}{f_y} = \dfrac{400 \times 10^3}{500} = 800 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **4 bars of 16 mm $\phi$** ($A_{st,prov} = 4 \times 201 = 804$ mm² $\approx 800$ mm² ✓)

**Step 5: Diagonal strut force.**

$$C = \dfrac{V_u}{\sin \theta} = \dfrac{600}{\sin 63.4^\circ} = \dfrac{600}{0.894} = 671 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Required strut width at the node (CCT node).**

CCT node strength: $0.60 f_{ck} A_{nz} \geq C$ (using $\beta_n = 0.60$ for simplicity):

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{nz,req} &= \dfrac{C}{0.60 \, f_{ck}} = \dfrac{671 \times 10^3}{0.60 \times 30} = 37{,}278 \text{ mm}^2 \\
w_s &= \dfrac{A_{nz,req}}{b} = \dfrac{37{,}278}{300} = 124.3 \text{ mm} \approx 125 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Provide a bearing plate of $300 \times 125$ mm or increase $d$ to ensure adequate bearing.

**Step 7: Check anchorage of the main tie bars.**

Total tie force per bar: $T / 4 = 100$ kN/bar.

Anchorage length required:

$$L_d = \dfrac{f_y \phi}{4 \tau_{bd}} = \dfrac{500 \times 16}{4 \times 1.4 \times 1.6} = \dfrac{8000}{8.96} = 893 \text{ mm}$$

(using $\tau_{bd} = 1.4$ for M30, deformed bars, per IS 456 Table 26)

If $L_d$ cannot be provided in the column width, provide a **horizontal hairpin anchorage** or **welded plate** at the back face of the column.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Using $A_{st,req} = T/f_y$ and forgetting to **multiply by 1.0 (no further factor) and check the anchorage**. Bars must develop their full yield strength at the node face.

---

### 3.6 Summary Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for STM Design

| Step | Task | Output | Cross-check |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify D-regions of the structure | Region map | Saint-Venant: extent ≈ D from discontinuity |
| 2 | Sketch free-body diagram of D-region | Geometry, dimensions, load magnitudes | Reactions from structural analysis |
| 3 | Choose an STM truss topology | Truss with struts, ties, nodes | All applied loads must enter the truss |
| 4 | Solve truss (method of joints / sections) | Member forces $C_i$ and $T_j$ | Sum of forces at every node = 0 |
| 5 | Verify strut angle criterion | $\theta \geq 25^\circ$ | Re-design truss if violated |
| 6 | Size ties (steel area) | $A_{st,req} = T / f_y$ | $\geq A_{st,min}$ if specified |
| 7 | Size struts (concrete area) | $A_{cs,req} = C / (0.60 f_{ck})$ | Adjust dimensions if necessary |
| 8 | Check nodal capacities | $C \leq 0.60 f_{ck} A_{nz}$ (CTT), etc. | Bearing plate if not adequate |
| 9 | Detail anchorage | $L_d$ provided ≥ $L_d$ required | Use hooks/plates if needed |
| 10 | Provide minimum distributed reinforcement | $\rho_{min}$ for cracks / splitting | 0.0025 for deep beams (ACI) |


> [!NOTE]
> **Visual cue:** The D-regions are where you see **concentrated reactions / loads or geometric notches**. The B-region (if it exists) is the zone where **standard beam theory** (IS 456 / flexure + shear) is valid.
