$$\text{Max } w/t = 0.42 \sqrt{\dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5}{240}} = 0.42 \times 28.87 = 12.1$$

> Since $b/t = 24 > 12.1$, the **unstiffened flange is over the limit** and the allowable stress must be reduced (per IS 801 Cl. 3.4). However, the section can still be used — but the reduction factor is applied.

**Step 3 — Slenderness Parameter $\lambda$**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1.052 \times 48}{2.0} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{240}{2.0 \times 10^5}}$$

$$\lambda = 25.25 \times 0.03464 = 0.875$$

**Step 4 — Reduction Factor for Unstiffened Element**

$$\rho = \dfrac{0.95 - 0.22 / 0.875}{0.875} = \dfrac{0.95 - 0.2514}{0.875} = \dfrac{0.6986}{0.875} = 0.798$$

**Step 5 — Effective Width**

$$b_{eff} = 0.798 \times 48 = 38.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Effective Area**
Computing $A_{eff}$ considering the reduced flange (the two flanges each reduce from 48 mm to 38.3 mm; the web remains fully effective):

$$A_{eff} \approx A_g - 2 t (b - b_{eff}) = (120 \times 2 + 50 \times 2 \times 2) - 2 \times 2 \times (48 - 38.3)$$

$$A_{eff} = (240 + 200) - 2 \times 2 \times 9.7 = 440 - 38.8 = 401.2 \text{ mm}^2 = 4.012 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 7 — Slenderness Ratio about the Minor Axis**
For a plain channel of depth 120 mm and flange 50 mm:
- $I_{yy} \approx 1.74 \text{ cm}^4$
- $r_{yy} \approx 1.85 \text{ cm}$

$$\dfrac{KL}{r_{yy}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 250}{1.85} = 135.1$$

**Step 8 — Compute $C_c$**

$$C_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi^2 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{240}} \approx 128.3$$

Since $KL/r = 135.1 > C_c = 128.3$ → **Elastic (Euler) buckling** governs.

**Step 9 — Compute $F_a$**

$$F_a = \dfrac{12 \pi^2 E}{23 (KL/r)^2} = \dfrac{12 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{23 \times (135.1)^2}$$

$$F_a = \dfrac{2.369 \times 10^7}{23 \times 18{,}252} = \dfrac{2.369 \times 10^7}{419{,}796} = 56.43 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 10 — Safe Axial Load**

$$P_{safe} = F_a \times A_{eff} = 56.43 \times 401.2 = 22{,}640 \text{ N} \approx 22.6 \text{ kN}$$

> **Answer:** Effective flange width = **38.3 mm**, Safe axial load = **22.6 kN**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Frequently Lose Marks on Light Gauge Section Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.052 factor in $\lambda$** — Many students use $\lambda = b/t \cdot \sqrt{f_y/E}$ instead of the correct $\lambda = 1.052 \cdot b/t \cdot \sqrt{f_y/E}$. This under-estimates $\lambda$ and over-estimates the effective width, giving a **dangerously unsafe** design.
>
> 2. **Applying the wrong reduction-factor formula** — The formula for **stiffened** elements ($\rho = (1 - 0.22/\lambda)/\lambda$) and **unstiffened** elements ($\rho = (0.95 - 0.22/\lambda)/\lambda$) are different. Using the stiffened formula for an outstanding flange of a channel is a **common** error.
>
> 3. **Using the gross area instead of effective area** — When a plate element has $\rho < 1.0$, you MUST recompute $A$, $I$, and $Z$ using $b_{eff}$ for the affected elements. Simply using the gross $A$ yields an **unsafe** column design.
>
> 4. **Neglecting the corner bend radius** — The flat width $b$ is measured **between the corner bends**, not as the nominal flange width. Omitting this correction can cause a 3–8% error in $b_{eff}$.
>
> 5. **Ignoring lateral-torsional buckling (LTB)** — For unbraced beams, simply checking against $F_b = 0.6 f_y$ is incomplete. IS 801 Cl. 5.3 requires checking against the **LTB reduction curve** if the unbraced length exceeds the limiting value.
>
> 6. **Forgetting units** — All stresses in IS 801 are in **MPa**; moments in **N·mm**; lengths in **mm**. A unit slip-up leads to numerical answers that are off by factors of $10^3$ to $10^6$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for IS 811 / IS 801 / SP 6-5 Light Gauge Sections:**

- [ ] **IS 811:1987** = *product specification* — thickness 1–8 mm, $f_y$ 240 MPa typical, ductility + tolerance rules.
- [ ] **IS 801:1975** = *design code* — effective width, allowable stresses, LTB, connection design.
- [ ] **SP 6-5:1980** = *handbook* — section property tables, design charts, worked examples.
- [ ] Light gauge sections are **cold-formed** at **room temperature** — no heat treatment.
- [ ] The **effective width concept** is the single most important design tool.
- [ ] **Stiffened** elements are supported along **both** longitudinal edges; **unstiffened** along only one.
- [ ] Slenderness parameter: $\lambda = \dfrac{1.052 \cdot b}{t} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E}}$. **Do not forget 1.052.**
- [ ] Limiting $\lambda_{lim} = 0.673$ — below this, $\rho = 1.0$.
- [ ] Stiffened: $\rho = (1 - 0.22/\lambda)/\lambda$ for $\lambda > 0.673$.
- [ ] Unstiffened: $\rho = (0.95 - 0.22/\lambda)/\lambda$ for $\lambda > 0.673$.
- [ ] Max w/t for stiffened element = $1.44 \sqrt{E/f_y}$ ≈ 41 (for $f_y = 240$).
- [ ] Max w/t for unstiffened element = $0.42 \sqrt{E/f_y}$ ≈ 12.1 (for $f_y = 240$).
- [ ] Three buckling modes: **local**, **distortional**, **lateral-torsional**.
- [ ] Effective width → Effective area → Recompute all section properties.
- [ ] For limit state: $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.10$; $\gamma_m = 1.10$ for steel.
- [ ] Maximum slenderness limits: $KL/r \leq 200$ (compression), $KL/r \leq 300$ (tension).
- [ ] Lipped channels and lipped Z-sections are the **most efficient** profiles.
- [ ] LGSF is widely used in **composite slabs** and **permanent formwork** for concrete — the reason this topic appears in PECET631.
- [ ] **Memorize the value 0.673** — it appears in every KTU question on this topic.
- [ ] **In KTU numericals, always show** $\lambda$ → $\rho$ → $b_{eff}$ → $A_{eff}/I_{eff}/Z_{eff}$ → stress check. Skipping any step = loss of method marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Cross Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET631 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Cross Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Advanced Design of Concrete Structures** framework (aligned with IS 456:2000 and IS 1343:2012), the phrase **"Types of Cross Sections"** is used in two distinct but interrelated senses, and the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 requires mastery of **both**:

**(A) Geometric / Shape-based Classification** – the physical outline of the concrete member (Rectangular, T, L, I, Box, Circular, Spandrel, etc.) chosen to resist the internal force system efficiently.

**(B) Analytical / State-based Classification** – the way the section is *idealized mathematically* for computing stresses and section properties:
1. **Gross (Homogeneous Concrete) Section**
2. **Uncracked Transformed Section (Stage I / Pre-Cracking)**
3. **Cracked Transformed Section (Stage II / Post-Cracking)**
4. **Partially Cracked Section (Stage I–II Transition)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently interweave these two classifications. A *T-shaped beam* (Geometric Type) requires you to decide whether you are computing its properties as a *Gross Section* or a *Cracked Transformed Section* (Analytical Type) before applying equilibrium equations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **sponge cake with raisins inside** (a reinforced concrete beam).

- The **Gross Section** analogy: You treat the entire cake (concrete) AND the raisins (steel) as one solid block. This is roughly valid *before* the cake is stressed, just like uncracked concrete.
- The **Uncracked Transformed Section** analogy: You "pump up" each raisin to look like cake (multiply steel area by modular ratio $n$). The section is still whole, but steel is *boosted* to behave equivalently as concrete.
- The **Cracked Transformed Section** analogy: You pull the cake apart along a horizontal line below the centroid (concrete below the neutral axis is in tension and has *cracked* off). You only keep the upper "intact" cake portion, with the raisins (now transformed) sticking down into the empty zone.
- **Geometric Types** (Rectangular, T, L, I, Box): Just like different *cookie cutter shapes*, each shape is chosen to best resist specific loading (bending, torsion, shear, combined).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Cross-section selection is a *design decision* (geometric type), but cross-section **analysis** is a *mathematical idealization* (analytical type). KTU problems almost always combine both.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Modular Ratio:** $n = \dfrac{E_s}{E_c}$ (typically **$n = 13$** for M20 and **$n = 9.33$** for M30 per IS 456 short-term, clamped to **$n \le 0.83 m$** for long-term creep, but the *uncracked* section uses the long-term $n$).
- **Concrete Grade Range:** M20 – M60 for RC, M40 – M80 for PSC.
- **Steel Grade:** Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 550 (with $E_s = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$).
- **Effective Span Ratio:** $L/d$ limits vary with *section type* and support conditions per IS 456 Cl. 23.2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Analytical Classification — The "Three States" of a RC Section

When a reinforced concrete beam is progressively loaded, it passes through **three analytical states**:

| Analytical State | Stage | Concrete in Tension Zone | Steel Behaviour | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gross Section** | Pre-design | Assumed intact (no crack) | Embedded as-is | Preliminary sizing, deflection checks before cracking |
| **Uncracked Transformed** | Stage I (Service, $M < M_{cr}$) | Intact, fully effective | Transformed: $A_s' = (n-1) A_s$ | Pre-cracking stress, temperature/shrinkage checks |
| **Cracked Transformed** | Stage II (Service, $M \ge M_{cr}$) | **Ignored** (cracked) | Transformed: $A_s' = n A_s$ | Working-stress design, serviceability deflection |
| **Partially Cracked** | Transition | Partial contribution | Hybrid | Rare; used in detailed non-linear FEM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific:** For *prestressed concrete* (IS 1343), the **Gross Section** is the primary analytical model at transfer, while the **Cracked Transformed Section** is used for service loads *after* decompression. The **Uncracked Transformed Section** governs when computing stresses in uncracked PSC beams.

### 2.2 Geometric Classification — Section Shapes & Their Engineering Utility

| Geometric Type | Best For | Torsional Rigidity | Formwork Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular** | Simply-supported beams, columns | Low | Low | Residential slabs/beams |
| **T-Section** | Continuous beams in monolithic slabs | Moderate | Low | Floor beams, bridge decks |
| **L-Section** | Edge beams, spandrels | Low–Moderate | Low | Peripheral floor beams |
| **I-Section** | Long-span beams, girders | Moderate | High | Industrial, bridges |
| **Box (Closed) Section** | Torsion-dominant members, PSC bridges | **Very High** | Very High | Curved bridges, pier caps |
| **Circular / Spiral** | Axial + bending columns | Symmetric | Medium | Piers, piles |
| **Flanged (T/L inverted)** | Cantilevers, negative moment regions | Moderate | Low | Cantilever slabs |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are derivable from first principles of **transformed section analysis** and are universally testable. Subscripts: $g$ = gross, $u$ = uncracked, $cr$ = cracked, $st$ = steel.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modular Ratio | $n = E_s / E_c$ | dimensionless | All states |
| 2 | Modular Ratio (IS 456) | $n = 280 / (3 \sigma_{cbc})$ or tabulated | dimensionless | WSM |
| 3 | Gross Moment of Inertia (Rect.) | $I_g = b d^3 / 12$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | Gross section |
| 4 | Gross Section Modulus | $Z_g = b d^2 / 6$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Gross section |
| 5 | Transformed Steel Area (Uncracked) | $A_{s,tr} = (n-1) A_s$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Stage I |
| 6 | Transformed Steel Area (Cracked) | $A_{s,tr} = n A_s$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Stage II |
| 7 | NA Depth (Cracked Rect., Singly Reinf.) | $b x^2 / 2 + n A_s x - n A_s d = 0$ | quadratic in $x$ | Stage II |
| 8 | Cracked Moment of Inertia (Rect.) | $I_{cr} = b x^3 / 3 + n A_s (d - x)^2$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | Stage II |
| 9 | Cracking Moment | $M_{cr} = f_{cr} I_g / y_t$ | N·mm | Transition |
| 10 | Effective $I$ (Branson, IS 456 Annex C) | $I_e = I_{cr} + \dfrac{I_g - I_{cr}}{(1 + (M_{cr}/M)^{2})}$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | Deflection |
| 11 | Flanged Effective Width | $b_f = b_w + l_0/6 + 6 d_f$ (per IS 456 Cl. 23.1.2) | mm | T/L beams |
| 12 | Hollow Box $I_x$ (Outer $B,D$, Inner $b_i, d_i$) | $I_x = (B D^3 - b_i d_i^3)/12$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | Box girders |
| 13 | Torsion Constant (Rect., approx.) | $J \approx k \cdot b^3 d$ with $k = f(h/b)$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | Torsion |

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Serviceability (Deflection & Crack Control)** is dictated by the *effective* moment of inertia, which is a weighted average of the gross and cracked values — directly linking analytical section type to field performance.
- **Prestressed Concrete Design** is entirely dependent on gross-section properties at transfer (since PSC is *uncracked* by design).
- **Torsion Design** of curved bridge girders, staircases, and edge beams mandates the use of *closed box* geometric sections due to the polar moment advantage.
- **Seismic Design** uses *capacity-based* section selection (e.g., flanged sections with adequate compression zone width for ductility).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition below is written out explicitly. No step-skipping. The aim is to make a KTU student reproduce this on the answer script.

### 3.1 Derivation: Neutral Axis Depth for a Cracked Rectangular Section (Singly Reinforced)

**Given:** Rectangular RC beam, width $b$, effective depth $d$, tension steel area $A_s$. Concrete below NA is *cracked* and ignored. Steel is transformed to equivalent concrete with area $n A_s$ located at depth $d$.

**Step 1 — Idealize the Cracked Section**

The cracked transformed section consists of:
- A concrete compression block of width $b$ and depth $x$ (the neutral axis depth) above the NA.
- A "pseudo-concrete" rectangle of area $n A_s$ and infinitesimal thickness, located at depth $d$ (this is the transformed tension steel).

**Step 2 — Apply the Fundamental Equilibrium of Transformed Section**

For a transformed section to be in pure bending with zero net axial force (no external axial load), the **first moment of the transformed area about the NA must equal zero**:

$$\sum A_i \cdot y_i = 0$$

> The translated equilibrium condition states that the compression area above the NA must balance the tension area below the NA about the NA itself.

**Step 3 — Identify the Two Areas and Their Lever Arms**

- **Compression Area:** $A_c = b \cdot x$ (full concrete block above NA).
  - Its centroid is at $x/2$ above the NA, so lever arm $= x/2$.
- **Tension Area (Transformed Steel):** $A_{st} = n \cdot A_s$.
  - Its centroid is at $(d - x)$ below the NA, so lever arm $= d - x$.

**Step 4 — Write the First-Moment Equation**

$$A_c \cdot \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \;=\; A_{st} \cdot (d - x)$$

Substituting the area expressions:

$$(b x) \cdot \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \;=\; (n A_s) \cdot (d - x)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify to a Standard Quadratic**

$$\frac{b x^2}{2} \;=\; n A_s (d - x)$$

Distribute the right-hand side:

$$\frac{b x^2}{2} \;=\; n A_s d \;-\; n A_s x$$

Bring all terms to the left-hand side to obtain a quadratic in $x$:

$$\frac{b x^2}{2} + n A_s x - n A_s d \;=\; 0$$

Multiply by $2/b$ to put in monic form:

$$x^2 + \left(\frac{2 n A_s}{b}\right) x - \left(\frac{2 n A_s d}{b}\right) \;=\; 0$$

> This is the **canonical KTU-form** quadratic whose positive root gives the neutral axis depth $x$.

**Step 6 — Solve Using the Quadratic Formula**

$$x = \frac{-\left(\dfrac{2 n A_s}{b}\right) + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{2 n A_s}{b}\right)^2 + 4 \left(\dfrac{2 n A_s d}{b}\right)}}{2}$$

Simplify by taking the discriminant common factor:

$$x = \frac{-\dfrac{2 n A_s}{b} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{2 n A_s}{b}\right)^2 + \dfrac{8 n A_s d}{b}}}{2}$$

A more compact, board-friendly form is:

$$x = \frac{n A_s}{b} \left[ \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 b d}{n A_s}} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Cracked Moment of Inertia**

The moment of inertia of the cracked transformed section about its NA is the sum of two contributions:

- **Concrete compression block** (rectangle, NA through base): $I_1 = \dfrac{b x^3}{3}$
- **Transformed tension steel** (area, parallel-axis theorem): $I_2 = n A_s (d - x)^2$

Therefore:

$$I_{cr} = \frac{b x^3}{3} + n A_s (d - x)^2$$

> This is the **working-stress moment of inertia** of the cracked section used for service-load stress and deflection calculations.

### 3.2 Derivation: Uncracked Transformed Section — Moment of Inertia

**Given:** A *non-cracked* rectangular RC section of width $b$, overall depth $D$, with steel at depth $d$ (tension) and $d_c$ (compression, if any). Concrete is intact; tension concrete is included; steel is replaced by equivalent concrete of area $(n-1)A_s$ at the same centroid.

**Step 1 — Establish the Reference Axis**

Take the reference axis through the **geometric centroid of the gross concrete section** (depth $D/2$ from top).

**Step 2 — Apply the Parallel-Axis Theorem for the Transformed Steel**

For a layer of steel at distance $\bar{y}$ from the gross centroid, its contribution to the moment of inertia is:

$$I_{steel, layer} = (n-1) A_s \bar{y}^2$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Contributions**

For symmetric tension and compression steel at equal distances $\bar{y}_s$ from the gross centroid:

$$I_{ut} = I_g + (n-1) A_{s,total} \bar{y}_s^2$$

For unsymmetric reinforcement, the general expression is:

$$I_{ut} = I_g + \sum_i (n-1) A_{si} \bar{y}_i^2$$

**Step 4 — Illustrative Numeric Form**

For a singly reinforced rectangular section (steel at $d$, centroid at $D/2$):

$$I_{ut} = \frac{b D^3}{12} + (n-1) A_s \left( d - \frac{D}{2} \right)^2$$

> **Key Note:** Unlike the cracked section, here we **add** (not subtract) the steel contribution. The factor is $(n-1)$ (not $n$) because the gross section already includes the *concrete-equivalent* of the steel location as plain concrete.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Section Type (Decision Tree)

The following logic determines which section type a KTU problem expects:

$$
\text{Section Type Selection}
\;=\;
\begin{cases}
\text{Gross}, & \text{if } M \le M_{cr} \text{ OR Transfer stage in PSC OR Temperature/Shrinkage analysis} \\[4pt]
\text{Uncracked Transformed}, & \text{if } M \le M_{cr} \text{ AND reinforcement is significant (RC)} \\[4pt]
\text{Cracked Transformed}, & \text{if } M > M_{cr} \text{ AND Working-Stress Method (WSM) used} \\[4pt]
\text{Effective } I \text{ (Branson)}, & \text{if Deflection calculation (IS 456 Annex C)} \\[4pt]
\text{Partially Cracked}, & \text{if non-linear FEM or refined serviceability analysis}
\end{cases}
$$

> The **cracking moment** $M_{cr}$ itself is computed using the **gross section**:

$$M_{cr} = \frac{f_{cr} \cdot I_g}{y_t}$$

where $f_{cr}$ is the modulus of rupture ($0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ per IS 456) and $y_t$ is the distance from the gross centroid to the extreme tension fibre.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Cross-Section State Evolution

The diagram below maps the *logical* evolution of a section as the applied moment increases, and identifies the governing equations at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["APPLIED LOADING M INCREASES"] --> B["M LESS THAN M_cr ?"]
    B -- "YES" --> C["GROSS SECTION Stage 0"]
    C --> C1["Use I_g equals bD cubed over 12"]
    C --> C2["Used in PSC Transfer Pre-Cracking Checks"]
    B -- "NO" --> D["STAGE I UNCRACKED TRANSFORMED"]
    D --> D1["Concrete tension INTACT"]
    D --> D2["Steel Area equals n minus 1 times A_s"]
    D --> D3["I_ut equals I_g plus n minus 1 A_s y squared"]
    A --> E["M GREATER THAN M_cr ?"]
    E -- "YES" --> F["STAGE II CRACKED TRANSFORMED"]
    F --> F1["Concrete tension IGNORED below NA"]
    F --> F2["Steel Area equals n times A_s"]
    F --> F3["Quadratic bx squared over 2 plus nA_s x minus nA_s d equals 0"]
    F --> F4["I_cr equals bx cubed over 3 plus nA_s d minus x squared"]
    F --> G["DEFLECTION CHECK IS 456 ANNEX C"]
    G --> G1["Branson Effective I_e formula"]
    G --> G2["Iterate with Service Moment M"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Geometric Section Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["DESIGN START"] --> Q["DOMINANT LOADING ?"]
    Q --> R1["BENDING DOMINANT"]
    Q --> R2["TORSION DOMINANT"]
    Q --> R3["AXIAL DOMINANT"]
    R1 --> S1["RECTANGULAR OR T OR I SECTION"]
    S1 --> S1A["Continuous Slab use T"]
    S1 --> S1B["Long Span use I"]
    S1 --> S1C["Simple Span use Rect"]
    R2 --> S2["CLOSED BOX OR HOLLOW SECTION"]
    S2 --> S2A["Curved Bridge use Box"]
    S2 --> S2B["Staircase Slab use Rect with Links"]
    R3 --> S3["RECTANGULAR OR CIRCULAR COLUMN"]
    S3 --> S3A["High Axial use Circular Spiral"]
    S3 --> S3B["Biaxial Bending use Rect"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Cracked Section Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W["GIVEN b, d, A_s, f_ck, f_y"] --> X["STEP 1 Compute modular ratio n"]
    X --> Y["STEP 2 Compute transformed steel area n times A_s"]
    Y --> Z["STEP 3 Form quadratic bx squared over 2 plus nA_s x minus nA_s d equals 0"]
    Z --> AA["STEP 4 Solve for x positive root"]
    AA --> AB["STEP 5 Check x less than x_u,max IS 456 Cl 38.1"]
    AB -- "OK" --> AC["STEP 6 Compute I_cr equals bx cubed over 3 plus nA_s d minus x squared"]
    AB -- "NOT OK" --> AD["REDESIGN Increase d or b"]
    AC --> AE["STEP 7 Compute service stresses sigma_c and sigma_s"]
    AE --> AF["STEP 8 Verify against IS 456 limits"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic (Text-Based) — Comparison of the Three Section Types

| Section State | Schematic Representation | Concrete in Tension | Steel Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gross** |  ████████████████  (single solid block) | Included as concrete | Native $A_s$ at native $E_s$ |
| **Uncracked Transformed** |  ████[███]████[███] (intact, with steel dots enlarged) | Included as concrete | Transformed $(n-1)A_s$ at depth $d$ |
| **Cracked Transformed** |  ████ (top) ░░░░░░ (gap) ▼ (steel dot) | **Ignored below NA** | Transformed $n A_s$ at depth $d$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The notation ▼ indicates a *concentrated* transformed steel area; ████ indicates effective concrete; ░░░░ indicates the *neglected* cracked tension zone.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Attempt!**
> 1. **Confusing the modular ratio factor:** Use $(n-1)$ for *uncracked* and $n$ for *cracked*. Mixing these costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the parallel-axis term** when computing $I_{ut}$ for unsymmetric sections (e.g., flanged beams) — deducts **2 marks**.
> 3. **Solving the wrong quadratic:** $b x^2/2 = n A_s (d-x)$ is for *singly reinforced rectangular cracked sections*. Doubly reinforced or flanged sections need a different equation. Marks lost: **3+**.
> 4. **Not validating $x \le x_{u,max}$** per IS 456 Cl. 38.1 — examiners explicitly check ductility.
> 5. **Mixing geometric and analytical types:** If a flanged beam is given, you cannot just use the rectangular formula; use the *effective flange width* $b_f$ correctly.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate clearly between the "Gross Section" and the "Cracked Transformed Section" used in the working-stress analysis of RC beams. Under what loading conditions is each preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Gross Section** is the full concrete section *ignoring the presence of reinforcement* and is treated as a homogeneous elastic body. It is used to compute the **cracking moment** $M_{cr}$ and for preliminary sizing. The **Cracked Transformed Section** idealizes the section by *ignoring the concrete below the neutral axis* (since it has cracked) and replacing the tension steel by an equivalent concrete area equal to $n A_s$. It is used for **service-load stress and deflection calculations** after cracking has occurred. The Gross Section is preferred at *transfer* in PSC and *below* $M_{cr}$; the Cracked Transformed Section is preferred *above* $M_{cr}$ in WSM design of RC beams.

*(Allocation: 1.5 marks for definition + 1 mark for condition + 0.5 mark for distinction clarity.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is a *closed box section* preferred over an *open I-section* for bridge girders subjected to significant torsional moments? Justify with reference to the relevant IS code clause.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **closed box section** has its torsional shear flow circulating in a *continuous closed loop*, producing a much larger **torsional constant $J$** (polar moment) and hence superior torsional rigidity per unit material. An **open I-section**, by contrast, has a thin, open profile whose torsional resistance relies almost entirely on the St. Venant warping constant — a *secondary* mechanism that is much weaker. IS 1343:2012 (Cl. 13) and IS 456:2000 (Cl. 41) implicitly require closed sections for *high torsion* cases such as curved bridges, because spalling of the top flange under torsion is prevented. Box sections also have *excellent lateral stability* and a low drag coefficient, making them aerodynamically and structurally ideal for long-span bridge girders.

*(Allocation: 1.5 marks for mechanism + 1 mark for IS clause + 0.5 mark for engineering justification.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE Module):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **neutral axis depth $x$** for a *singly reinforced rectangular cracked transformed section* of width $b$, effective depth $d$, and tension steel area $A_s$. State the equilibrium principle used.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular RC beam has $b = 230 \text{ mm}$, $d = 450 \text{ mm}$, $A_s = 942 \text{ mm}^2$ (4 bars of 20 mm dia, Fe 415). Take $E_s = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $E_c = 25000 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Compute:
   (i) The modular ratio $n$ and the transformed steel area.
   (ii) The neutral axis depth $x$ of the **cracked transformed section**.
   (iii) The **cracked moment of inertia** $I_{cr}$.
   (iv) The **service tensile stress in steel** under an applied service moment $M = 60 \text{ kN·m}$.  **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

*Equilibrium Principle:* In a transformed section under pure bending with no net axial force, the **first moment of the transformed area about the neutral axis is zero** ($\sum A_i y_i = 0$). This ensures no net axial resultant.

*Compressive Area:* $A_c = b x$, centroid at $x/2$ above NA.

*Tensile Area (Transformed Steel):* $A_{st} = n A_s$, centroid at $(d - x)$ below NA.

*Equilibrium equation:*

$$(b x) \cdot \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) = (n A_s) \cdot (d - x)$$

*Simplification:*

$$\frac{b x^2}{2} = n A_s d - n A_s x$$

$$\boxed{\frac{b x^2}{2} + n A_s x - n A_s d = 0}$$

*Solving the quadratic (positive root):*

$$x = \frac{n A_s}{b} \left[ \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 b d}{n A_s}} - 1 \right]$$

*(Marks: 2 for principle statement, 3 for derivation, 2 for final boxed equation and solution.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Modular ratio & transformed steel area:**

$$n = \frac{E_s}{E_c} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{25000} = 8$$

$$n A_s = 8 \times 942 = 7536 \text{ mm}^2$$

**(ii) Neutral axis depth:**

Substituting into the quadratic $\dfrac{b x^2}{2} + n A_s x - n A_s d = 0$:

$$\frac{230 \cdot x^2}{2} + 7536 \cdot x - 7536 \cdot 450 = 0$$

$$115 x^2 + 7536 x - 3391200 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$x = \frac{-7536 + \sqrt{7536^2 + 4 \cdot 115 \cdot 3391200}}{2 \cdot 115}$$

$$x = \frac{-7536 + \sqrt{56791296 + 1559952000}}{230}$$

$$x = \frac{-7536 + \sqrt{1616743296}}{230} = \frac{-7536 + 40208.25}{230} = \frac{32672.25}{230}$$

$$\boxed{x \approx 142.05 \text{ mm}}$$

Check: $x_u,max = 0.531 d = 0.531 \times 450 = 238.95 \text{ mm}$. Since $142.05 < 238.95$, the section is **under-reinforced** ✔ (safe).

**(iii) Cracked moment of inertia:**

$$I_{cr} = \frac{b x^3}{3} + n A_s (d - x)^2$$

Compute each term:

$$\frac{230 \times (142.05)^3}{3} = \frac{230 \times 2,866,956}{3} = \frac{659,400,000}{3} \approx 219,800,000 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$8 \times 942 \times (450 - 142.05)^2 = 7536 \times (307.95)^2 = 7536 \times 94,833.20 \approx 714,664,000 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\boxed{I_{cr} \approx 219.8 \times 10^6 + 714.7 \times 10^6 = 934.5 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4}$$

**(iv) Service tensile stress in steel:**

The lever arm between the compression centroid ($x/2$) and the tension steel ($d$) is $z = d - x/2$:

$$z = 450 - \frac{142.05}{2} = 450 - 71.025 = 378.975 \text{ mm}$$

The bending stress in the *transformed* steel is amplified back to the *real* steel stress by dividing by $n$:

$$\sigma_s = \frac{M \cdot (d - x)}{I_{cr}} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{M \cdot (d - x)}{I_{cr}}$$

Wait — for the *actual* steel, the stress is:

$$\sigma_s = n \cdot \frac{M \cdot (d - x)}{I_{cr}}$$

Substituting $M = 60 \times 10^6$ N·mm:

$$\sigma_s = 8 \times \frac{60 \times 10^6 \times (450 - 142.05)}{934.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_s = 8 \times \frac{60 \times 10^6 \times 307.95}{934.5 \times 10^6} = 8 \times 19.78 = 158.2 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_s \approx 158.2 \text{ MPa}}$$

This is well below the allowable $230 \text{ MPa}$ for Fe 415 in WSM — **section is safe in service**.

*(Marks allocation: 1 + 1 for part (i); 2 for part (ii); 1.5 for part (iii); 1.5 for part (iv).)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** For an *uncracked transformed section* of a doubly reinforced rectangular RC beam of width $b = 250 \text{ mm}$, overall depth $D = 500 \text{ mm}$, with $A_{st} = 1256 \text{ mm}^2$ at $d = 450 \text{ mm}$ and $A_{sc} = 628 \text{ mm}^2$ at $d_c = 50 \text{ mm}$, compute the **uncracked transformed moment of inertia** $I_{ut}$. Take $n = 13$.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Branson's formula** for effective moment of inertia used in IS 456. Why is it necessary to use an "effective" $I$ rather than a single cracked or gross value for deflection calculation?  **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Computation of $I_{ut}$ (7 Marks):**

*Step 1 — Gross moment of inertia of the concrete rectangle:*

$$I_g = \frac{b D^3}{12} = \frac{250 \times (500)^3}{12} = \frac{250 \times 1.25 \times 10^8}{12} = 2.6042 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

*Step 2 — Distance of each steel layer from the gross centroid (at $D/2 = 250$ mm from top):*

- Tension steel at $d = 450$ mm: $\bar{y}_{st} = 250 - 50 = 200$ mm? No — the gross centroid is at the mid-depth = 250 mm from top, so the tension steel at depth 450 mm is at $450 - 250 = 200$ mm **below** the centroid. So $\bar{y}_{st} = 200$ mm.
- Compression steel at $d_c = 50$ mm: $\bar{y}_{sc} = 250 - 50 = 200$ mm **above** the centroid. So $\bar{y}_{sc} = 200$ mm.

*Step 3 — Steel contribution via parallel-axis theorem:*

For an *uncracked* section, the steel is transformed by adding $(n-1) A_s$ at the steel location:

$$I_{steel} = (n-1) A_{st} \bar{y}_{st}^2 + (n-1) A_{sc} \bar{y}_{sc}^2$$

$$I_{steel} = 12 \times 1256 \times (200)^2 + 12 \times 628 \times (200)^2$$

$$I_{steel} = 12 \times 200^2 \times (1256 + 628) = 12 \times 40{,}000 \times 1884 = 9.0432 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

*Step 4 — Total uncracked transformed moment of inertia:*

$$I_{ut} = I_g + I_{steel} = 2.6042 \times 10^9 + 0.90432 \times 10^9$$

$$\boxed{I_{ut} \approx 3.5085 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4}$$

*Validation:* Compare with the cracked $I_{cr}$ which would be much smaller (typically 1/3 to 1/2 of $I_{ut}$ for a lightly reinforced section). ✔

*(Marks: 1.5 for $I_g$, 1.5 for distances, 2 for parallel-axis, 1 for summation, 1 for final boxed value.)*

---

**(b) Branson's Formula Explanation (7 Marks):**

*Branson's Effective Moment of Inertia* (per IS 456:2000 Annex C, Cl. C-2.1 and C-2.2):

$$I_{e,eff} = \frac{I_{cr}}{1.2 - \left(1.2 - \dfrac{M_{cr}}{M}\right) \dfrac{I_{cr}}{I_g}} \le I_g$$

A simplified form (used in IS 456 Cl. 23.2.1a for continuous beams):

$$I_e = I_{cr} + \frac{I_g - I_{cr}}{\left(1 + (M_{cr}/M)^2\right)}$$

**Why an "Effective" $I$ is needed:**

A real RC beam *transitions* from uncracked (Stage I) to cracked (Stage II) as the bending moment along its span increases — different regions of the *same* beam may be in different states (e.g., continuous beam: negative moment regions crack first, positive moment regions may stay uncracked under service load).

Using a single $I_g$ (gross) would *under-estimate* deflections in cracked regions, and using a single $I_{cr}$ would *over-estimate* deflections in uncracked regions. Branson's formula provides a **weighted average** of the two, weighted by the ratio $M_{cr}/M$:

- When $M \ll M_{cr}$: $I_e \to I_g$ (essentially uncracked).
- When $M \gg M_{cr}$: $I_e \to I_{cr}$ (fully cracked).
- In the transition: smooth interpolation, matching experimental data.

This accounts for **tension-stiffening** (the concrete between cracks still contributes some stiffness, raising the effective $I$ above the fully-cracked $I_{cr}$).

*(Marks: 2 marks for the formula, 2 marks for explaining the $M_{cr}/M$ weighting, 2 marks for tension-stiffening rationale, 1 mark for limit $I_e \le I_g$.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Three Analytical Section Types** (Gross, Uncracked Transformed, Cracked Transformed) are *mathematical idealizations*, not physical sections.
- [ ] **Gross Section** → use $I_g = bD^3/12$ for preliminary design and for $M_{cr}$ calculation.
- [ ] **Uncracked Transformed** → use factor $(n-1)$ for steel contribution; $I_{ut} = I_g + (n-1) \sum A_{si} \bar{y}_i^2$.
- [ ] **Cracked Transformed** → use factor $n$ for steel; ignore tension concrete; solve $\dfrac{bx^2}{2} + nA_s x - nA_s d = 0$.
- [ ] **Cracked $I_{cr}$** → $I_{cr} = \dfrac{bx^3}{3} + nA_s (d-x)^2$ for rectangular singly reinforced section.
- [ ] **Modular ratio $n$** is **13** for M20, **9.33** for M30 in WSM; long-term $n$ is multiplied by creep factor.
- [ ] **Always check** $x \le x_{u,max} = 0.531 d$ for Fe 415 to ensure ductile (under-reinforced) behaviour.
- [ ] **Flanged beams** (T/L) require $b_f$ as per IS 456 Cl. 23.1.2 — minimum of $b_f$ and actual flange width.
- [ ] **Box sections** for torsion — choose *closed* over *open* for high torsional rigidity.
- [ ] **Branson's effective $I$** for deflection — weighted average; accounts for tension stiffening.
- [ ] **For PSC** (prestressed concrete): use Gross Section at *transfer*; use Cracked Transformed for *service loads* only if decompression moment is exceeded.
- [ ] **Effective flange width** $b_f$ for T-beams in buildings: $b_f = b_w + l_0/6 + 6 d_f$ (per IS 456).
- [ ] **Steel stress formula** in WSM (cracked section): $\sigma_s = n \cdot M (d-x) / I_{cr}$.
- [ ] **Concrete stress formula** in WSM (cracked section): $\sigma_c = M \cdot x / I_{cr}$.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Module 4 focus areas** likely include *serviceability analysis of flanged sections*, *torsion design of box girders*, and *comparison of gross vs. cracked section properties* for irregular sections.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Tip:** When asked "which section type to use?", always state *the loading condition* ($M > M_{cr}$ or not) and the *design method* (WSM/LSM) — that alone fetches **1–2 marks** even if the rest of the answer is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Buckling and Post Buckling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET631 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Local Buckling and Post Buckling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Local buckling** is a structural instability phenomenon in which a slender component of a structural cross-section (such as a flange, web, or plate element) buckles locally under compression, while the overall member remains straight and unbuckled. In the context of advanced concrete design — particularly for **concrete-filled steel tubes (CFST)**, **steel-jacketed concrete sections**, **steel plate-concrete composite walls**, and **reinforcing steel sections** — local buckling governs the slenderness limits of the steel encasement before the full plastic capacity of the section is mobilized. **Post-buckling** refers to the *load-carrying reserve* of a thin plate element **after** the onset of local buckling. Unlike classical Euler-type global buckling (which causes immediate collapse), a flat plate in compression possesses significant **post-buckling reserve strength** because transverse membrane (tensile) stresses develop along the edges, redistributing the compressive load and allowing the plate to continue carrying load well beyond the initial bifurcation (critical) load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic under Module 4 examines the *interaction between steel confinement elements and concrete core* in composite/hybrid concrete construction. Local buckling of the steel skin determines the **confinement effectiveness** and hence the ultimate compressive strain of encased/prestressed concrete elements. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Crushed Soda Can Imagine pressing a full, sealed **aluminum soda can** slowly along its axis. The can does *not* suddenly shorten and bend like a long column (Euler buckling) — instead, the **cylindrical wall wrinkles** into a series of small diamond-shaped lobes. The can is still standing upright, the global geometry is intact, but the *thin wall* has buckled locally. This is **local buckling**. Now, keep pressing. Astonishingly, the can continues to resist further load — the wrinkles get denser and more pronounced, yet the can still holds your weight. This residual strength, present *after* the wrinkles have formed, is **post-buckling reserve**, caused by the development of transverse membrane tensions that *stretch* the wall sideways and force it to keep carrying axial compression through a combination of bending and membrane action. The same physics governs the **steel tube of a CFST column** or the **flange of a steel plate embedded in a concrete shear wall**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit | |:------:|:---------|:---------------------| | $E_s$ | Young's modulus of structural steel | **200 GPa** (IS 2062 / ASTM A36) | | $E_c$ | Young's modulus of concrete | **5000 √f_ck MPa** (IS 456) | | $\nu$ | Poisson's ratio of steel | **0.30** | | $f_y$ | Yield stress of steel | **250–550 MPa** (grade-dependent) | | $f'_{ck}$ | Characteristic cylinder strength of concrete | **20–80 MPa** | | $\lambda_L$ | Local slenderness ratio (b/t) | **≤ 40 (compact) ; 40–60 (non-compact) ; > 60 (slender)** | | $\varepsilon_y$ | Yield strain of steel | $f_y / E_s$ | > [!NOTE] > The plate-slenderness parameter $\lambda_p$ is the **single most important non-dimensional number** in local buckling problems. It compares the geometric slenderness of the plate to the material yield strain. $$ \lambda_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr,L}}} $$ where $\sigma_{cr,L}$ is the **classical elastic local buckling stress** of the plate element under uniform edge compression. --- ## 1.4 The Two Failure Regimes in Concrete-Steel Composite Elements In hybrid sections (e.g., CFST columns, double-skin tubes, steel plate shear walls with concrete infill), three distinct instability modes coexist: 1. **Global (Euler) buckling** of the whole member. 2. **Local buckling** of the steel skin (small-wavelength, plate-type wrinkling). 3. **Material crushing** of the concrete core. The **interaction** between modes (1) and (2) is what KTU 2024 Module 4 expects the student to master, since it dictates the **width-to-thickness limits (b/t)** specified in codes such as **Eurocode 4**, **AISC 360**, and **IS 11384** for composite concrete-steel members. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of Local Buckling in a Plate Element Consider a flat rectangular plate of length $a$, width $b$, and thickness $t$, simply supported on all four edges and subjected to uniform in-plane compressive stress $\sigma$ along two opposite edges (the *loaded* edges). The unloaded edges are *stress-free*. The plate has bending stiffness per unit width: $$ D = \dfrac{E_s \, t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)} $$ When $\sigma$ reaches a critical value $\sigma_{cr}$, the plate bifurcates from the flat (membrane) state into a slightly deflected (flexural) state with multiple half-waves in the loading direction. ### 2.1.1 Why and How the Plate Bends - **Why:** The plate has finite bending stiffness $D$. Under in-plane compression, the flat configuration becomes *unstable* — a small lateral perturbation triggers out-of-plane bending, which releases membrane strain energy faster than it raises bending strain energy. - **How:** The deflected shape satisfies the **biharmonic plate equation** under uniform edge compression. The condition of neutral equilibrium yields the bifurcation load. ## 2.2 Classical Elastic Local Buckling Stress The classical (small-deflection) elastic critical stress for a simply supported plate in uniform compression is: $$ \sigma_{cr,L} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{\sigma} \, \pi^{2} \, E_s}{12(1-\nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2} $$ The dimensionless **buckling coefficient** $k_{\sigma}$ depends on the loading and boundary conditions: | Edge condition / loading | $k_{\sigma}$ | |:------------------------|:------------:| | All four edges simply supported, uniform compression | **4.00** | | One loaded edge clamped, other simply supported | **5.42** | | Both loaded edges clamped, unloaded edges simply supported | **6.97** | | One loaded edge free (e.g., outstanding flange) | **0.425** | | Shear loading (simply supported) | **5.34** | | All edges clamped, uniform compression | **10.07** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For an outstanding flange of a steel section in a composite column**, $k_\sigma = 0.425$ (one edge free). This is the **lowest**, which is why outstands are the first to buckle locally. ## 2.3 Post-Buckling Behaviour — The "Reserve Strength" Classical small-deflection theory predicts that once $\sigma = \sigma_{cr,L}$, the plate carries no further load. **This is wrong for practical plates.** The plate can carry *significant* additional load because: 1. As the plate deflects laterally, its middle-surface stretches, generating **transverse membrane tensions** $N_{xy}$ along the supported edges. 2. These tensions resist further out-of-plane deformation. 3. The load is redistributed: regions near the supported edges carry stress $\sigma > \sigma_{cr,L}$, while the central buckled region sheds stress. 4. The result: a stable post-buckling load–shortening response up to yielding of the edge strips at $\sigma = f_y$. > [!NOTE] > This phenomenon is the **physical basis** of the *effective width concept* of **von Kármán (1932)**, later codified by **Winter (1947)**, and used in **Eurocode 3 Part 1.5** and **AISI North American Specification** for cold-formed steel. ## 2.4 Effective Width Concept (Winter's Formula) Instead of analysing the full non-uniform post-buckling stress distribution, we replace it with an *equivalent* uniform stress $f_y$ acting on a reduced **effective width** $b_{eff}$ placed at the supported edges. Winter's empirical reduction (calibrated against hundreds of test results) is: $$ \boxed{\; b_{eff} \;=\; b \,\rho \;=\; b \left[\dfrac{1 - 0.22 / \lambda_p}{\lambda_p}\right] \;\leq\; b\;} $$ with the **plate slenderness parameter**: $$ \lambda_p \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr,L}}} \;=\; \dfrac{b}{t}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{f_y \, 12(1-\nu^{2})}{k_\sigma \pi^{2} E_s}} $$ Equivalently (more compact form), with $\bar{\lambda}_p = \sqrt{f_y/\sigma_{cr,L}}$: $$ \rho \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{\lambda}_p - 0.22}{\bar{\lambda}_p^{2}} \qquad \text{for} \; \bar{\lambda}_p > 0.673 $$ and $\rho = 1.0$ for $\bar{\lambda}_p \le 0.673$ (the "stocky" regime where no local buckling reduction is needed). > [!TIP] > **Memorise the threshold $\bar{\lambda}_p = 0.673$** — it is the boundary between *fully effective* and *reduced effective* cross-section. Exam questions on this topic invariably test it. ## 2.5 The Three Slenderness Classes for Steel Elements in Concrete Construction | Class | Slenderness $\bar{\lambda}_p$ | Behaviour | Design rule | |:-----:|:----------------------------:|:----------|:------------| | **1 — Stocky / Compact** | $\le 0.673$ | No local buckling before yield; full plastic moment usable | Use **full section**, $b_{eff} = b$ | | **2 — Non-compact** | $0.673 < \bar{\lambda}_p \le \sqrt{2}$ | Local buckling **after** yield, before ultimate | Use **yield-line** capacity, partial reduction | | **3 — Slender** | $> \sqrt{2}$ | Local buckling **before** yield | Use **effective width** concept; elastic capacity only | > [!IMPORTANT] > For **CFST columns under IS / EC4**, the *limiting b/t* for circular tubes to qualify as **Class 1 (no local buckling)** is: > $$ \dfrac{d}{t} \le 90 \dfrac{235}{f_y} \quad \text{(EC4, circular CFST, pure compression)} $$ > Square/rectangular CFST: $b/t \le 52 \sqrt{235/f_y}$ (EC4, 2014). ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |:-:|:--------|:--------------| | 1 | $\sigma_{cr,L} = \dfrac{k_\sigma \pi^2 E_s}{12(1-\nu^2)}\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ | Critical elastic local buckling stress of a plate | | 2 | $D = \dfrac{E_s t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Flexural rigidity of a plate per unit width | | 3 | $\bar{\lambda}_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr,L}}} = \dfrac{b}{t}\sqrt{\dfrac{12(1-\nu^2) f_y}{k_\sigma \pi^2 E_s}}$ | Plate slenderness parameter (Winter) | | 4 | $b_{eff} = b \,\rho$, $\rho = \dfrac{\bar{\lambda}_p - 0.22}{\bar{\lambda}_p^{2}}$ | Winter's effective width (for $\bar{\lambda}_p > 0.673$) | | 5 | $\rho = 1$ for $\bar{\lambda}_p \le 0.673$ | Stocky regime: no reduction | | 6 | $P_{cr,local} = \sigma_{cr,L} \cdot b \cdot t$ | Local buckling load of a plate element | | 7 | $P_{u,post} \approx f_y \cdot b_{eff} \cdot t$ | Ultimate post-buckling load (effective-width method) | | 8 | $k_\sigma = 0.425$ | Buckling coefficient for an outstanding flange (one free edge) | | 9 | $k_\sigma = 4.0$ | Buckling coefficient for a plate simply supported on all four sides under uniform compression | | 10 | $\lambda_{L} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_y}{P_{cr,L}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y A}{\sigma_{cr,L} A}} = \bar{\lambda}_p$ | Local slenderness in column-style format | > [!NOTE] > **Utility in real engineering:** These formulas drive the design of **shear-wall boundary elements** (where flange outstands must be checked), **CFST columns in tall buildings**, **bridge piers**, and **hybrid offshore concrete structures** — all hot topics in KTU 2024 civil engineering practice. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Local Buckling Stress of a Simply Supported Plate We start from the **governing differential equation** of a flat plate in flexure under in-plane compressive force $N_x$ per unit length (small-deflection, classical plate theory): $$ D \nabla^{4} w + N_x \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} = 0 $$ **Step 1 — Identify the deflection ansatz.** For a simply supported rectangular plate (length $a$, width $b$) in uniform compression along $x$, the deflected buckled shape is a double-sine series. The first (lowest) mode is: $$ w(x,y) \;=\; W_{mn} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right) $$ where $m$ is the number of half-waves in the loading ($x$) direction, $n$ is the number across the width ($y$). **Step 2 — Substitute into the governing equation.** Compute the biharmonic: $$ \nabla^{4} w \;=\; W_{mn} \pi^{4} \left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^{2}\right]^{2} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right) $$ Compute the second derivative in $x$: $$ \dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} \;=\; -W_{mn} \left(\dfrac{m\pi}{a}\right)^{2} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi y}{b}\right) $$ Substituting into the governing equation and cancelling the non-zero sines: $$ D \pi^{4} \left[\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^{2}\right]^{2} \;=\; N_x \left(\dfrac{m \pi}{a}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for the critical load $N_{x,cr}$.** $$ N_{x,cr} \;=\; \dfrac{D \pi^{2}}{b^{2}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{m b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{m b}\, n^{2}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 4 — Express in stress form, with $n = 1$ (lowest transverse mode).** Setting $n = 1$ and using $D = E_s t^3 / [12(1-\nu^2)]$ and $\sigma_{cr,L} = N_{x,cr} / t$: $$ \sigma_{cr,L} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} D}{t \, b^{2}} \left(\dfrac{m b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{m b}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \sigma_{cr,L} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E_s}{12(1-\nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2} \underbrace{\left(\dfrac{m b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{m b}\right)^{2}}_{k_{\sigma}(m,a/b)} $$ **Step 5 — Identify the buckling coefficient $k_\sigma$.** $$ k_{\sigma}(m, a/b) \;=\; \left(\dfrac{m b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{m b}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 6 — Find the minimum over $m$.** The buckling coefficient is *minimised* when the term in the brackets is minimised. Differentiating with respect to $(a/mb)$ and setting to zero gives the *minimum* at: $$ \dfrac{m b}{a} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{a}{m b} = 1 $$ i.e., **square half-waves**. At this optimum: $$ k_{\sigma,\min} \;=\; (1 + 1)^{2} \;=\; 4.0 $$ This reproduces the textbook value $k_\sigma = 4.0$ for a simply supported plate in uniform compression. > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning of the minimum:** The plate "prefers" to buckle in *square* lobes because that shape minimises the total strain-energy release per unit increase in deflection. This is also why diamond-shaped lobes appear on crushed soda cans. **Final consolidated result:** $$ \boxed{\; \sigma_{cr,L} \;=\; \dfrac{4.0 \pi^{2} E_s}{12(1-\nu^{2})} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2} \;} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Plate Slenderness Parameter (Winter's $\bar{\lambda}_p$) Starting from the critical stress, the slenderness parameter is *defined* as the ratio of yield stress to critical stress, both under square-root: $$ \bar{\lambda}_p \;\equiv\; \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr,L}}} $$ Substituting the result of Derivation 1: $$ \bar{\lambda}_p \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y \cdot 12(1-\nu^{2})}{k_\sigma \pi^{2} E_s}} \cdot \dfrac{b}{t} $$ **Numerical example (worked):** Take a steel plate of grade E250 (Fe410), $f_y = 250$ MPa, $E_s = 200\,000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$, simply supported (so $k_\sigma = 4.0$), and $b/t = 40$. Step (a) — Compute the prefactor: $$ \sqrt{\dfrac{12(1 - 0.30^{2})}{4.0 \pi^{2}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{12 \times 0.91}{4.0 \times 9.8696}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{10.92}{39.478}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.2766} \;=\; 0.5260 $$ Step (b) — Multiply by $b/t$ and $\sqrt{f_y/E_s}$: $$ \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E_s}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{250}{200\,000}} \;=\; \sqrt{1.25 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.03536 $$ Step (c) — Combine: $$ \bar{\lambda}_p \;=\; 0.5260 \times 0.03536 \times 40 \;=\; 0.744 $$ Step (d) — Classify: $\bar{\lambda}_p = 0.744 > 0.673$, so the plate is **non-compact / slender**, and we must apply effective-width reduction. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Width Reduction (Winter's $\rho$) The reduction factor $\rho$ must satisfy: - $\rho \to 1$ as $\bar{\lambda}_p \to 0.673$ (continuity at threshold). - $\rho \to 0$ as $\bar{\lambda}_p \to \infty$ (very thin plate carries no load). - Empirical fit by Winter to **136 stub-column tests**: $$ \rho \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{\lambda}_p - 0.22}{\bar{\lambda}_p^{2}} $$ Step (a) — Check continuity at $\bar{\lambda}_p = 0.673$: $$ \rho(0.673) \;=\; \dfrac{0.673 - 0.22}{0.673^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.453}{0.4529} \;=\; 1.0003 \;\approx\; 1 $$ Step (b) — Asymptotic behaviour as $\bar{\lambda}_p \to \infty$: $$ \lim_{\bar{\lambda}_p \to \infty} \rho \;=\; \lim_{\bar{\lambda}_p \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{\bar{\lambda}_p} \;=\; 0 $$ **Application to the worked example (continued):** $\bar{\lambda}_p = 0.744$: $$ \rho \;=\; \dfrac{0.744 - 0.22}{0.744^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.524}{0.5535} \;=\; 0.947 $$ Therefore: $$ b_{eff} \;=\; 0.947 \times b $$ The reduction is only ~5.3% — the plate is still largely effective. If $b/t$ were 80 instead of 40, $\bar{\lambda}_p$ would double to ~1.49, and: $$ \rho(1.49) \;=\; \dfrac{1.49 - 0.22}{1.49^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.27}{2.22} \;=\; 0.572 $$ i.e., a **~43% loss of effective width** — a dramatic reduction. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Local vs. Global Buckling | Aspect | Global (Euler) Buckling | Local Buckling | Post-Buckling | |:-------|:----------------------:|:--------------:|:-------------:| | Wavelength | Long (member length scale) | Short (cross-section scale) | n/a (deflection grows) | | Cause | Slenderness of *whole member* | Slenderness of *plate element* | Membrane action in deflected plate | | Bifurcation? | Yes (sharp) | Yes (small-deflection theory) | No (smooth, stable) | | Reserve strength | **None** (catastrophic) | Some | **Significant** (50–200% of $P_{cr,L}$) | | Failure mode | Sudden sideways deflection | Small wrinkles / lobes | Stable wrinkles spreading | | Design remedy | Bracing, increase radius of gyration | Thicker plates, stiffeners, lower b/t | Implicitly via effective width | | Engineering use | Long columns, beams | Thin webs, flanges, tubes | Cold-formed sections, CFST tubes | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Local Buckling Decision Tree in a Composite Concrete-Steel Section ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Steel Skin / Flange / Tube
in Concrete Composite Section] --> B{Compute b/t Ratio} B -->|Low b/t| C[Class 1: Stocky
lambda_p less or equal 0.673] B -->|Medium b/t| D[Class 2: Non-compact
0.673 less than lambda_p less than 1.414] B -->|High b/t| E[Class 3: Slender
lambda_p greater than 1.414] C --> F[Design Action:
Use full cross-section b_eff = b] D --> G[Design Action:
Use partial effective width
rho computed from Winter] E --> H[Design Action:
Use full effective-width reduction
with rho factor] F --> I[Compute Ultimate Axial Capacity
Pu = fy * A_full + 0.67 fck * Ac] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Concrete Core:
Triaxial confinement factor kc] J --> K[Combined Capacity Pn
Compare with Applied Load Pu] style A fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style E fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 style K fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#5e35b1 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Buckled Plate Geometry and Stress Redistribution ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BEFORE[Before Local Buckling] B1[Flat plate
Uniform stress sigma across full width b]:::flat end subgraph CRITICAL[At Critical Load sigma_cr] C1[Slight out-of-plane deflection
Bifurcation point]:::crit end subgraph POST[Post-Buckling State] P1[Edge strips yield at fy
Central region sheds stress]:::post1 P2[Transverse membrane tension Nxy develops]:::post2 end subgraph EFFECTIVE[Effective Width Idealisation] E1[Effective width b_eff
carries uniform fy]:::eff1 E2[Discarded central width
stresses set to zero]:::eff2 end BEFORE --> CRITICAL --> POST --> EFFECTIVE classDef flat fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b classDef crit fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00 classDef post1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c classDef post2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c classDef eff1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 classDef eff2 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f ``` ## 4.3 Load–Shortening Curve (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[O: Zero Load
Flat Plate] -->|Linear elastic| B[Point A: sigma_cr Local Buckling Onset] B -->|Post-buckling reserve| C[Point B: Yield at Edges
sigma_edge = fy] C -->|Plastic spreading| D[Point C: Full Effective Yield
Pu = fy b_eff t] D -->|Tensile membrane| E[Point D: Ultimate
Membrane stretching] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style E fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Where Local Buckling Occurs in a Concrete-Filled Steel Tube (CFST) Column ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph COLUMN[CFST Column Cross-Section] S1[Outer Steel Tube
Wall thickness t]:::steel S2[Concrete Core
f_ck grade]:::concrete S3[Rebar Cage
Longitudinal bars]:::rebar end subgraph LB[Local Buckling Modes in CFST] M1[Mode 1: Tube Wall Outward Bulging
k_sigma = 4.0]:::mode M2[Mode 2: Tube Wall Elephant-Foot
Near base under high axial]:::mode M3[Mode 3: Inward Dent of Tube
Loss of concrete confinement]:::mode end S1 --> LB S2 -.Confines.- S1 S3 -.Provides additional capacity.- S2 classDef steel fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#0d47a1 classDef concrete fill:#d7ccc8,stroke:#4e342e classDef rebar fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c classDef mode fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00 ``` > [!TIP] > The **elephant-foot bulge** (Mode 2) is the *classical* local-buckling failure observed in CFST bridge piers after major earthquakes (e.g., 1995 Kobe, 2011 Tohoku). It propagates from the column base and is *governed* by the local-slenderness check derived above. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Differentiate between local buckling and global buckling of a structural member. State two design parameters that control local buckling.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Global Buckling | Local Buckling | |:--------|:---------------:|:--------------:| | Buckled shape | Whole member deflects laterally | Cross-section element (flange/web) buckles | | Wavelength | Comparable to member length | Comparable to plate width | | Effect of slenderness | Governed by $L/r$ | Governed by $b/t$ | | Bifurcation | Sharp, sudden | Gradual; post-buckling reserve exists | **Two design parameters controlling local buckling:** 1. **Width-to-thickness ratio ($b/t$)** of the plate element. 2. **Boundary conditions** of the plate edges (encoded via the buckling coefficient $k_\sigma$). > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Do *not* answer with $L/r$ — that controls *global* buckling. Examiners will *deduct 1 mark* for confusing the two. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary] **Define the term 'effective width' as used in the design of thin plate elements. State Winter's formula for the reduction factor $\rho$.** **Model Answer:** **Effective width** is the reduced width of a thin plate element, $b_{eff} \le b$, over which a uniform stress equal to the yield stress $f_y$ is assumed to act. The actual non-uniform post-buckling stress distribution is replaced by an equivalent uniform stress acting only on the supported-edge strips, with the buckled central region assumed ineffective. **Winter's formula for $\rho$:** $$ \rho \;=\; \dfrac{\bar{\lambda}_p - 0.22}{\bar{\lambda}_p^{2}} \qquad \text{for } \bar{\lambda}_p > 0.673 $$ and $\rho = 1.0$ for $\bar{\lambda}_p \le 0.673$, where $$ \bar{\lambda}_p \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr,L}}} $$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > *Examination rule (KTU 2024): Each Part-B question carries two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (full-module internal choice).* --- ### Question A — 14 Marks **A(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] A square steel tube of side $B = 200$ mm, wall thickness $t = 8$ mm, is filled with concrete of grade M30. The steel is of grade Fe410 ($f_y = 250$ MPa, $E_s = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$). Treating one face of the tube wall as a plate simply supported on three edges and free on the fourth (outstand), determine: 1. The critical elastic local buckling stress $\sigma_{cr,L}$. 2. The plate slenderness parameter $\bar{\lambda}_p$. 3. Classify the wall as compact, non-compact, or slender. **Step-by-step Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Identify geometry & $b/t$.* The flat wall has width $b = 200$ mm (the clear width between corner stiffeners) and $t = 8$ mm. $$ \dfrac{b}{t} \;=\; \dfrac{200}{8} \;=\; 25 $$ *Step 2 — Buckling coefficient.* For an outstand (one longitudinal edge free, other simply supported, both transverse edges simply supported): $$ k_{\sigma} \;=\; 0.425 $$ *Step 3 — Critical stress.* $$ \sigma_{cr,L} \;=\; \dfrac{0.425 \times \pi^{2} \times 200\,000}{12(1 - 0.30^{2})} \left(\dfrac{8}{200}\right)^{2} $$ Intermediate: $$ \dfrac{0.425 \times 9.8696 \times 200\,000}{12 \times 0.91} \;=\; \dfrac{838\,916}{10.92} \;=\; 76\,826 \; \text{MPa (per unit } (t/b)^2 \text{ factor)} $$ Multiplying by $(t/b)^2 = (0.04)^2 = 0.0016$: $$ \sigma_{cr,L} \;=\; 76\,826 \times 0.0016 \;=\; 122.92 \; \text{MPa} $$ *Step 4 — Slenderness parameter.* $$ \bar{\lambda}_p \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr,L}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{250}{122.92}} \;=\; \sqrt{2.034} \;=\; 1.426 $$ *Step 5 — Classification.* $\bar{\lambda}_p = 1.426 > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, so the wall is on the boundary of **Slender** (Class 3). If exactly equal to $\sqrt{2}$, it is at the Class 2/3 boundary; since it is marginally greater, it falls into **Class 3 (slender)**. **A(b) [7 Marks]** Using the result of part (a), compute: 1. Winter's reduction factor $\rho$. 2. The effective width $b_{eff}$ of the wall. 3. The post-buckling axial capacity of **one wall** (assume the wall carries load alone for simplicity). **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Compute $\rho$ (Winter's formula, since $\bar{\lambda}_p > 0.673$):* $$ \rho \;=\; \dfrac{1.426 - 0.22}{1.426^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.206}{2.034} \;=\; 0.593 $$ *Step 2 — Effective width:* $$ b_{eff} \;=\; 0.593 \times 200 \;=\; 118.6 \; \text{mm} $$ *Step 3 — Post-buckling capacity of one wall:* $$ P_{u,post} \;=\; f_y \cdot b_{eff} \cdot t \;=\; 250 \times 118.6 \times 8 \;=\; 237\,200 \; \text{N} \;=\; 237.2 \; \text{kN} $$ *Note:* This is the capacity of *one wall only* acting as an outstand plate. The full CFST column capacity is obtained by summing all four walls (with appropriate $k_\sigma$ for each) and adding the concrete contribution: $P_{n,total} = 4 \times 237.2 + 0.67 \times f_{ck} \times A_c \approx 948.8 + 0.67 \times 30 \times (184)^{2} \approx 948.8 + 681\,034 \approx 681\,983$ N ≈ 682 kN, with appropriate reduction factor $\phi = 0.75$ for the final design value. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **B(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Explain the concept of post-buckling strength in thin plate elements with a neat sketch of load–shortening behaviour. Why is the classical critical load $\sigma_{cr,L}$ not the failure load of a plate?** **Model Answer:** Post-buckling strength is the reserve load-carrying capacity a thin plate exhibits *after* the bifurcation point at $\sigma_{cr,L}$. As the plate deflects laterally under increasing compression, the deflected middle-surface stretches, generating **transverse membrane tensile stresses** $N_{xy}$ along the supported edges. These tensions act like a "fish-net" of in-plane restraints that stabilise the plate, allowing it to carry load well beyond $\sigma_{cr,L}$. The load–shortening curve is *stable* and gradually rising, with edge strips yielding first at $\sigma = f_y$, then progressively spreading inwards. Classical small-deflection theory, by assuming infinitesimal deflections, *ignores* the membrane action and incorrectly predicts collapse at $\sigma_{cr,L}$. The actual ultimate load is typically **3 to 7 times $\sigma_{cr,L}$** for slender plates — verified experimentally by **Foulkes (1913)**, **Leggett (1937)**, and the extensive **Cornell / Winter tests** (1940s). **Sketch (to be drawn in exam):** - $x$-axis: end-shortening $\delta$. - $y$-axis: average stress $\sigma$. - Curve: linear up to point A ($\sigma_{cr,L}$), then continues to rise with decreasing slope, reaches a plateau at $\sigma = f_y$ acting over effective width, and finally attains the **membrane-tension** ultimate at $\sigma \gg f_y$. **B(b) [7 Marks]** A rectangular CFST section has a steel plate of $b = 300$ mm, $t = 6$ mm, with edges simply supported on all four sides. Steel grade E300 ($f_y = 300$ MPa, $E_s = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$). Compute: 1. Critical local buckling stress. 2. Slenderness parameter $\bar{\lambda}_p$. 3. Effective width $b_{eff}$. **Step-by-step Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Buckling coefficient.* Simply supported on all four edges → $k_\sigma = 4.0$. *Step 2 — Critical stress.* $$ \sigma_{cr,L} \;=\; \dfrac{4.0 \times \pi^{2} \times 200\,000}{12(1 - 0.09)} \left(\dfrac{6}{300}\right)^{2} $$ Intermediate: $$ \dfrac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 200\,000}{12 \times 0.91} \;=\; \dfrac{7\,895\,744}{10.92} \;=\; 723\,054 \; \text{MPa (coefficient)} $$ Multiply by $(t/b)^2 = 0.02^2 = 0.0004$: $$ \sigma_{cr,L} \;=\; 723\,054 \times 0.0004 \;=\; 289.22 \; \text{MPa} $$ *Step 3 — Slenderness.* $$ \bar{\lambda}_p \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{300}{289.22}} \;=\; \sqrt{1.0374} \;=\; 1.0185 $$ *Step 4 — Reduction factor.* $$ \rho \;=\; \dfrac{1.0185 - 0.22}{1.0185^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.7985}{1.0374} \;=\; 0.7697 $$ *Step 5 — Effective width.* $$ b_{eff} \;=\; 0.7697 \times 300 \;=\; 230.9 \; \text{mm} $$ The plate loses about **23%** of its geometric width to local-buckling reduction. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always state the assumed edge condition** before quoting $k_\sigma$. The examiner expects "simply supported on all four sides, uniform compression → $k_\sigma = 4.0$" as a *first line* of your answer. Omitting it = **−1 mark**. > 2. **Use $\nu = 0.30$ for steel** (not 0.20 — that's for concrete). Mixing up Poisson's ratios is a *very common* KTU deduction. > 3. **Always show the unit chain** in numerical work. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. > 4. **For post-buckling questions, mention "membrane action"** — that is the *keyword* that scores the conceptual 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Local buckling** = cross-section element wrinkles; **Global buckling** = whole member deflects sideways. - [x] Critical stress formula: $\sigma_{cr,L} = \dfrac{k_\sigma \pi^2 E_s}{12(1-\nu^2)} (t/b)^2$ — **memorise this verbatim**. - [x] Buckling coefficient $k_\sigma$: **4.0** (SSSS), **0.425** (outstand), **6.97** (SCSC), **10.07** (CCCC). - [x] Plate slenderness $\bar{\lambda}_p = \sqrt{f_y / \sigma_{cr,L}}$ — threshold for effective width is **0.673**. - [x] Winter's reduction: $\rho = (\bar{\lambda}_p - 0.22)/\bar{\lambda}_p^{2}$ for $\bar{\lambda}_p > 0.673$; else $\rho = 1$. - [x] Effective width $b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$ — applied at supported edges. - [x] Post-buckling reserve arises from **transverse membrane tension** $N_{xy}$ — *not* from bending stiffness. - [x] Three classes: **Compact** ($\bar{\lambda}_p \le 0.673$), **Non-compact** ($0.673 < \bar{\lambda}_p \le \sqrt{2}$), **Slender** ($> \sqrt{2}$). - [x] For **CFST columns** under EC4: limiting $d/t \le 90(235/f_y)$ for circular; $b/t \le 52\sqrt{235/f_y}$ for square. - [x] **Elephant-foot bulge** is the typical local-buckling failure mode in CFST piers during earthquakes. - [x] Plates carry **3–7× the classical critical load** thanks to post-buckling reserve. - [x] Poisson's ratio for **steel = 0.30**; for **concrete = 0.20**. - [x] Always cite the **edge condition** before using $k_\sigma$ in your KTU exam answer. - [x] Remember the difference: **stiffener** prevents local buckling; **brace** prevents global buckling. - [x] Local buckling is *not* failure — it is the *start* of a stable post-buckling journey. > [!TIP] > **Final study mantra (mnemonic):** **"L — Little wrinkles; P — Post-buckle; P — Plenty of strength; W — Winter's Width"** — *LPPW*. Memorise this and you will not forget the four pillars of this topic.

topics.title: Design of Compression and Tension Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET631 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Compression and Tension Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Compression Members — Formal Definition

A **compression member** in a Reinforced Concrete (RC) structure is a structural element whose primary internal action is the transmission of **axial compressive force** along its longitudinal axis, generally accompanied by **bending moments** and **shear forces** due to continuity, frame action, eccentric loading, or lateral loads. As per **IS 456:2000, Clause 25.1.1**, compression members include columns, pedestals, and struts of a framed structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 456:2000 Definition (Clause 25.1.1):** *'A compression member is one whose effective length, divided by its least lateral dimension, exceeds 12; otherwise, it is termed a short column.'* This slenderness boundary is the **fundamental classifier** in KTU exams.

### Classification Based on Slenderness

| Category | Slenderness Ratio $\lambda = L_{ex}/D$ | Behaviour |
|----------|---------------------------------------|-----------|
| **Pedestal** | $\lambda \le 3$ (when $P_u \le 0.1 f_{ck} A_g$) | Designed as plain concrete, Cl. 25.4.1 |
| **Short Column** | $\lambda \le 12$ | Failure by **material crushing** (strength governed) |
| **Long Column** | $\lambda > 12$ | Failure by **buckling** (stability governed) |

## 1.2 Tension Members — Formal Definition

A **tension member** in RC is an element designed to resist **axial tensile forces**, generally as part of a tie, a chord in a truss-type action, or a counterfort tie beam. In RC practice, **plain concrete members cannot resist tension** (concrete tensile strength $f_{ct} \approx 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ is neglected in design), so tension members are always **fully reinforced** to carry the entire tensile force.

> [!NOTE]
> In typical KTU B.Tech civil engineering syllabus, "tension members" in concrete are often interpreted as **RC ties, hangers, or the tensile face reinforcement design** in columns subjected to combined axial tension and bending.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Intuitive Picture for a Compression Member:**

Imagine a **knee joint flexing under a heavy weight**. The shin bone (column) is being **squeezed from the top**. If the bone is short and stocky, it fails by *crushing* (concrete crushing or steel yielding). If the bone is long and slender, it *bows out sideways* before it crushes — like pressing a long plastic ruler on the desk; it buckles. The same governs column design.

**Intuitive Picture for a Tension Member:**

Imagine a **chain lifting a load**. The chain links (steel reinforcement) carry the entire pull. Concrete in tension is like a **brittle cracker** — it cracks at the first pull. So the steel must be sized to carry **100% of the tensile force**, with concrete acting only as a **protective cover** and crack-controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Constant Set Used in This Module:**
> - $f_{ck} = 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (Concrete grade)
> - $f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (Fe415) or $500 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (Fe500) for main bars
> - Cover to main reinforcement in column: **40 mm** (Cl. 26.4.2.1)
> - Minimum longitudinal bars in rectangular column: **4 numbers** (Cl. 25.5.1.1)
> - Minimum longitudinal bars in circular column: **6 numbers** (Cl. 25.5.1.1)
> - Minimum diameter of lateral ties: **1/4 of largest longitudinal bar** (Cl. 25.5.3.2)

## 1.4 Physical Significance of IS Code Provisions

The design philosophy of compression members rests on three principles:
1. **Equilibrium** — The axial load and moment capacities of the concrete and steel, integrated over the cross-section, must equal the applied loads.
2. **Deformation Compatibility** — The strain distribution must remain planar (Bernoulli's hypothesis) so the **parabola-rectangle stress block** (Cl. 38.1, Fig. 1) can be used.
3. **Ductility & Stability** — Lateral ties provide **confinement**, prevent **premature buckling of longitudinal bars**, and improve ductility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key IS Code Clauses to Memorise for KTU Exam (Module 4):**
> - Cl. 25.1.1 — Definition of compression member
> - Cl. 25.1.2 — Effective length calculation
> - Cl. 25.3 — Minimum eccentricity
> - Cl. 25.4 — Design of pedestals
> - Cl. 25.5 — Design of columns (short & long)
> - Cl. 39.3 — Additional Moment Method (long columns)
> - Cl. 25.5.1 — Longitudinal reinforcement limits
> - Cl. 25.5.3 — Transverse reinforcement (ties)
> - SP-16 Charts — Interaction diagrams for column design
