# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effective Length ($L_e$)

The effective length of a compression member is the length between **points of contraflexure** (zero moment) in the buckled shape. IS 456:2000, **Cl. 25.1.2 & Table 25 (Figs. 26, 27, 28)** provide values based on end conditions:

| End Conditions (in plane) | Symbol | $L_{ex}/L$ (Effective length ratio) |
|---------------------------|:------:|:-----------------------------------:|
| Effectively held in position and restrained against rotation at both ends | $\psi = 0$ (Fixed-Fixed) | **0.65 L** |
| Effectively held in position and restrained against rotation at one end, and at the other end restrained against rotation but not held in position | $\psi = 0$ (one end fixed, other pinned — sway) | **0.80 L** |
| Effectively held in position at both ends, and restrained against rotation at one end | (One end fixed, one end free — sway) | **0.80 L** |
| Effectively held in position and restrained against rotation at one end, and at the other end neither held in position nor restrained against rotation | (Pinned-Pinned) | **1.00 L** |
| Held in position and restrained against rotation at one end, and at the other end restrained against rotation but not held in position (no sway) | (Fixed-Pinned no sway) | **0.70 L** |
| Held in position but not restrained against rotation at both ends (sway) | (Sway frame) | **1.20 L** |

For most KTU problems, $L_{ex} = 0.65 L$ (both ends fixed, non-sway) is the most common assumption for braced frames.

## 2.2 Minimum Eccentricity (Cl. 25.3 — VERY HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.3:** *A compression member shall be designed for the minimum eccentricity equal to the unsupported length of the member divided by 500 plus lateral dimension/30, subject to a minimum of 20 mm. Where, however, the resultant of the load falls beyond the core of the section, the design shall be based on actual eccentricity.*

$$e_{x, \min} = \max \left( \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30}, \; 20 \text{ mm} \right)$$

$$e_{y, \min} = \max \left( \frac{L}{500} + \frac{b}{30}, \; 20 \text{ mm} \right)$$

**Why minimum eccentricity?** Because **all columns in practice have some accidental eccentricity** due to construction tolerances, uneven load transfer, and out-of-plumb erection. The IS code enforces this to ensure a non-zero moment design even for "axially loaded" columns.

## 2.3 Short Column Design — Axial Load Only

For a **short column** ($\lambda \le 12$) carrying a concentric load $P_u$, the maximum allowable axial load is governed by **Cl. 25.5.1 (Eq. 26)**:

$$P_{u, \max} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$$

where:
- $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$ = net concrete area
- $A_{sc}$ = area of longitudinal steel
- $A_g$ = gross cross-sectional area

> [!CAUTION]
> The factor **0.45 $f_{ck}$** is the *short-term allowable* on concrete to prevent brittle failure, while the factor **0.75 $f_y$** keeps the steel within elastic shakedown. The **combined 0.45 + 0.75** ensures steel yields before concrete crushes — providing **ductile failure mode**.

## 2.4 Short Column Design — Axial Load + Uniaxial Bending

For a column subjected to **axial load $P_u$** and **uniaxial moment $M_{ux}$** (or equivalently, axial load with eccentricity $e = M_u/P_u$), IS 456 recommends the use of **SP-16 Design Charts** (interaction diagrams) for Fe415 and Fe500, for various $d'/D$ ratios (typically 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, etc.).

**Analytical Form (Eq. based, Cl. 38.1, IS 456, Annex G — Optional, but used in KTU derivations):**

$$P_u = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc} - \text{Moment reduction term}$$

The SP-16 chart gives two non-dimensional parameters:

$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D} \quad \text{(non-dimensional axial load)} \quad \text{vs} \quad \frac{M_u}{f_{ck} b D^2} \quad \text{(non-dimensional moment)}$$

For a given $p/f_{ck}$ ratio (reinforcement index), the chart directly yields the design values.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Design of Compression and Tension Members**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Code Reference | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = L_{ex} / D$ | Cl. 25.1.1 | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Min Eccentricity (x-axis) | $e_{x,\min} = \max(L/500 + D/30, \; 20)$ | Cl. 25.3 | mm |
| 3 | Min Eccentricity (y-axis) | $e_{y,\min} = \max(L/500 + b/30, \; 20)$ | Cl. 3 | mm |
| 4 | Pure Axial Capacity (Short) | $P_{u,\max} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$ | Cl. 25.5.1 | N |
| 5 | Min Longitudinal Steel | $A_{sc, \min} = 0.008 A_g$ | Cl. 25.5.1.1 | mm² |
| 6 | Max Longitudinal Steel | $A_{sc, \max} = 0.04 A_g$ | Cl. 25.5.1.1 | mm² |
| 7 | Min Bar Diameter (Rectangular) | 4 nos. for rect, 6 nos. for circular | Cl. 25.5.1.1 | numbers |
| 8 | Min Bar Diameter | $\ge 12$ mm | Cl. 25.5.1.1 | mm |
| 9 | Tie Diameter | $\phi_{tie} \ge \phi_{main}/4$ | Cl. 25.5.3.2 | mm |
| 10 | Tie Spacing | $s_{tie} \le \min(L_c, \; 16\phi_{main}, \; 300 \text{ mm})$ | Cl. 25.5.3.2 | mm |
| 11 | Long Column Additional Moment | $M_{ax} = \dfrac{P_u D}{2000} \cdot \dfrac{l_{ex}}{D}$ (or use $C_m$ factor) | Cl. 39.3 | N·mm |
| 12 | Tension Member Capacity | $P_{ut} = 0.87 f_y A_{st}$ (tensile capacity of steel) | Cl. 26.2.1 | N |
| 13 | Max Bending Capacity (with axial tension) | $M_{u,\max}$ from SP-16, $P_u$ from 26.2.1 | SP-16 | N·mm |
| 14 | Biaxial Bending Check | $\left( \dfrac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}} \right)^{\alpha_n} + \left( \dfrac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}} \right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1.0$ | Cl. 25.5.3, IS 456, G-2.4 (Eq. 67) | — |
| 15 | $\alpha_n$ coefficient for biaxial bending | $\alpha_n = 0.7 + \dfrac{1.7 P_u}{0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}}$ (limited 0.5 to 1.0) | Cl. 25.5.3 (IS 318 or G-2.4) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Compression Members:** Foundation columns, building pillars, bridge piers, pile caps, shear walls.
> - **Tension Members:** RC ties in arched structures, hangers in transfer girders, tie beams in combined footings, horizontal tension chords in framed tube buildings.

## 2.6 Longitudinal Reinforcement Limits — Reasoning

**Lower limit (0.8% of $A_g$):**
Prevents brittle tension failure and provides crack control. Without minimum steel, the section would behave essentially like plain concrete — undesirable for seismic zones.

**Upper limit (4% of $A_g$):**
Prevents over-reinforcement (which would shift failure to brittle concrete crushing), keeps the section practical to construct, and controls congestion at laps.

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Using $A_{sc} = 0.04 A_g$ as a hard maximum — actually, for **lap splices in columns**, IS 456 limits steel to **0.04 $A_g$**, but in the lap zone, **only 50% bars can be lapped** at one section, OR equivalent spirals must be used. Many students miss this.

## 2.7 Lateral Ties — Confinement Mechanics

Lateral ties serve three critical functions:
1. **Prevent buckling of longitudinal bars** under sustained compression.
2. **Provide confinement** to the core concrete, increasing its effective strength and ductility.
3. **Resist lateral shear** in the column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spacing rule (Cl. 25.5.3.2):** $s \le \min(L_{c, \text{least lateral dimension}}, \; 16\phi_{\text{main}}, \; 300 \text{ mm})$
>
> **For seismic / ductile detailing (IS 13920):** $s \le 100$ mm, with $135°$ hooks (this is advanced and may not be in PECET631).

## 2.8 Long Column Design (Additional Moment Method, Cl. 39.3)

Long columns must be designed as **short columns subject to an additional moment $M_{ax}$** that accounts for slenderness-induced deflection:

$$M_{ax} = \frac{P_u D}{2000} \cdot \left( \frac{l_{ex}}{D} \right)^2$$

But this is replaced by the **more accurate Cl. 39.3 equation** when the slenderness ratio exceeds 12:

For **braced columns** (no sway):

$$M_{ax} = C_m \cdot M_2 \quad \text{where} \quad C_m = 0.6 + 0.4 \frac{M_1}{M_2} \ge 0.4$$

For **unbraced columns** (sway frames):

$$M_{ax} = M_2 \quad \text{(full design moment)}$$

The total design moment for the long column:

$$M_{u,\text{design}} = M_{ax} + M_t$$

where $M_t$ is the maximum computed first-order moment from frame analysis.

The column is then designed as a short column with $P_u$ and $M_{u,\text{design}}$ using the SP-16 interaction chart.

## 2.9 Biaxial Bending in Columns

When load eccentricity exists in **both principal directions**, the column is subjected to biaxial bending. The **interaction equation (IS 318 / IS 456 G-2.4, Eq. 67)** must be satisfied:

$$\left( \frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}} \right)^{\alpha_n} + \left( \frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}} \right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1.0$$

where:
- $M_{ux}, M_{uy}$ = moments about x and y axes
- $M_{ux1}, M_{uy1}$ = uniaxial moment capacities about x and y axes (from SP-16)
- $\alpha_n$ = exponent depending on axial load level

$$\alpha_n = 0.7 + 1.7 \left( \frac{P_u}{P_{uz}} \right) \quad ; \quad 0.5 \le \alpha_n \le 1.0$$

where $P_{uz} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$ is the **squash load** (pure axial capacity).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pure Axial Capacity (Cl. 25.5.1, Eq. 26)

**Starting Point:** The column is subjected to concentric axial load $P_u$ at limit state.

**Equilibrium:** The internal forces in concrete (uniform stress $0.45 f_{ck}$) and steel (uniform stress $0.75 f_y$) must equal the applied load $P_u$:

$$P_u = P_{c} + P_{s}$$

where $P_c$ is the force in concrete and $P_s$ is the force in steel.

**Step 1 — Force in concrete:**

The concrete area resisting the load is the **gross area minus steel area** (since steel has displaced some concrete):

$$A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$$

Concrete force:

$$P_c = 0.45 f_{ck} \cdot A_c = 0.45 f_{ck} (A_g - A_{sc})$$

**Step 2 — Force in steel:**

The steel is in compression. IS 456 limits its design stress to $0.75 f_y$:

$$P_s = 0.75 f_y \cdot A_{sc}$$

**Step 3 — Summation:**

$$P_u = 0.45 f_{ck} (A_g - A_{sc}) + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$$

**Step 4 — Final form (Eq. 26):**

$$\boxed{P_{u,\max} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}}$$

**Numerical Verification Example:** Let $A_g = 300 \times 300 = 90000$ mm², $A_{sc} = 0.02 \times 90000 = 1800$ mm², $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm².

$$A_c = 90000 - 1800 = 88200 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$P_{u,\max} = (0.45)(25)(88200) + (0.75)(415)(1800)$$

$$= 992250 + 560250 = 1552500 \text{ N} \approx 1552.5 \text{ kN}$$

This is the maximum concentric axial capacity.

## 3.2 Derivation of Minimum Eccentricity Formula (Cl. 25.3)

**Background:** Real columns always have some un-intended eccentricity due to:
- Construction tolerances (out-of-plumb) → eccentricity $\approx L/500$ rad
- Floor-to-floor load transfer deviations → eccentricity $\approx D/30$

**Step 1:** IS 456 conservatively adds these two contributions:

$$e_{\min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30}$$

**Step 2:** IS 456 enforces an **absolute lower bound** of 20 mm to cover the case of very short, very thin columns where the formula yields less than 20 mm.

**Step 3:** Final form:

$$\boxed{e_{x, \min} = \max\left( \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30}, \; 20 \text{ mm} \right)}$$

**Verification Example:** Column 3 m long, 300 mm deep.

$$e_{\min} = \frac{3000}{500} + \frac{300}{30} = 6 + 10 = 16 \text{ mm}$$

Since $16 < 20$, IS 456 demands we use:

$$e_{\min} = 20 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(absolute floor applied)}$$

This is a common KTU trick — students calculate 16 mm and **lose 1 mark** for not applying the floor.

## 3.3 Derivation of Additional Moment for Long Columns (Cl. 39.3)

**Physical Basis:** A slender column under axial load $P_u$ deflects laterally by an amount $\Delta$. The deflection creates an additional moment $P_u \cdot \Delta$, which increases the deflection further — a classical second-order ($P$-$\Delta$) effect.

**Step 1 — Elastic buckled shape assumption:**

For a pin-ended column of length $L$ buckled in a sine shape, the maximum lateral deflection is:

$$\Delta = \delta \cdot \left( \frac{1}{1 - P_u/P_{cr}} \right)$$

where $\delta$ is the first-order deflection and $P_{cr}$ is the Euler buckling load.

**Step 2 — Euler critical load:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

For braced columns, after simplification, the **Code-prescribed additional moment** becomes:

$$M_{ax} = \frac{P_u D}{2000} \left( \frac{l_{ex}}{D} \right)^2$$

**Step 4 — More accurate form (CSA-Sized code):**

For a **braced column** with end moments $M_1, M_2$ (with $M_2 > M_1$):

$$M_{ax} = C_m \cdot M_2 \ge 0.4 M_2$$

$$C_m = 0.6 + 0.4 \cdot \frac{M_1}{M_2}$$

**Step 5 — Final design moment:**

$$\boxed{M_{u, \text{total}} = M_{ax} + M_{t,\max}}$$

where $M_{t,\max}$ is the maximum first-order moment from frame analysis (manual or software). The column is then designed as a short column using this enlarged moment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The 14-mark Part B questions in Module 4 frequently ask: *"Design a column of size $B \times D$, M25 concrete, Fe415 steel, subjected to $P_u$ and $M_u$, with effective length $L_{ex}$. Consider slenderness effects."* The full procedure requires computing $M_{ax}$ and using the enlarged moment in SP-16.

## 3.4 Derivation of Biaxial Bending Equation (IS 318, Eq. 67)

**Background:** Under biaxial loading, the column has eccentricities in both directions. The uniaxial moment capacity in each direction is computed (from SP-16 with $M_{uy}=0$ and $M_{ux}=0$ respectively). The check equation has the form:

$$F\left( \frac{P_u}{P_{uz}}, \frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}}, \frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}} \right) \le 1.0$$

**Step 1 — Bresler's Reciprocal Load Equation (1960s):**

$$\frac{1}{P_{ui}} = \frac{1}{P_{uox}} + \frac{1}{P_{uoy}} - \frac{1}{P_{uo}}$$

where $P_{ui}$ is the biaxial capacity, $P_{uox}$ and $P_{uoy}$ are uniaxial capacities (about x and y), and $P_{uo}$ is the pure axial capacity.

**Step 2 — IS Code simplified form:**

The IS code uses the **exponential interaction form** (more accurate and code-prescribed):

$$\boxed{\left( \frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}} \right)^{\alpha_n} + \left( \frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}} \right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1.0}$$

**Step 3 — Determination of $\alpha_n$:**

$$\alpha_n = 0.7 + 1.7 \frac{P_u}{P_{uz}}$$

with bounds:

$$0.5 \le \alpha_n \le 1.0$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation of $\alpha_n$:**

- If $P_u / P_{uz} \to 0$ (pure bending) → $\alpha_n = 0.7$
- If $P_u / P_{uz} \to 0.176$ (intermediate) → $\alpha_n = 1.0$
- If $P_u / P_{uz}$ is high → $\alpha_n$ capped at 1.0 (linear interaction)

**Numerical Example:** $P_u = 1000$ kN, $P_{uz} = 2000$ kN → $P_u/P_{uz} = 0.5$ → $\alpha_n = 0.7 + 1.7(0.5) = 1.55$, but **capped at 1.0**.

Therefore $\alpha_n = 1.0$, and the equation becomes **linear**:

$$\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}} + \frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}} \le 1.0$$

## 3.5 Complete Step-by-Step Design Procedure (Short Column with Uniaxial Bending)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the KTU-recommended Part-B (14 marks) workflow. Memorise this sequence.**

**Given:** Column size $B \times D$, $f_{ck}$, $f_y$, $P_u$, $M_{ux}$, $L_{ex}$, $L_{ey}$, $L$ (unsupported length), unsupported length of column.

**Step 1: Compute Minimum Eccentricity**

$$e_{x, \min} = \max\left( \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30}, \; 20 \text{ mm} \right)$$

**Step 2: Compare with Actual Eccentricity**

$$e_x = \frac{M_{ux}}{P_u}$$

If $e_x \ge e_{x, \min}$, design with actual $e_x$. If $e_x < e_{x, \min}$, design with $e_{x, \min}$ to be on the safe side.

**Step 3: Compute Slenderness Ratios**

$$\lambda_x = \frac{L_{ex}}{D}, \quad \lambda_y = \frac{L_{ey}}{B}$$

**Step 4: Classify Column**

- If $\max(\lambda_x, \lambda_y) \le 12$ → **Short Column** (no additional moment)
- Else → **Long Column** (compute $M_{ax}$ and design as short with enlarged moment)

**Step 5: Compute Non-Dimensional Load and Moment**

For a chosen reinforcement (e.g., 4 bars of 20 mm $\phi$ + 4 bars of 16 mm $\phi$ distributed equally on opposite faces), compute $p = A_{sc}/A_g$:

$$\frac{p}{f_{ck}} = \frac{A_{sc}}{A_g \cdot f_{ck}}$$

**Step 6: Enter SP-16 Chart**

Enter the SP-16 design chart for the appropriate $d'/D$ ratio. Read off:

$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{M_u}{f_{ck} b D^2}$$

**Step 7: Verify the section can sustain the load**

If the chart point lies **inside the curve** for the assumed reinforcement, the section is safe. Else, increase reinforcement (re-enter chart with new $p/f_{ck}$).

**Step 8: Check Longitudinal Steel Limits**

$$0.008 A_g \le A_{sc} \le 0.04 A_g$$

**Step 9: Design Lateral Ties**

$$\phi_{tie} \ge \frac{\phi_{main, \max}}{4}$$

$$s_{tie} \le \min(B, D, 16 \phi_{main}, 300 \text{ mm})$$

**Step 10: Draw Sectional Detailing and Reinforcement Plan**

Show:
- Section plan with bar layout
- Cover to main steel: 40 mm
- Tie arrangement with $90°/135°$ hooks

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**State the IS 456 provisions for minimum eccentricity in column design. Why is minimum eccentricity specified?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> As per **IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.3**, the minimum eccentricity in a column design shall not be less than:
>
> $$e_{x,\min} = \max\left( \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30}, \; 20 \text{ mm} \right)$$
>
> Minimum eccentricity is specified because, in practice, all columns experience some **unintended eccentricity** arising from:
> 1. Construction inaccuracies and out-of-plumb erection,
> 2. Asymmetric load transfer from beams/slabs,
> 3. Lateral forces (wind/seismic) at any instant.
>
> The code mandates this to ensure a non-zero moment design for nominally "axially loaded" columns, preventing an **unrealistic zero-moment pure axial design** that could lead to brittle failure.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a pedestal and a column as per IS 456:2000.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pedestal | Column |
|---------|----------|--------|
| **Definition** | Compression member with effective length ≤ 3 times least lateral dimension, where $P_u \le 0.1 f_{ck} A_g$ (Cl. 25.4.1) | Compression member with $L_{ex}/D > 3$ or $P_u > 0.1 f_{ck} A_g$ |
| **Reinforcement** | Designed as plain concrete; light nominal steel may be provided | Must be reinforced per Cl. 25.5 |
| **Design Philosophy** | Bearing on concrete (no specific reinforcement calculation required) | Designed for combined axial load and bending |
| **Slenderness** | $\lambda \le 3$ | $\lambda > 3$ |
| **Failure Mode** | Concrete bearing | Material failure (short) or buckling (long) |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 20 mm floor on minimum eccentricity** costs 1 mark — always apply the max() function.
> 2. **Not checking slenderness in both directions** ($\lambda_x$ and $\lambda_y$) costs 1-2 marks. Long-column check depends on **max** slenderness.
> 3. **Wrong SP-16 chart** (wrong $d'/D$ or wrong $f_y$) leads to wrong design — verify cover and material first.
> 4. **Forgetting tie spacing rule** (must be ≤ least of $B, D, 16\phi, 300$ mm) is a common omission.
> 5. **Pedestal vs. column confusion** — many students apply column design to pedestals.
> 6. **Forgetting to check $A_{sc}$ in $[0.008 A_g, 0.04 A_g]$** — this is mandatory.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]**

A reinforced concrete short column of size **400 mm × 500 mm** is subjected to a **factored axial load $P_u = 1500$ kN** and **factored moment $M_{ux} = 200$ kN·m** about the major axis. The unsupported length of the column is **3.5 m**, with both ends fixed (non-sway frame). The materials are **M25 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**. Design the column using SP-16 charts. Take cover = 40 mm, $d' = 50$ mm.

**Sub-parts:**

**(a)** Compute the design forces, slenderness ratio, minimum eccentricity, and classify the column. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design the longitudinal and lateral reinforcement. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution A(a): 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$P_u = 1500 \text{ kN}, \quad M_{ux} = 200 \text{ kN·m}, \quad B \times D = 400 \times 500 \text{ mm}$$

$$f_{ck} = 25 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad L = 3.5 \text{ m} = 3500 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Length (Fixed-Fixed, Non-Sway):**

$$L_{ex} = 0.65 L = 0.65 \times 3.5 = 2.275 \text{ m} = 2275 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Slenderness Ratio:**

$$\lambda_x = \frac{L_{ex}}{D} = \frac{2275}{500} = 4.55$$

Since $\lambda_x = 4.55 < 12$, **the column is SHORT** — no additional moment required.

**Step 4 — Actual Eccentricity:**

$$e_x = \frac{M_{ux}}{P_u} = \frac{200 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{1500 \times 10^3 \text{ N}} = 133.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum Eccentricity (Cl. 25.3):**

$$e_{x, \min} = \max\left( \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30}, \; 20 \text{ mm} \right)$$

$$= \max\left( \frac{3500}{500} + \frac{500}{30}, \; 20 \right)$$

$$= \max(7 + 16.67, \; 20) = \max(23.67, \; 20) = 23.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Since $e_x = 133.33$ mm $> e_{x, \min} = 23.67$ mm**, use the actual eccentricity $e_x = 133.33$ mm.

**Step 6 — Compute $M_{ux}$ and $P_u$ values for SP-16:**

The moments act about the **major axis** (depth $D = 500$ mm). Bending occurs about the axis perpendicular to the 500 mm dimension.

**Step 7 — Non-dimensional parameters (needed for SP-16 chart):**

$$f_{ck} b D = 25 \times 400 \times 500 = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 5000 \text{ kN}$$

$$f_{ck} b D^2 = 25 \times 400 \times 500^2 = 2.5 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm} = 2500 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D} = \frac{1500}{5000} = 0.30$$

$$\frac{M_{ux}}{f_{ck} b D^2} = \frac{200}{2500} = 0.08$$

**Step 8 — Choose SP-16 chart for Fe415, $d'/D = 50/500 = 0.10$:**

Enter the chart with these two non-dimensional values. Read off the required reinforcement index:

$$\frac{p}{f_{ck}} \approx 0.10 \Rightarrow p = 0.10 \times 25 = 2.5\%$$

$$A_{sc} = 0.025 \times 400 \times 500 = 0.025 \times 200000 = 5000 \text{ mm}^2$$

---

#### **Model Solution A(b): 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Verify Steel Limits:**

$$A_{sc, \min} = 0.008 \times 200000 = 1600 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{sc, \max} = 0.04 \times 200000 = 8000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$1600 \le 5000 \le 8000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Bar Selection:**

Provide 4 bars of 25 mm $\phi$ + 4 bars of 20 mm $\phi$:

- 4 × 25 mm $\phi$: $4 \times 491 = 1964$ mm²
- 4 × 20 mm $\phi$: $4 \times 314 = 1256$ mm²
- **Total** = $1964 + 1256 = 3220$ mm²

This is less than required 5000 mm², so increase to 8 bars of 25 mm $\phi$ + 2 bars of 16 mm $\phi$ (corner-bar reinforcement).

- 8 × 25 mm $\phi$: $8 \times 491 = 3928$ mm²
- 2 × 16 mm $\phi$: $2 \times 201 = 402$ mm²
- **Total** = $3928 + 402 = 4330$ mm²

Re-enter SP-16 chart with refined $p = 4330/200000 = 2.165\%$, confirm safe.

Alternatively, adopt **8 bars of 25 mm $\phi$** uniformly distributed:

$$A_{sc} = 8 \times 491 = 3928 \text{ mm}^2, \quad p = 3928/200000 = 1.96\%$$

Re-check SP-16 — if safe, adopt this arrangement.

**Step 3 — Lateral Tie Design (Cl. 25.5.3.2):**

Main bar diameter = 25 mm.

Tie diameter:

$$\phi_{tie} \ge \frac{\phi_{main}}{4} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \phi_{tie} = 8 \text{ mm}$$

Tie spacing:

$$s_{tie} \le \min(B, D, 16\phi_{main}, 300)$$

$$= \min(400, 500, 16 \times 25, 300)$$

$$= \min(400, 500, 400, 300) = 300 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt: 8 mm $\phi$ ties @ 300 mm c/c.**

**Step 4 — Detailing Summary:**

- **Section plan:** 400 × 500 mm, cover 40 mm
- **Main reinforcement:** 8 nos. 25 mm $\phi$ (4 at corners + 2 on each long face)
- **Ties:** 8 mm $\phi$ @ 300 mm c/c
- **Cover:** 40 mm to main steel

**Step 5 — Drawing:**

Sketch showing:
- Section: 400 × 500 mm rectangle
- 4 corner bars 25 mm $\phi$
- 2 mid-bars on each 500 mm face
- Ties at 300 mm c/c
- Cover shown as 40 mm dimension

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

An RC column of size **300 mm × 450 mm** carries a **factored axial load $P_u = 1200$ kN**, and **biaxial moments $M_{ux} = 120$ kN·m and $M_{uy} = 80$ kN·m** about the major and minor axes respectively. Effective length in both directions is **3.0 m**. Materials: M25 concrete, Fe415 steel, $d' = 50$ mm. Check the adequacy of providing **8 bars of 20 mm $\phi$** as longitudinal reinforcement. Design the lateral ties.

**Sub-parts:**

**(a)** Compute the slenderness, eccentricities, and uniaxial moment capacities. (7 marks)

**(b)** Check biaxial bending adequacy using the IS 456 interaction equation. Design the ties. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution B(a): 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$B \times D = 300 \times 450 \text{ mm}, \quad L_{ex} = L_{ey} = 3.0 \text{ m} = 3000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Slenderness Ratios:**

$$\lambda_x = \frac{L_{ex}}{D} = \frac{3000}{450} = 6.67 < 12 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\lambda_y = \frac{L_{ey}}{B} = \frac{3000}{300} = 10 < 12 \quad \checkmark$$

**Column is SHORT — no additional moment required.**

**Step 3 — Eccentricities:**

$$e_x = \frac{M_{ux}}{P_u} = \frac{120 \times 10^6}{1200 \times 10^3} = 100 \text{ mm}$$

$$e_y = \frac{M_{uy}}{P_u} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{1200 \times 10^3} = 66.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Eccentricity (Cl. 25.3):**

$$e_{x, \min} = \max\left(\frac{3000}{500} + \frac{450}{30}, \; 20\right) = \max(6 + 15, 20) = 21 \text{ mm}$$

$$e_{y, \min} = \max\left(\frac{3000}{500} + \frac{300}{30}, \; 20\right) = \max(6 + 10, 20) = 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Both actual eccentricities exceed the minimums** — proceed with actual values.

**Step 5 — Compute Steel Area Provided:**

$$A_{sc, \text{provided}} = 8 \times \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} = 8 \times 314.16 = 2513 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$p = \frac{A_{sc}}{A_g} = \frac{2513}{300 \times 450} = \frac{2513}{135000} = 1.86\%$$

**Check limits:**

$$A_{sc, \min} = 0.008 \times 135000 = 1080 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

$$A_{sc, \max} = 0.04 \times 135000 = 5400 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Compute Pure Axial Capacity (Squash Load):**

$$A_c = A_g - A_{sc} = 135000 - 2513 = 132487 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$P_{uz} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$$

$$= 0.45 \times 25 \times 132487 + 0.75 \times 415 \times 2513$$

$$= 1490479 + 782299 = 2272778 \text{ N} = 2272.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Uniaxial Moment Capacity from SP-16:**

For SP-16 chart, $d'/D = 50/450 = 0.111$ (use 0.10 chart), $f_y = 415$:

With $p = 1.86\%$, $p/f_{ck} = 1.86/25 = 0.0744$ (read off chart).

**Uniaxial capacity about x-axis (bending about major axis, $D = 450$):**

Compute non-dimensional load:

$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D} = \frac{1200 \times 10^3}{25 \times 300 \times 450} = \frac{1200000}{3375000} = 0.356$$

From SP-16 (Fe415, $d'/D = 0.10$, $p/f_{ck} = 0.0744$):

$$\frac{M_{ux1}}{f_{ck} b D^2} \approx 0.14$$

$$M_{ux1} = 0.14 \times 25 \times 300 \times 450^2 = 212.6 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 212.6 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Uniaxial capacity about y-axis (bending about minor axis, $B = 300$):**

By symmetry of SP-16 charts (with $d'/B = 50/300 = 0.167$ — use 0.15 chart):

$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} D b} = \frac{1200 \times 10^3}{25 \times 450 \times 300} = 0.356$$

From SP-16 (Fe415, $d'/B = 0.15$):

$$\frac{M_{uy1}}{f_{ck} b^2 D} \approx 0.16$$

$$M_{uy1} = 0.16 \times 25 \times 300^2 \times 450 = 162 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 162 \text{ kN·m}$$

---

#### **Model Solution B(b): 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha_n$:**

$$\alpha_n = 0.7 + 1.7 \cdot \frac{P_u}{P_{uz}}$$

$$= 0.7 + 1.7 \times \frac{1200}{2272.8} = 0.7 + 1.7 \times 0.528 = 0.7 + 0.898 = 1.598$$

But $0.5 \le \alpha_n \le 1.0$, so **$\alpha_n = 1.0$** (cap applied).

**Step 2 — Biaxial Bending Check (IS 456 / IS 318, Eq. 67):**

$$\left( \frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}} \right)^{\alpha_n} + \left( \frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}} \right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1.0$$

With $\alpha_n = 1.0$:

$$\left( \frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}} \right)^{1.0} + \left( \frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}} \right)^{1.0} = \frac{120}{212.6} + \frac{80}{162} = 0.564 + 0.494 = 1.058$$

**Result: 1.058 > 1.0 — Section is NOT adequate.**

**Step 3 — Options to Fix:**

**Option 1:** Increase steel. Try 8 bars of 22 mm $\phi$:

$$A_{sc} = 8 \times 380 = 3040 \text{ mm}^2, \quad p = 3040/135000 = 2.25\%$$

Re-enter SP-16 — new $M_{ux1}$ and $M_{uy1}$ will be larger, possibly making the check pass.

**Option 2:** Increase column size to 350 × 500 mm or 400 × 500 mm.

**Option 3:** Use higher concrete grade (M30 or M35).

**For this solution, adopt Option 1 with 8 bars of 22 mm $\phi$:**

$$p = 2.25\% \Rightarrow \frac{p}{f_{ck}} = \frac{2.25}{25} = 0.09$$

From SP-16 chart (Fe415, $d'/D = 0.10$, $p/f_{ck} = 0.09$):

$$M_{ux1, \text{new}} \approx 235 \text{ kN·m}, \quad M_{uy1, \text{new}} \approx 180 \text{ kN·m}$$

Recheck:

$$\frac{120}{235} + \frac{80}{180} = 0.511 + 0.444 = 0.955 < 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Section is now adequate.**

**Step 4 — Lateral Tie Design:**

Main bar diameter = 22 mm.

Tie diameter:

$$\phi_{tie} \ge \frac{22}{4} = 5.5 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \phi_{tie} = 8 \text{ mm}$$

Tie spacing:

$$s_{tie} \le \min(B, D, 16\phi_{main}, 300)$$

$$= \min(300, 450, 16 \times 22, 300) = \min(300, 450, 352, 300) = 300 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt: 8 mm $\phi$ ties @ 300 mm c/c.**

**Step 5 — Final Detailing:**

- **Section:** 300 × 450 mm
- **Main steel:** 8 bars of 22 mm $\phi$ (4 corners + 2 on each long face)
- **Ties:** 8 mm $\phi$ @ 300 mm c/c with 135° hooks at alternate ends (good practice)
- **Cover:** 40 mm

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 4: Design of Compression and Tension Members**

### ⭐ A. Definitions and Classification
- **Compression Member** = transmits axial compressive force (Cl. 25.1.1)
- **Pedestal:** $\lambda \le 3$ and $P_u \le 0.1 f_{ck} A_g$ — designed as plain concrete
- **Short Column:** $\lambda \le 12$ — material strength governs
- **Long Column:** $\lambda > 12$ — stability governs; additional moment via Cl. 39.3

### ⭐ B. Effective Length ($L_e$)
- **Fixed-Fixed (non-sway):** $L_e = 0.65 L$
- **Fixed-Pinned (non-sway):** $L_e = 0.70 L$
- **Pinned-Pinned:** $L_e = 1.00 L$
- **Sway frame:** $L_e = 1.20 L$

### ⭐ C. Minimum Eccentricity (Cl. 25.3)
- $e_{x,\min} = \max(L/500 + D/30, \; 20 \text{ mm})$
- $e_{y,\min} = \max(L/500 + b/30, \; 20 \text{ mm})$
- **Always check actual vs. minimum — use larger value!**

### ⭐ D. Pure Axial Capacity (Cl. 25.5.1, Eq. 26)
- $P_{u,\max} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$

### ⭐ E. Longitudinal Steel (Cl. 25.5.1.1)
- $A_{sc, \min} = 0.008 A_g$ (0.8%)
- $A_{sc, \max} = 0.04 A_g$ (4%)
- Min 4 bars (rectangular) or 6 bars (circular), dia ≥ 12 mm

### ⭐ F. Lateral Ties (Cl. 25.5.3.2)
- $\phi_{tie} \ge \phi_{main}/4$ (use 8 mm minimum in practice)
- $s_{tie} \le \min(B, D, 16\phi_{main}, 300 \text{ mm})$

### ⭐ G. Long Column — Additional Moment (Cl. 39.3)
- **Braced:** $M_{ax} = C_m M_2$, $C_m = 0.6 + 0.4 (M_1/M_2) \ge 0.4$
- **Unbraced:** $M_{ax} = M_2$
- **Total:** $M_{u,\text{total}} = M_{ax} + M_{t,\max}$ → design as short column

### ⭐ H. Biaxial Bending (Eq. 67, IS 456 / IS 318)
- $\left( M_{ux}/M_{ux1} \right)^{\alpha_n} + \left( M_{uy}/M_{uy1} \right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1.0$
- $\alpha_n = 0.7 + 1.7(P_u/P_{uz})$; bounds: $0.5 \le \alpha_n \le 1.0$

### ⭐ I. Tension Members
- Steel carries entire tension: $A_{st} = T_u / (0.87 f_y)$
- Concrete acts as cover/protection only
- Provide lateral ties for stability

### ⭐ J. Common KTU Pitfalls (HIGH-YIELD)
- ❌ Forgetting the 20 mm floor on minimum eccentricity
- ❌ Checking slenderness in only one direction
- ❌ Selecting the wrong SP-16 chart (wrong $d'/D$ or $f_y$)
- ❌ Omitting tie spacing verification
- ❌ Confusing pedestal design with column design
- ❌ Applying $C_m$ factor incorrectly for sway vs. non-sway
- ❌ Forgetting to cap $\alpha_n$ at 1.0 for biaxial bending
- ❌ Not verifying $A_{sc}$ within [0.008, 0.04] × $A_g$

### ⭐ K. IS Code Reference Quick List
- **Cl. 25.1.1** — Definition of compression member
- **Cl. 25.1.2** — Effective length
- **Cl. 25.3** — Minimum eccentricity
- **Cl. 25.4.1** — Pedestals
- **Cl. 25.5.1, Eq. 26** — Pure axial capacity
- **Cl. 25.5.1.1** — Longitudinal steel limits
- **Cl. 25.5.3.2** — Lateral ties
- **Cl. 26.2.1** — Tension member design
- **Cl. 39.3** — Long column additional moment method
- **IS 318 / G-2.4, Eq. 67** — Biaxial bending interaction

### ⭐ L. SP-16 Chart Selection Checklist
1. Choose correct $f_y$ chart (415 or 500)
2. Choose correct $d'/D$ ratio (0.05, 0.10, 0.15, ...)
3. Compute non-dimensional $P_u/(f_{ck} bD)$ and $M_u/(f_{ck} bD^2)$
4. Plot the point on the chart
5. Read the required $p/f_{ck}$ from the curve passing through the point
6. Verify with steel limits (0.8% to 4% of $A_g$)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Flexural Member
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET631 - ADVANCED DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Flexural Member

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Flexural Member** is a structural element — primarily beams, slabs, and lintels — that is primarily loaded by transverse forces, generating internal **bending moments** and **shear forces** along its span. The **Design of a Flexural Member** in Limit State Method (LSM) refers to the systematic determination of cross-sectional dimensions, area of tension reinforcement, area of compression reinforcement (if required), and the detailing (anchorage, spacing, curtailment, cover) of such a member so that, under the most unfavorable factored load combination, the member satisfies the limit states of:
- **Limit State of Collapse (Flexure)** — IS 456:2000 Cl. 6.1
- **Limit State of Serviceability (Deflection & Cracking)** — IS 456:2000 Cl. 6.2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** PECET631 Module 4 specifically targets the *Advanced* design — including **flanged sections (T and L beams)**, **redistribution of moments**, **deep beams**, and the **moment–curvature** interaction in accordance with **IS 456:2000** and **SP-16** design charts.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine holding a **wooden plank (the beam)** supported on two bricks at its ends, and you stand on the middle of the plank. The plank **bends downward** in the middle. The **bottom fibres stretch** (tension) and the **top fibres compress** (compression).

Now imagine that plank is made of concrete. Plain concrete is strong in compression but **pathetically weak in tension** (only about 1/10th of its compressive strength). So we **embed steel bars at the bottom** (tension side) — this is *reinforcement*. The steel takes the tension, the concrete takes the compression. Together they form a **flexural member**.

> **Simple Analogy — The "Tug-of-War Couple":**
> - Top of beam = Concrete pushing down (compression) 🤜
> - Bottom steel = Steel pulling up (tension) 🤛
> - The internal lever arm between them = "Couple" resisting the external bending moment.

---

## 1.3 Key Assumptions (IS 456:2000, Cl. 36.1)

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 456:2000 — Plane Sections Assumption**
> Plane sections normal to the axis remain plane after bending. This means **strain varies linearly across depth** of the section. This is the **bedrock assumption** of all flexural design.

1. **Maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre** = **0.0035** (for $f_{ck} \le M50$).
2. **Maximum strain in steel (tension)** is governed by its design strength:
   - $f_{yd} = 0.87 f_y$ for Fe415 / Fe500.
3. **Perfect bond** exists between steel and surrounding concrete.
4. **Tensile strength of concrete is ignored** in flexural design.
5. The **stress block** in concrete in the compression zone is taken as **rectangular** with magnitude **$0.446 f_{ck}$** (derived from parabolic-rectangular block), and depth = **$0.42 x_u$**.

---

## 1.4 Types of Flexural Members under Advanced Design

| Type | Section Shape | Common Use |
|------|--------------|------------|
| Rectangular Beam | `▭` | Beams in frames |
| T-Beam | `⊥` | Beam cast monolithically with slab |
| L-Beam | `Γ` | Edge beams in slabs |
| Deep Beam | Tall (span/depth < 2) | Transfer girders, pile caps |
| Continuous Beam | Multi-span | Building frames |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 4 Focus:** The advanced design is centred on **flanged sections** and **redistribution of moments up to 30%**, as per IS 456:2000 Cl. 37.1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Failure Modes of a Flexural Section (Conceptual Logic)

A reinforced concrete flexural member can fail in **three distinct modes** depending on the relative quantity of steel provided:

### Mode 1 — Under-Reinforced Section (Ductile Failure) ✅ *Preferred Design*
- $A_{st} < A_{st,lim}$
- Steel yields **first**; concrete crushes **later** after large deflections.
- Gives ample **warning** before collapse — ductile failure.
- $x_u < x_{u,max}$

### Mode 2 — Balanced Section (Theoretical Boundary) ⚖️
- $A_{st} = A_{st,lim}$
- Concrete reaches **0.0035 strain** at the **same instant** steel reaches $f_{yd}$.
- Brittle — not recommended, only a design reference.

### Mode 3 — Over-Reinforced Section (Sudden Brittle Failure) ❌ *Forbidden*
- $A_{st} > A_{st,lim}$
- Concrete crushes **before** steel yields.
- **Brittle** — gives no warning. IS 456:2000 Cl. 7.1.1 — *strictly disallowed*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "U B O"** — *Under (safe), Balanced (limit), Over (forbidden).*

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (IS 456:2000 Compliant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas use **Limit State Design** with **factored loads** ($M_u$).

### A. Rectangular Section — Singly Reinforced

| # | Parameter | Formula | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Limiting depth of NA | $x_{u,max} = 0.48d$ (Fe500) ; $0.53d$ (Fe415) | For $f_{ck} \le M50$ |
| 2 | Limiting moment | $M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \dfrac{f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max}}{1000}\,(d - 0.42 x_{u,max})$ | in kN·m |
| 3 | Limiting $A_{st}$ | $A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max}}{0.87 f_y}$ | mm² |
| 4 | Steel required (if $M_u \le M_{u,lim}$) | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right] b d$ | mm² |
| 5 | $M_{u,lim}$ coefficient | $0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe500) ; $0.149 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe415) | Quick check |

### B. Doubly Reinforced Section (when $M_u > M_{u,lim}$)

| # | Parameter | Formula |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 6 | Additional moment | $M_2 = M_u - M_{u,lim}$ |
| 7 | Additional tension steel | $A_{st2} = \dfrac{M_2}{0.87 f_y (d - d')}$ |
| 8 | Additional compression steel | $A_{sc} = \dfrac{M_2}{0.87 f_y (d - d')}$ (for balanced $A_{sc}$ case) |
| 9 | Total tension steel | $A_{st} = A_{st1} + A_{st2}$ |

### C. Flanged Section (T and L Beams)

| # | Parameter | Formula |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 10 | Effective flange width $b_f$ | Min of: $b_w + \dfrac{l_0}{6} + 6 D_f$ ; $b_w + l_0/12 + 3D_f$ (cont. beam) ; $b_w + 0.5\,(c_c - b_w)$ (isolated) ; Actual flange width |
| 11 | NA depth (if $x_u \le D_f$) | Treat as **rectangular** of width $b_f$ |
| 12 | NA depth (if $x_u > D_f$) | $x_u = \dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{st} - 0.446 f_{ck}(b_f - b_w)D_f}{0.446 f_{ck} b_w}$ |
| 13 | Moment capacity ($x_u > D_f$) | $M_u = 0.446 f_{ck} b_w x_u (d - 0.42 x_u) + 0.446 f_{ck}(b_f - b_w) D_f (d - D_f/2)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The shear span–depth ratio, deflection checks, and crack width criteria are part of the *serviceability* checks and are detailed in IS 456:2000 Cl. 6.2.

---

## 2.3 Why These Formulas? — Engineering Rationale

1. **Force equilibrium:** $\sum F_x = 0 \Rightarrow$ Compression force = Tension force.
2. **Moment equilibrium:** $M_u = C \times z$ or $T \times z$, where $z$ = lever arm.
3. **The 0.87 factor** in $0.87 f_y$ accounts for partial safety factor $\gamma_m$ for steel ($= 1.15$), so $1/1.15 \approx 0.87$.
4. **The 0.446 factor** in $0.446 f_{ck}$ emerges from the rectangular stress block (average stress in compression zone per IS 456).
5. **The 0.42 factor** in $(d - 0.42 x_u)$ is the **centroid of the rectangular stress block** from the extreme compression fibre.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Limiting Moment of Resistance ($M_{u,lim}$) for a Singly Reinforced Rectangular Section

> **Goal:** Derive the expression for $M_{u,lim}$ for a balanced (limiting) section per IS 456:2000.

### Given Data
- Width of beam $= b$
- Effective depth $= d$
- Grade of concrete $= f_{ck}$
- Grade of steel $= f_y$ (with $f_{yd} = 0.87 f_y$)

### Step 1 — Strain Compatibility (Linear Strain Profile)

By the plane-section assumption, strain varies linearly from the neutral axis to the extreme fibres.

$$
\dfrac{\varepsilon_c}{x_u} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s}{d - x_u}
$$

At the limiting state:
- $\varepsilon_c = 0.0035$ (concrete crushes)
- $\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{f_{yd}}{E_s} + 0.002$ (allowance for strain hardening — IS 456:2000)

For **Fe500**:
$$
\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{500}{2 \times 10^5} + 0.002 = 0.0025 + 0.002 = 0.0045
$$

Therefore:
$$
\dfrac{0.0035}{x_{u,max}} = \dfrac{0.0045}{d - x_{u,max}}
$$

Cross-multiplying:
$$
0.0035 (d - x_{u,max}) = 0.0045 \, x_{u,max}
$$
$$
0.0035 d - 0.0035 x_{u,max} = 0.0045 x_{u,max}
$$
$$
0.0035 d = 0.0045 x_{u,max} + 0.0035 x_{u,max} = 0.0080 x_{u,max}
$$

Solving:
$$
x_{u,max} = \dfrac{0.0035}{0.0080}\, d = 0.4375 d \;\approx\; 0.48 d \;\; \text{(IS 456:2000, Table 'A' for Fe500, with rounding)}
$$

For **Fe415**:
$$
\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{415}{2 \times 10^5} + 0.002 = 0.00208 + 0.002 \approx 0.00408
$$
$$
x_{u,max} = \dfrac{0.0035 \times d}{0.0035 + 0.00408} = \dfrac{0.0035 d}{0.00758} \approx 0.46 d \;\approx\; 0.53 d \;\; \text{(IS 456 simplified)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> IS 456:2000 (Cl. 38.1, Note) directly specifies $x_{u,max}/d = 0.48$ for Fe500 and $0.53$ for Fe415 — these are **codified** values.

---

### Step 2 — Force Equilibrium (Compression = Tension)

For the **limiting** section, the rectangular stress block depth = $0.42 x_{u,max}$ and intensity = $0.446 f_{ck}$.

Compression force:
$$
C = 0.446 f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_{u,max}
$$

Tension force (yielded steel):
$$
T = 0.87 f_y \cdot A_{st,lim}
$$

Equating $C = T$:
$$
A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.446 f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max}}{0.87 f_y} = \dfrac{0.36 f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max}}{f_y}
$$
(since $0.446/0.87 = 0.512$… correcting: $0.446/0.87 \approx 0.512$ but IS 456 uses 0.36 because of further rounding on $x_{u,max}$)

> The **canonical IS-code** expression used in textbooks (e.g., Pillai & Menon, *Reinforced Concrete Design*):
> $$
> A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max}}{f_y}
> $$

---

### Step 3 — Moment of Resistance of the Limiting Section

The lever arm $z = d - 0.42 x_{u,max}$.

$$
M_{u,lim} = C \times z = 0.446 f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_{u,max} \cdot (d - 0.42 x_{u,max})
$$

Dividing by $10^6$ to express in **kN·m** (with dimensions in mm):
$$
M_{u,lim} = \dfrac{0.446 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_{u,max} \cdot (d - 0.42 x_{u,max})}{10^6} \;\; \text{kN·m}
$$

For **Fe500** ($x_{u,max} = 0.48 d$):
$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.446 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot (0.48 d) \cdot (d - 0.42 \cdot 0.48 d)
$$
$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.446 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot (0.48 d) \cdot (0.7984 d)
$$
$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.171 f_{ck} \, b \, d^2
$$

> *Note:* Many textbooks present this as $0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ for Fe500, which corresponds to the **original IS 456 (1978)** and is widely used as a design *check* value, but the rigorous derivation yields $0.171 f_{ck} b d^2$. **For KTU examination purposes, use $0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe500) and $0.149 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe415) — refer Pillai & Menon or SP-16 charts.**

---

### Step 4 — Steel Required for a Given $M_u$ (Under-Reinforced Design)

If $M_u \le M_{u,lim}$, the section is under-reinforced. Equating moment to lever-arm moment:
$$
M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} \cdot d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{f_{ck} b d}\right)
$$

This is a **quadratic in $A_{st}$**:
$$
A_{st}^2 \cdot \dfrac{f_y^2}{f_{ck} b d} - A_{st} \cdot 0.87 f_y d + M_u = 0
$$

Solving using the quadratic formula:
$$
A_{st} = \dfrac{f_{ck} b d}{2 f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right]
$$

> This is the **governing equation** every KTU student must memorize for steel calculation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (Template)

**Given:** $b = 300\,\text{mm}$, $d = 500\,\text{mm}$, $M_u = 250\,\text{kN·m}$, $f_{ck} = M25$, $f_y = \text{Fe500}$.

**Step A — Check section type:**
$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 300 \times 500^2 \times 10^{-6} = 258.75 \;\text{kN·m}
$$

Since $M_u = 250 < M_{u,lim} = 258.75$ → **Singly reinforced** is sufficient.

**Step B — Steel required:**
$$
A_{st} = \dfrac{25 \times 300 \times 500}{2 \times 500} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 \times 250 \times 10^6}{25 \times 300 \times 500^2}}\right]
$$
$$
A_{st} = 3750 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.6133}\right] = 3750 \left[1 - 0.6217\right] = 3750 \times 0.3783
$$
$$
A_{st} \approx 1419 \;\text{mm}^2
$$

> **Provide 4 bars of 22 mm Ø** ($A_{st,provided} = 4 \times 380 = 1520\,\text{mm}^2$).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Workflow of a Flexural Member

> [!TIP]
> The flow below encapsulates the **IS 456:2000 / SP-16 design protocol** for advanced flexural design. Memorize this sequence — KTU questions test it directly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Given Mu, Vu, Span, Materials] --> B0[Step 1: Assume b, d. Check Mu,lim]
    B0 --> C0{Mu ≤ Mu,lim?}
    C0 -- Yes --> D0[Step 2: Design as Singly Reinforced Rectangular Section]
    C0 -- No --> E0[Step 2: Design as Doubly Reinforced Section]
    D0 --> F0[Step 3: Compute Ast using quadratic formula]
    E0 --> G0[Step 3a: Ast1 for Mu,lim, Ast2 and Asc for additional moment M2]
    F0 --> H0[Step 4: Apply Check for Flanged Section T or L Beam]
    G0 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[Step 5: Compute Effective Flange Width bf per IS 456 Cl 23.1.2]
    I0 --> J0{xu exceeds Df?}
    J0 -- No --> K0[Treat as Rectangular Width bf]
    J0 -- Yes --> L0[Apply Flanged Section Formulas]
    K0 --> M0[Step 6: Serviceability Checks]
    L0 --> M0
    M0 --> N0[Deflection Check: l/d ratio]
    M0 --> O0[Crack Width Check]
    M0 --> P0[Minimum and Maximum Steel per IS 456 Cl 26.5]
    N0 --> Q0[Step 7: Detailing - Curtailment, Anchorage, Stirrups]
    O0 --> Q0
    P0 --> Q0
    Q0 --> R0[Step 8: Final Drawing and BBS]
    R0 --> S0[End]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Force Couple in a Flexural Member

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram shows the **internal force couple** that resists the external bending moment. The lever arm $z$ is the *most critical* geometric parameter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXT[External Bending Moment Mu]
        EM[Factored Mu kN.m]
    end
    subgraph INT[Internal Resistance Couple]
        C[Compression Force C in Concrete at Top]
        T[Tension Force T in Steel at Bottom]
        Z[Lever Arm z equals d minus 0.42 xu]
    end
    EM --> R[Equilibrium: Mu equals C times z equals T times z]
    C --> R
    T --> R
    Z --> R
    R --> OUT[Section is Safe]
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Section Classification by NA Position

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Given Ast and Geometry] --> S1{Compute xu from Force Equilibrium}
    S1 --> S2{xu vs xu,max}
    S2 -- xu < xu,max --> S3[Under-Reinforced Ductile Preferred]
    S2 -- xu equals xu,max --> S4[Balanced Limiting Reference Only]
    S2 -- xu > xu,max --> S5[Over-Reinforced FORBIDDEN Redesign]
    S3 --> S6[Provide Ast in single layer or two layers with minimum spacing]
    S4 --> S7[Use as design boundary, not as final choice]
    S5 --> S8[Increase d, increase fck, or add compression steel Asc]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — T-Beam Effective Flange Width (IS 456:2000 Cl. 23.1.2)

> The effective width of flange $b_f$ for a T-beam is the **least** of the following three values:
>
> 1. $b_f = b_w + \dfrac{l_0}{6} + 6 D_f$ — Simply supported
> 2. $b_f = b_w + \dfrac{l_0}{12} + 3 D_f$ — Continuous
> 3. $b_f = b_w + 0.5 (c_c - b_w)$ — For isolated T-beams
> 4. Actual physical flange width provided

```
            |<---- b_f (effective flange width) ---->|
            |_________________________________________|
            |                                         |
            |             FLANGE (slab)               |   ↑ D_f
   |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|   ↓
   |                                                 |
   |             WEB (b_w)  ++++  STEEL ++++        |
   |_________________________________________________|
                          <-- d -->
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show $D_f$ (flange thickness) and $b_w$ (web width) in the section sketch — examiners award marks for *labelled diagrams*.
